cases.title: Two Months of Low Energy and Loss of Interest
cases.chiefComplaint: "I just feel flat and exhausted all the time, and nothing is fun anymore."
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents for evaluation of low mood for the past 2.5 months. She reports persistent sadness most of the day, nearly every day, with marked loss of interest in activities she previously enjoyed, including running and seeing friends. She endorses fatigue, early-morning awakening with difficulty returning to sleep, poor appetite with a 4 kg unintentional weight loss, difficulty concentrating at her marketing job, and feelings of worthlessness. She denies thoughts of suicide, self-harm, or wanting to be dead. She denies any period of elevated or irritable mood, decreased need for sleep, or impulsive spending. There is no history of trauma, hallucinations, or delusions. She drinks one glass of wine weekly and denies illicit drug use. She takes no medications and has no allergies. PMH is unremarkable. Family history is notable for depression in her mother, treated with sertraline. She is married, works full-time, and has no children. She has never previously sought mental health treatment and has had no prior manic or hypomanic episodes.
cases.exam: The patient is well-groomed but with constricted, dysphoric affect and psychomotor slowing. Speech is soft with increased latency but normal rate and content. Thought process is linear and goal-directed without flight of ideas. She denies suicidal or homicidal ideation, auditory or visual hallucinations, and delusions. Insight and judgment are intact; she is fully oriented with grossly normal attention and memory. Thyroid is non-enlarged and non-tender without nodules. No tremor, lid lag, or proptosis. Cardiac, pulmonary, and neurologic exams are unremarkable. No signs of self-injury are seen on the extremities.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of symptoms most strongly supports a diagnosis of major depressive disorder over normal sadness or another mood disorder?
options: Sadness lasting two weeks that resolves with distraction and does not impair function
Depressed mood plus anhedonia, sleep and appetite disturbance, fatigue, poor concentration, and worthlessness for over two weeks
Episodes of elevated mood alternating with low mood over several days
Persistent low-grade depressed mood for more than two years without discrete episodes
stages.explanation: MDD requires at least five of nine SIGECAPS-type symptoms (including depressed mood or anhedonia as one) present most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks, causing functional impairment. This patient has depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep change, appetite/weight loss, fatigue, poor concentration, and worthlessness, easily meeting criteria. Option A describes normal sadness without duration or impairment. Option C suggests bipolar disorder, but she has no history of mania/hypomania. Option D describes persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia), which requires a chronic two-year course rather than her discrete 2.5-month episode. Establishing the time course and the absence of mania is essential before initiating treatment.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before attributing her symptoms solely to MDD, which baseline laboratory test is most appropriate to exclude a common medical mimic?
options: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH)
Dexamethasone suppression test
Serum ceruloplasmin
24-hour urinary cortisol
stages.explanation: Hypothyroidism is a frequent, treatable cause of depressive symptoms (fatigue, low mood, weight change, poor concentration), so checking TSH is a standard, high-yield screen before or alongside starting an antidepressant. A CBC and metabolic panel are also reasonable. The dexamethasone suppression test (Cushing screening) and 24-hour urinary cortisol are not indicated without clinical features of hypercortisolism such as striae, central obesity, or hypertension. Serum ceruloplasmin screens for Wilson disease, which would present with neuropsychiatric and hepatic findings in a younger patient with movement abnormalities, not isolated depression. TSH is the single best initial test here.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Na 139 mEq/L
Glucose 88 mg/dL
Urine pregnancy test: negative
Urine toxicology: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her workup is unremarkable. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic treatment for her major depressive disorder?
options: Amitriptyline
Phenelzine
Sertraline
Quetiapine
stages.explanation: SSRIs such as sertraline are first-line pharmacotherapy for MDD because of their favorable efficacy, tolerability, and safety in overdose. Sertraline is also a reasonable choice given her mother responded to it, which can predict response. Amitriptyline, a tricyclic, is effective but carries anticholinergic side effects and significant lethality in overdose, making it second-line. Phenelzine, an MAOI, requires dietary tyramine restriction and has dangerous drug interactions; it is reserved for refractory or atypical depression. Quetiapine, an atypical antipsychotic, is used as adjunctive therapy or in bipolar depression, not as monotherapy for uncomplicated unipolar MDD. An SSRI is the clear initial agent.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: When counseling her about starting sertraline, which expectation should you communicate regarding the timeline of benefit and follow-up?
options: Full mood benefit typically occurs within 48 hours; stop the drug if not improved by day 3
Therapeutic effect usually takes 4-6 weeks; arrange close follow-up in 1-2 weeks to monitor tolerability and any worsening
Antidepressants work immediately, so no follow-up is needed for three months
She should double the dose herself every three days until she feels better
stages.explanation: Antidepressants generally require 4-6 weeks at an adequate dose to produce full therapeutic effect, though some improvement in sleep and energy may appear sooner. Early follow-up at 1-2 weeks is recommended to assess tolerability, adherence, and emergence of side effects, and to monitor for any worsening mood or new suicidal ideation, which can rarely occur early in treatment, particularly in younger patients. Option A is wrong because benefit is not seen in 48 hours and premature discontinuation undermines treatment. Option C neglects necessary monitoring. Option D is unsafe; dose titration should be physician-guided. Setting realistic expectations improves adherence and outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Major depressive disorder, single episode, moderate
pearls: MDD requires 5 of 9 symptoms (SIGECAPS plus depressed mood) for at least 2 weeks, with depressed mood or anhedonia mandatory.
Always screen for prior mania/hypomania before starting an antidepressant to avoid missing bipolar disorder and precipitating a manic switch.
Check TSH (and consider CBC/CMP) to exclude medical mimics such as hypothyroidism before attributing symptoms to MDD.
SSRIs are first-line; counsel that full benefit takes 4-6 weeks and arrange early follow-up to monitor tolerability and suicidality.

cases.title: Chronic Worry, Restlessness, and Trouble Sleeping
cases.chiefComplaint: "I can't stop worrying about everything, and my muscles are always tense."
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with excessive worry that she describes as present "almost every day" for the past 10 months. She worries about her job performance, her children's safety, finances, and minor daily tasks, and finds the worry difficult to control. She reports muscle tension in her neck and shoulders, restlessness, irritability, difficulty concentrating, fatigue, and trouble falling asleep. She denies discrete episodes of sudden intense fear with palpitations peaking within minutes, denies heat intolerance, palpitations at rest, or unintentional weight loss. She has noticed no tremor or diarrhea. She drinks two cups of coffee daily and no alcohol; she denies tobacco or illicit drugs. PMH is unremarkable. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Family history includes an anxious mother. She is married with two children and works as an accountant; symptoms are now affecting her work and relationships. She denies low mood, anhedonia, suicidal ideation, trauma, obsessions, or compulsions.
cases.exam: The patient appears anxious and fidgety with mildly tense posture but is cooperative and fully oriented. Affect is anxious but reactive; mood is described as "on edge." Thought process is linear without obsessions, delusions, or perceptual disturbances. She denies suicidal ideation. Neck exam reveals a normal-sized, non-tender thyroid without nodules or bruit. There is no fine tremor, lid lag, proptosis, or warm moist skin. Cardiac exam shows regular rate and rhythm without murmur; lungs are clear. Deep tendon reflexes are normal without hyperreflexia. Trapezius and paraspinal muscles are tender to palpation consistent with tension.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes generalized anxiety disorder from panic disorder in this patient?
options: The presence of palpitations and shortness of breath
Persistent, hard-to-control worry about multiple domains lasting most days for at least six months
Avoidance of crowded places for fear of embarrassment
Recurrent discrete attacks of intense fear peaking within minutes
stages.explanation: GAD is defined by excessive, difficult-to-control worry about multiple life domains occurring more days than not for at least six months, accompanied by symptoms such as restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, and sleep disturbance. This patient's 10-month course of pervasive worry with muscle tension and insomnia fits GAD. Option D describes the hallmark of panic disorder (recurrent unexpected panic attacks), which she denies. Option A lists nonspecific somatic symptoms that can occur in either disorder. Option C describes agoraphobia/social anxiety avoidance, which she does not report. The chronic, generalized, uncontrollable nature of the worry is the key distinguishing feature.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given the angle of ruling out a medical mimic, which test is most appropriate to order first?
options: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH)
Echocardiogram
Brain MRI
24-hour Holter monitor
stages.explanation: Hyperthyroidism can closely mimic GAD with anxiety, restlessness, irritability, and insomnia, so checking TSH is the highest-yield initial test to exclude a treatable endocrine cause. Although her exam lacks classic thyrotoxic signs (tremor, lid lag, tachycardia, weight loss), screening is still appropriate and inexpensive. An echocardiogram is not indicated without cardiac findings. Brain MRI is unnecessary in the absence of focal neurologic signs or atypical features. A Holter monitor would be considered for documented paroxysmal palpitations or arrhythmia, which she does not describe. TSH is the best first step to rule out hyperthyroidism before settling on a psychiatric diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
CBC: within normal limits
Basic metabolic panel: within normal limits
Urine toxicology: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her thyroid studies are normal. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her generalized anxiety disorder?
options: Scheduled alprazolam three times daily
An SSRI (e.g., escitalopram) and/or cognitive behavioral therapy
Propranolol as monotherapy
Quetiapine at bedtime
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for GAD is an SSRI or SNRI, cognitive behavioral therapy, or their combination; these provide durable benefit with a favorable safety profile. Scheduled benzodiazepines such as alprazolam are not first-line for chronic GAD because of dependence, tolerance, sedation, and rebound anxiety; they may be used briefly while an SSRI takes effect. Propranolol can blunt peripheral autonomic symptoms (e.g., performance anxiety tremor) but does not treat the cognitive worry of GAD as monotherapy. Quetiapine has some efficacy but carries metabolic and extrapyramidal risks and is not first-line. An SSRI plus CBT addresses the core disorder safely and effectively.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: She asks why you are avoiding a daily benzodiazepine even though it makes her feel calmer quickly. What is the best explanation?
options: Benzodiazepines have no effect on anxiety and only cause placebo relief
Long-term scheduled benzodiazepine use risks tolerance, physical dependence, sedation, falls, and cognitive impairment, whereas SSRIs and CBT offer safer, lasting control
Benzodiazepines are contraindicated because they cause hyperthyroidism
She should instead take a benzodiazepine plus alcohol for better effect
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepines provide rapid symptomatic relief but, with chronic scheduled use, lead to tolerance, physical dependence, withdrawal on discontinuation, sedation, increased fall risk, and cognitive impairment, especially with long-term use. SSRIs/SNRIs and CBT treat the underlying disorder with sustained benefit and a better safety profile, making them preferred for a chronic condition like GAD. Option A is false; benzodiazepines do reduce acute anxiety. Option C is fabricated; they do not cause hyperthyroidism. Option D is dangerous, since combining benzodiazepines with alcohol potentiates CNS and respiratory depression. Clear counseling about dependence risk supports adherence to first-line therapy.

pearls: GAD = excessive, hard-to-control worry across multiple domains, more days than not for ≥6 months, with ≥3 of: restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance.
Always check TSH to exclude hyperthyroidism, which mimics anxiety with restlessness, tremor, and insomnia.
First-line treatment is an SSRI/SNRI and/or CBT; benzodiazepines are avoided long-term due to dependence and tolerance.
Propranolol helps peripheral/performance anxiety but does not address the cognitive worry central to GAD.

cases.title: Recurrent Chest Pain and Shortness of Breath With Normal Heart Tests
cases.chiefComplaint: "My heart was pounding and I felt like I was dying again, just like last time."
cases.history: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department for the fourth time in three months with sudden chest tightness, palpitations, shortness of breath, lightheadedness, tingling in his hands, and a sense of impending doom. Episodes begin abruptly, peak within about 10 minutes, and resolve within 30 minutes. They occur unpredictably, including at rest and once while watching television; one woke him from sleep. Between episodes he is increasingly anxious about having another attack and has begun avoiding the gym and driving alone. Prior ED visits included a normal ECG, normal troponins, and a normal chest radiograph. He has no PMH, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He drinks alcohol socially, denies cocaine or stimulant use, and drinks 3-4 energy drinks daily. Family history is notable for an anxious sibling. He works in sales and is otherwise healthy. He denies fever, cough, leg swelling, hemoptysis, or recent immobilization or long travel.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious, diaphoretic, and hyperventilating early in the visit but settles over 20 minutes. He is alert and fully oriented. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses are equal. Lungs are clear bilaterally with no wheezes or crackles, and there is no chest-wall tenderness. There is no jugular venous distention and no lower-extremity edema, tenderness, or calf asymmetry. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. As his breathing normalizes, the tachycardia and tingling resolve. No thyromegaly or tremor is appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His ECG and troponin are again normal. In a young patient with recurrent, self-limited episodes, what is the most appropriate next step in this ED visit?
options: Immediate coronary angiography
Reassurance after confirming a reassuring focused cardiopulmonary workup and screening for panic disorder
Start empiric anticoagulation for presumed pulmonary embolism
Administer thrombolytics for presumed acute coronary syndrome
stages.explanation: In a healthy 27-year-old with repeated, brief, self-resolving episodes and serially normal ECGs and troponins, the priority is a focused cardiopulmonary assessment to exclude dangerous mimics, followed by reassurance and screening for panic disorder once the workup is reassuring. Coronary angiography is invasive and unjustified without evidence of ischemia in a low-risk young patient. Empiric anticoagulation is inappropriate without clinical suspicion or risk factors for pulmonary embolism (no tachypnea-hypoxia mismatch, no immobilization, low Wells score). Thrombolytics are dangerous and unindicated with a normal ECG and troponin. Recognizing the classic panic pattern avoids unnecessary, costly, and risky testing.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no ST changes
Troponin: undetectable x2
D-dimer: 280 ng/mL (normal)
TSH: 2.0 mIU/L (normal)
Urine toxicology: negative for cocaine/amphetamines
Chest radiograph: clear lungs, normal cardiac silhouette

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history and reassuring workup, which diagnosis best explains his presentation?
options: Panic disorder
Specific phobia
Acute coronary syndrome
Generalized anxiety disorder
stages.explanation: Panic disorder is defined by recurrent unexpected panic attacks (abrupt surges of intense fear peaking within minutes with somatic symptoms such as palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, paresthesias, and fear of dying) plus at least one month of persistent concern about future attacks or maladaptive behavior change, such as his avoidance of the gym and driving. ACS is excluded by repeatedly normal ECGs and troponins in a low-risk young man. Specific phobia involves fear cued by a specific object or situation, not the unexpected, out-of-the-blue attacks he describes. GAD features chronic generalized worry rather than discrete autonomic surges; his anticipatory worry is specifically about further attacks, characteristic of panic disorder.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate long-term treatment to recommend for his panic disorder?
options: Daily scheduled lorazepam indefinitely
An SSRI and/or cognitive behavioral therapy, plus reducing caffeine intake
Beta-blocker monotherapy with no further follow-up
No treatment, since the attacks are harmless
stages.explanation: First-line long-term treatment for panic disorder is an SSRI (or SNRI) and/or cognitive behavioral therapy, which reduce attack frequency and anticipatory anxiety with durable benefit. Counseling him to cut down his heavy caffeine/energy-drink intake is important, as caffeine can precipitate panic symptoms. Indefinite scheduled benzodiazepines risk dependence and tolerance and are not preferred for maintenance, though a short course may bridge until an SSRI takes effect. Beta-blocker monotherapy does not adequately treat the disorder and ignores needed follow-up. Doing nothing is inappropriate, since untreated panic disorder causes significant distress, functional impairment, and repeated ED utilization. SSRI plus CBT is the standard of care.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: He worries his heart is damaged and asks what physically happens during an attack. What is the most accurate explanation?
options: The episodes reflect silent heart attacks that will eventually scar his heart
A surge of the body's fight-or-flight (sympathetic) response produces real but benign symptoms that peak quickly and resolve without harming the heart
His lungs are collapsing intermittently and will require surgery
The symptoms mean he has an undiagnosed seizure disorder
stages.explanation: Panic attacks arise from an abrupt surge of the sympathetic nervous system (fight-or-flight response), producing real physiologic symptoms such as tachycardia, dyspnea, chest tightness, and paresthesias that peak within minutes and resolve without cardiac damage. Explaining this benign physiology helps reduce catastrophic misinterpretation, which perpetuates the panic cycle. Option A is false and reinforces health anxiety; his serial workups show no ischemia. Option C is incorrect, as there is no evidence of pneumothorax and his lungs are clear. Option D is wrong; panic attacks are not seizures, and his presentation lacks postictal features or focal neurologic signs. Accurate education is itself therapeutic and complements CBT.

pearls: Panic disorder = recurrent unexpected panic attacks (peak within minutes) plus ≥1 month of worry about more attacks or maladaptive avoidance.
Diagnose only after excluding mimics (ACS, arrhythmia, PE, hyperthyroidism, stimulant use) with a focused, low-risk workup.
First-line treatment is an SSRI/SNRI and/or CBT; benzodiazepines are reserved for short-term bridging due to dependence risk.
Heavy caffeine and stimulant use can precipitate panic symptoms and should be reduced.

cases.title: Agitated Young Man Brought in by Police After Days Without Sleep
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by police after erratic, disruptive behavior; he states "I've never felt better in my life."
cases.history: A 23-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after he was found directing traffic and claiming he had been "chosen to fix the city's transit system." Per his roommate, over the past 9 days he has slept only 2-3 hours nightly yet feels energetic, has been talking rapidly and nonstop, started three new businesses, and spent $7,000 on equipment he cannot afford. He has been irritable and grandiose, sending dozens of emails to city officials. He denies drug use; collateral confirms no recent substance use. There is no prior psychiatric diagnosis, but his roommate notes a brief period of low mood last year that resolved on its own. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. Family history is significant for a father with "manic depression" requiring hospitalization. He attends college part-time. He denies hallucinations but believes he has a special mission. He has not eaten or slept much for days. There is no head trauma, fever, or known medical illness.
cases.exam: The patient is disheveled, hyperactive, and intrusive, pacing the room and difficult to redirect. Speech is loud, rapid, and pressured with flight of ideas. Mood is "fantastic"; affect is expansive and irritable when limits are set. He expresses grandiose delusions about a special mission but denies auditory or visual hallucinations. Thought process is tangential. He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time, with grossly intact memory but poor insight and impaired judgment. He denies suicidal or homicidal ideation. Pupils are equal and reactive; no nystagmus, tremor, or focal neurologic deficits. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before diagnosing a primary manic episode, which workup is most appropriate to rule out organic and substance-induced causes?
options: No workup is needed; the diagnosis is clinical
Urine toxicology, basic metabolic panel, and consider TSH; assess for intoxication or medical causes
Immediate lumbar puncture and EEG for all patients
Brain biopsy to evaluate for tumor
stages.explanation: First-presentation mania warrants screening for substance-induced and medical mimics: urine toxicology (stimulants such as cocaine or amphetamines can cause manic-like states), a basic metabolic panel, and TSH (hyperthyroidism can mimic mania), plus assessing for intoxication and, when indicated, medication effects. Saying no workup is needed is unsafe for a first episode. Routine lumbar puncture and EEG are reserved for cases with fever, altered consciousness, focal deficits, or suspicion of encephalitis/seizure, which he lacks. Brain biopsy is invasive and unjustified without imaging evidence of a lesion. Targeted, noninvasive testing efficiently excludes treatable organic causes before attributing the presentation to bipolar disorder.

stages.label: LABS & TOXICOLOGY
stages.content:
Urine toxicology: negative
Blood alcohol: 0 mg/dL
Na 140 mEq/L
TSH 1.6 mIU/L (normal)
Glucose 96 mg/dL
CT head (noncontrast): no acute intracranial abnormality

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the negative workup, which set of features confirms bipolar I disorder, current manic episode?
options: Two weeks of depressed mood with anhedonia and fatigue
At least one week of elevated/irritable mood with increased energy plus grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, and risky spending causing marked impairment
Recurrent panic attacks with anticipatory anxiety
Chronic low-grade depression for over two years
stages.explanation: A manic episode requires at least one week (or any duration if hospitalization is needed) of abnormally elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with increased goal-directed activity/energy, plus at least three additional symptoms (or four if mood is only irritable): grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, increased activity, and risky behavior such as excessive spending. His 9-day course with grandiose delusions, minimal sleep, pressured speech, and a $7,000 spending spree, causing severe impairment and requiring intervention, defines mania, establishing bipolar I disorder. Option A describes a depressive episode, option C panic disorder, and option D persistent depressive disorder, none of which capture his manic presentation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is agitated and refusing oral medication while remaining grandiose. What is the most appropriate management for acute severe mania with agitation?
options: Start an SSRI such as fluoxetine
Initiate a mood stabilizer or second-generation antipsychotic (e.g., lithium or olanzapine), using a short-acting antipsychotic +/- benzodiazepine for acute agitation
Discharge home with outpatient follow-up in one month
Begin high-dose stimulants to channel his energy
stages.explanation: Acute mania is treated with a mood stabilizer (lithium or valproate) or a second-generation antipsychotic (e.g., olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine), often as monotherapy or in combination for severe episodes; acute agitation can be managed with a short-acting antipsychotic, sometimes with a benzodiazepine, while definitive therapy is established. Antidepressant monotherapy (fluoxetine) is contraindicated, as it can worsen or prolong mania. Discharging an acutely manic, impaired patient with grandiose delusions is unsafe and ignores the need for stabilization, often requiring admission. Stimulants would dangerously exacerbate mania. Initiating a mood stabilizer or antipsychotic, with appropriate agitation management and admission, is the standard approach.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He insists on leaving, has no insight, and is making grandiose, financially ruinous plans. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Honor his request and discharge him immediately
Pursue inpatient psychiatric admission, involuntarily if necessary, given impaired judgment and risk of harm from his behavior
Refer him to an outpatient therapist next week with no admission
Prescribe a benzodiazepine and send him home alone
stages.explanation: An acutely manic patient with grandiose delusions, severely impaired judgment, and behavior posing risk of harm to himself (financial ruin, exhaustion, dangerous public behavior) generally requires inpatient psychiatric admission for stabilization and safety; involuntary hold criteria are typically met when a mental illness renders the patient a danger to self or others or gravely disabled and unable to care for himself. Discharging him or deferring to outpatient care is unsafe given his lack of insight and ongoing risky behavior. Sending him home alone with a benzodiazepine does not treat mania and leaves him unsupervised. Admission, involuntary if needed and legally justified, is the appropriate, safety-driven disposition.

cases.diagnosis: Bipolar I disorder, current episode manic, with psychotic features
pearls: Mania = ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalized) of elevated/irritable mood + increased energy, plus ≥3 (or 4 if irritable) of grandiosity, ↓need for sleep, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, ↑activity, risky behavior.
Always exclude substance-induced mania and medical mimics (stimulants, hyperthyroidism) at first presentation with toxicology, BMP, and TSH.
Treat acute mania with a mood stabilizer (lithium/valproate) or second-generation antipsychotic; never use antidepressant monotherapy, which can worsen mania.
Acutely manic patients with impaired insight and risk of harm often warrant inpatient admission, involuntary if criteria are met.

cases.title: Eight Months of Withdrawal and Hearing Voices
cases.chiefComplaint: Admitted after his parents reported he had been talking to people who were not there and refusing to leave his room.
cases.history: A 21-year-old college student is admitted to inpatient psychiatry. Over the past 8 months his parents noticed progressive social withdrawal, declining grades, and odd beliefs; he dropped out of school and stopped seeing friends. In the last 6 weeks he developed clear auditory hallucinations, hearing two voices that comment on his actions and tell him people are conspiring against him. He believes his neighbors are spying through the television and has stopped eating food he did not prepare himself, fearing poisoning. He denies depressed or elevated mood and there is no history of discrete mood episodes. He denies illicit drug use; collateral and prior toxicology have been negative. He takes no medications and has no allergies. Family history is notable for a paternal uncle with schizophrenia. He lives with his parents. He has poor self-care and disorganized thinking but no fever, headache, or recent illness. There is no history of seizures or head trauma. His symptoms have now persisted well beyond six months when counting the prodrome.
cases.exam: The patient is guarded, with poor eye contact and a flat, restricted affect. Speech is soft with occasional loosening of associations and tangentiality. He endorses auditory hallucinations and paranoid, persecutory delusions but denies suicidal or homicidal ideation when asked. He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time; attention and memory are grossly intact, arguing against delirium. Insight and judgment are poor. There are no focal neurologic deficits, no tremor, no nystagmus, and no rigidity. Pupils are equal and reactive. General medical exam, including cardiac and pulmonary, is unremarkable. He appears thin and mildly unkempt with poor grooming.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before diagnosing a primary psychotic disorder in this first-episode patient, which initial workup is most appropriate?
options: No medical workup is necessary in young patients
Urine toxicology and basic labs (CBC, metabolic panel, TSH) to exclude substance-induced psychosis and medical mimics
Immediate electroconvulsive therapy
Empiric antibiotics for presumed meningitis
stages.explanation: First-episode psychosis requires exclusion of substance-induced and medical causes. Urine toxicology screens for stimulants, cannabis, PCP, and hallucinogens; CBC, a metabolic panel, and TSH screen for metabolic, infectious, and endocrine contributors. Additional testing (e.g., HIV, RPR, B12, autoimmune encephalitis antibodies, or neuroimaging) is guided by atypical features. Saying no workup is needed is unsafe at first presentation. Electroconvulsive therapy is not a first-line diagnostic or treatment step for uncomplicated first-episode psychosis. Empiric antibiotics are unwarranted without fever, meningismus, or systemic signs of infection; he is afebrile with a nonfocal exam. Targeted labs and toxicology efficiently rule out organic mimics before confirming a primary psychotic disorder.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine toxicology: negative
CBC: within normal limits
Basic metabolic panel: within normal limits
TSH 1.9 mIU/L (normal)
RPR: nonreactive
CT head (noncontrast): no acute intracranial abnormality

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Workup is unremarkable. Which feature best supports schizophrenia rather than schizophreniform or brief psychotic disorder?
options: Symptoms lasting fewer than one month with full return to baseline
Continuous signs of disturbance for at least six months, including the prodrome, with at least one month of active psychotic symptoms and functional decline
Psychosis occurring only during major depressive episodes
Psychotic symptoms present exclusively for two weeks after a stressor
stages.explanation: Schizophrenia requires continuous signs of disturbance for at least six months, including prodromal or residual phases, with at least one month of active-phase symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized/catatonic behavior, or negative symptoms) and significant functional decline. This patient's 8-month course (6-month prodrome of withdrawal and declining function plus 6 weeks of active hallucinations and delusions) meets these criteria. Option A describes brief psychotic disorder (<1 month). Schizophreniform disorder lasts 1-6 months. Option C describes psychotic features within a mood disorder, but he has no mood episodes. Option D again describes a brief, stressor-related course. The duration plus functional decline distinguishes schizophrenia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic treatment for his schizophrenia?
options: Clozapine as initial therapy
A second-generation antipsychotic such as risperidone
An SSRI such as sertraline
A benzodiazepine taper alone
stages.explanation: First-line pharmacotherapy for schizophrenia is an antipsychotic, typically a second-generation agent such as risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole, or paliperidone, chosen by side-effect profile and patient factors. Clozapine is the most effective antipsychotic but is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failure of two adequate antipsychotic trials) because of agranulocytosis risk and mandatory ANC monitoring; it is not first-line at initial presentation. SSRIs treat depressive or anxiety symptoms but do not address psychosis. A benzodiazepine alone may transiently calm agitation but does not treat the underlying psychotic disorder. Starting a second-generation antipsychotic with close monitoring is the standard initial approach.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: You plan to start risperidone. Which baseline and ongoing monitoring is most important given second-generation antipsychotic risks?
options: Serial chest radiographs only
Weight/BMI, fasting glucose and lipids, plus monitoring for extrapyramidal symptoms and prolactin-related effects
No monitoring is required with second-generation agents
Weekly absolute neutrophil counts for all second-generation antipsychotics
stages.explanation: Second-generation antipsychotics carry metabolic risks, so baseline and ongoing monitoring of weight/BMI, fasting glucose, and lipids is essential, along with blood pressure. Risperidone in particular can elevate prolactin (causing galactorrhea, menstrual changes, sexual dysfunction) and can cause extrapyramidal symptoms at higher doses, so clinical monitoring for EPS and prolactin-related effects is important. Serial chest radiographs are irrelevant to antipsychotic monitoring. Saying no monitoring is needed ignores well-established metabolic and movement adverse effects. Weekly absolute neutrophil counts are required specifically for clozapine due to agranulocytosis risk, not for all second-generation antipsychotics. Metabolic plus EPS/prolactin monitoring is the appropriate, guideline-based strategy.

pearls: Schizophrenia requires ≥6 months of continuous disturbance (including prodrome) with ≥1 month of active-phase symptoms and functional decline.
Differentiate by duration: brief psychotic disorder <1 month, schizophreniform 1-6 months, schizophrenia ≥6 months.
Always exclude substance-induced psychosis and medical mimics at first episode with toxicology and targeted labs.
Second-generation antipsychotics are first-line; clozapine is reserved for treatment resistance and requires ANC monitoring for agranulocytosis.

cases.title: Post-Operative Tremor, Sweating, and Seeing Bugs on Day Three
cases.chiefComplaint: Nursing staff report the patient is tremulous, sweating, agitated, and "seeing bugs crawling on the wall" three days after surgery.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is on the medical ward recovering from an open reduction and internal fixation of a femur fracture three days ago. Overnight he became tremulous, diaphoretic, anxious, and agitated, reporting visual hallucinations of insects on the wall. On admission he disclosed drinking "a few beers," but collateral from family reveals he typically drinks a pint of vodka plus several beers daily and had his last drink the morning of admission. He has hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily and a remote history of an alcohol withdrawal seizure. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack daily. On the second post-op day he had mild tremor and tachycardia that were not treated. He now appears acutely confused and frightened. There is no head trauma, focal deficit, or fever reported earlier. He received only acetaminophen and a low-dose opioid for pain. He has not received any benzodiazepine or thiamine since admission.
cases.exam: The patient is diaphoretic, tremulous, and agitated, picking at the bedsheets and intermittently pointing at the wall describing insects. He is disoriented to place and time and inattentive, unable to follow simple commands consistently, indicating clouded consciousness. There is a coarse resting and postural tremor of the hands. Pupils are mildly dilated and reactive; conjunctivae are injected. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm; lungs are clear. There is no neck stiffness, no focal motor or sensory deficit, and no asterixis. The surgical incision is clean without erythema or purulent drainage. Mucous membranes are dry.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the timeline and findings, which diagnosis best explains his presentation?
options: Uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal (early tremulousness)
Delirium tremens
Wernicke encephalopathy
Postoperative wound infection with sepsis
stages.explanation: Delirium tremens (DTs) is the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal, typically beginning 48-96 hours after the last drink, and is characterized by delirium (clouded consciousness, disorientation, inattention), autonomic hyperactivity (tachycardia, hypertension, fever, diaphoresis), tremor, agitation, and hallucinations. This patient's day-3 timing, confusion, and autonomic instability with hallucinations fit DTs. Uncomplicated withdrawal (option A) features tremor and anxiety but a clear sensorium without delirium. Wernicke encephalopathy classically causes the triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia without prominent autonomic hyperactivity, and lacks his diaphoresis and tremor pattern, though it can coexist. Wound infection is unlikely given a clean incision, and would not explain the tremor and hallucinations of withdrawal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacologic management of his delirium tremens?
options: Haloperidol monotherapy
Benzodiazepines titrated using a symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar protocol, plus parenteral thiamine
Withhold all sedatives and observe
Intravenous beta-blocker as the primary treatment
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepines are the cornerstone of alcohol withdrawal and DT treatment because they restore GABAergic tone; symptom-triggered dosing guided by the CIWA-Ar scale is preferred and reduces total benzodiazepine exposure, though severe DTs often need aggressive, frequently fixed-schedule or escalating dosing. Parenteral thiamine should be given to prevent or treat Wernicke encephalopathy, ideally before glucose. Haloperidol alone does not treat the underlying GABA deficit, lowers the seizure threshold, and can prolong the QT, so it is only an adjunct for agitation/hallucinations after benzodiazepines. Withholding sedatives in DTs is dangerous, as untreated DTs carries significant mortality. Beta-blockers may blunt autonomic signs but do not prevent seizures or delirium and can mask worsening withdrawal.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Reflecting on his course, what likely contributed to his progression to delirium tremens, and what is the key lesson for at-risk admitted patients?
options: His amlodipine caused the withdrawal and should have been stopped

Untreated early withdrawal signs (day-2 tremor and tachycardia) and a prior withdrawal seizure are red flags; high-risk patients should be screened and prophylactically managed with scheduled CIWA monitoring and benzodiazepines
He should have been given an opioid taper to prevent DTs
Nothing could have changed the outcome; DTs is unpredictable and unrelated to prior history
stages.explanation: His progression was foreseeable: a documented history of an alcohol withdrawal seizure is among the strongest predictors of severe withdrawal/DTs, and his untreated day-2 tremor and tachycardia were early withdrawal signs that went unaddressed. The lesson is that at-risk admitted patients (heavy daily intake, prior seizure/DTs, high tolerance) should be identified on admission, started on scheduled CIWA-Ar monitoring, and treated promptly with benzodiazepines, plus thiamine, to prevent escalation. Amlodipine does not cause withdrawal (option A). Opioids do not prevent or treat alcohol withdrawal and would add respiratory-depression risk (option C). Saying DTs is unpredictable (option D) is false, since validated risk factors and proactive protocols substantially reduce morbidity and mortality.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 132 mEq/L
K 3.2 mEq/L
Mg 1.3 mg/dL
Glucose 104 mg/dL
AST 88 U/L / ALT 52 U/L
CBC: WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL, platelets 130 ×10³/µL
Blood alcohol level: 0 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His labs return as above. In addition to benzodiazepines and thiamine, which electrolyte management is most appropriate?
options: Withhold all electrolyte repletion until DTs resolves
Replete magnesium and potassium and monitor electrolytes closely
Give a large free-water bolus to correct sodium rapidly
Administer intravenous calcium gluconate as the priority
stages.explanation: Chronic alcohol use commonly causes hypomagnesemia and hypokalemia, both seen here, and these derangements can predispose to arrhythmias and seizures; repleting magnesium and potassium with close electrolyte monitoring is appropriate supportive care. Withholding repletion risks cardiac instability and lowers the seizure threshold. His mild hyponatremia (132) does not warrant aggressive or rapid correction, and a large free-water bolus is both unindicated and could worsen hyponatremia; overly rapid sodium correction risks osmotic demyelination. Calcium gluconate is not the priority absent symptomatic hypocalcemia or hyperkalemic ECG changes. Correcting magnesium and potassium while continuing benzodiazepines and thiamine optimizes safety during DT treatment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite escalating benzodiazepines per CIWA, he remains severely agitated with worsening autonomic instability. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discontinue benzodiazepines and switch to oral lorazepam only
Escalate to high-dose IV benzodiazepines and consider adjunctive phenobarbital or a continuous infusion (e.g., dexmedetomidine/propofol) with possible ICU transfer
Start disulfiram immediately
Begin a slow oral chlordiazepoxide taper and discharge planning
stages.explanation: Refractory or severe DTs unresponsive to escalating benzodiazepines warrants intensified therapy: high-dose IV benzodiazepines, adjunctive phenobarbital, or continuous sedative infusions (e.g., dexmedetomidine for autonomic control or propofol for refractory cases), typically with ICU-level monitoring for airway and hemodynamics. Switching to oral lorazepam alone is inadequate for an unstable, severely agitated patient who cannot reliably take or absorb oral medication. Disulfiram is a maintenance deterrent for sobriety and is contraindicated in acute withdrawal, as it does not treat DTs and can be harmful. A slow oral taper with discharge planning is premature and unsafe while he remains acutely unstable. Escalation with ICU-level care is the correct, life-saving step.

pearls: Delirium tremens occurs 48-96 hours after the last drink: delirium plus autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, fever), tremor, and hallucinations; it is potentially fatal.
Benzodiazepines via a symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar protocol are first-line; give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.
Replete magnesium and potassium, which are commonly depleted and predispose to arrhythmias and seizures.
Refractory DTs may require phenobarbital or continuous infusions (dexmedetomidine/propofol) with ICU care; haloperidol is only an adjunct and lowers the seizure threshold.

cases.title: A Grieving Widower Who Says He Has a Gun at Home
cases.chiefComplaint: "There's no point in going on without her. I've thought about ending it."
cases.history: A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his daughter, who is worried about his statements that he "would be better off dead." His wife of 40 years died four months ago. Since then he has had depressed mood, anhedonia, poor sleep with early awakening, a 6 kg weight loss, profound fatigue, guilt that he "should have done more" for her, and difficulty concentrating. Over the past three weeks his thoughts have intensified, and today he admits to a specific plan to shoot himself with a handgun he keeps in his bedroom, stating he has been thinking about "when the house is empty." He has access to the loaded firearm. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes, treated with lisinopril and metformin, with no known allergies. He lives alone since his wife died, drinks two whiskeys nightly (recently increased), and has become socially isolated. Family history includes depression in a brother. He has no prior suicide attempts but expresses hopelessness and feels like a burden.
cases.exam: The patient is thin and disheveled with poor eye contact and psychomotor slowing. Affect is constricted and tearful; mood is "empty." Speech is slow and soft with normal content. He endorses active suicidal ideation with a specific plan and access to a firearm, and expresses hopelessness and guilt; he denies auditory or visual hallucinations and denies homicidal ideation. He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time, with attention and memory grossly intact, arguing against delirium or major neurocognitive disorder. Insight is partial and judgment impaired by hopelessness. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable. There are no signs of self-injury or recent trauma on the extremities.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best characterizes his presentation, distinguishing it from uncomplicated bereavement?
options: Normal grief that requires only reassurance
Major depressive disorder with active suicidal ideation
Adjustment disorder with depressed mood
Major neurocognitive disorder (dementia)
stages.explanation: While grief is expected after loss, this patient meets full criteria for a major depressive episode: pervasive depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, marked weight loss, fatigue, excessive guilt, poor concentration, and active suicidal ideation with a plan, persisting and worsening over months and impairing function. Critically, he has active suicidal ideation with a specific plan and means, which is never normal bereavement. Normal grief comes in waves, preserves self-worth, and lacks a sustained suicidal plan, so reassurance alone is unsafe. Adjustment disorder does not meet full MDD criteria and would not include this severity or active suicidality. There is no cognitive deficit pattern to suggest dementia; orientation and memory are intact. MDD with suicidal ideation is the diagnosis.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which combination of features places him at the highest risk for suicide?
options: Female sex, married status, and strong social support
Older male, recent loss/widowhood, hopelessness, increased alcohol use, social isolation, and a specific plan with access to a firearm
Young age with no prior psychiatric history
Vague passive thoughts without any plan or means
stages.explanation: High-risk features (captured by mnemonics such as SAD PERSONS) include older age, male sex, recent loss/widowhood, depression and hopelessness, substance use, social isolation/living alone, and especially a specific, lethal plan with access to means (here, a loaded firearm). This patient embodies nearly all of these, marking him as very high risk. Option A lists protective factors (female sex, being married, social support), which lower risk. Option C describes a lower-risk demographic. Option D, passive ideation without plan or means, is lower risk than his active, planned ideation with firearm access. Recognizing the convergence of these factors mandates urgent protective action.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate disposition for this patient?
options: Discharge home with his daughter and outpatient follow-up next week
Psychiatric admission (involuntary if he refuses) with one-to-one observation and removal of access to the firearm
Prescribe an SSRI and a one-month supply of zolpidem and send him home
Refer to a grief support group as the sole intervention
stages.explanation: A patient with active suicidal ideation, a specific plan, and access to a lethal means (firearm) requires immediate safety measures: psychiatric admission for stabilization, involuntary commitment if he declines and meets danger-to-self criteria, continuous one-to-one observation, and means restriction by removing firearm access. Discharging him home, even with family, is unsafe given his planned ideation and available means. Prescribing an SSRI (which takes weeks to work) plus a month of a sedative-hypnotic and sending him home both fails to ensure immediate safety and provides a potential overdose agent. A grief support group does not address acute suicide risk. Inpatient admission with observation and means restriction is the standard of care.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After admission and ensuring safety, what is the most appropriate next step regarding firearm access and treatment planning?
options: Ignore the firearm since he is now hospitalized
Counsel the patient and family on lethal-means restriction (securing or removing the firearm) and initiate treatment for depression with close monitoring
Immediately start electroconvulsive therapy without any discussion
Begin a benzodiazepine taper and plan same-day discharge
stages.explanation: Lethal-means restriction is a core, evidence-based suicide-prevention intervention: clinicians should counsel the patient and family to secure or remove the firearm from the home, which reduces suicide risk even after discharge. Concurrently, initiating treatment for his major depressive disorder (e.g., an antidepressant, psychotherapy, and management of his alcohol use) with close monitoring is appropriate. Ignoring the firearm leaves a lethal means available upon discharge. Electroconvulsive therapy is highly effective for severe or refractory depression with suicidality but is not started without evaluation and consent, and is not the immediate next step here. A benzodiazepine taper with same-day discharge is unsafe and does not treat depression. Means restriction plus depression treatment is correct.

cases.diagnosis: Major depressive disorder with active suicidal ideation
pearls: Bereavement that meets full MDD criteria or includes active suicidal ideation with a plan is not normal grief and requires treatment.
High-risk suicide factors: older male, widowed/recent loss, hopelessness, substance use, social isolation, prior attempts, and a specific plan with access to lethal means.
Active suicidal ideation with a plan and available firearm warrants psychiatric admission (involuntary if needed), one-to-one observation, and means restriction.
Lethal-means counseling (securing/removing firearms) is an evidence-based intervention that lowers suicide risk.

cases.title: A Veteran With Nightmares, Flashbacks, and Constant Edginess
cases.chiefComplaint: "I can't sleep, I keep reliving the convoy attack, and I jump at every loud noise."
cases.history: A 31-year-old male combat veteran presents to the VA outpatient clinic about one year after returning from deployment. During his tour, his convoy was struck by an improvised explosive device, killing a close friend beside him. Since then he reports recurrent intrusive memories and vivid nightmares of the attack several nights weekly, flashbacks triggered by fireworks and car backfires, and intense distress with cues resembling the event. He avoids crowds, driving, and news about the war, and has emotionally distanced himself from his wife. He describes hypervigilance, an exaggerated startle response, irritability with occasional angry outbursts, poor concentration, and difficulty falling and staying asleep. He denies hallucinations and denies a primary manic or psychotic disorder. He drinks 4-5 beers most evenings to "calm down" and sleep, with escalating use. He takes no psychiatric medications and has no allergies. He has a remote PMH of a concussion. Family history is noncontributory. Symptoms have persisted well beyond one month and impair his marriage and work.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, oriented, and cooperative but appears tense and scans the room repeatedly, startling when a door closes. Affect is anxious and somewhat restricted; mood is "on guard." Speech is normal in rate and volume. Thought process is linear; he denies suicidal or homicidal ideation, hallucinations, and delusions. Insight is fair. There are no focal neurologic deficits, tremor, or signs of intoxication or withdrawal today. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable. He becomes visibly distressed and diaphoretic when briefly describing the convoy attack, then composes himself. No stigmata of chronic liver disease are present.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature is required to diagnose post-traumatic stress disorder rather than acute stress disorder in this patient?
options: Symptoms lasting between 3 days and 1 month after trauma
Symptom duration of more than one month following exposure to the traumatic event
Onset of symptoms only after six months with no earlier features
Absence of any avoidance symptoms
stages.explanation: PTSD requires symptoms lasting more than one month after exposure to actual or threatened death/serious injury, with symptoms in four clusters: intrusion (nightmares, flashbacks, intrusive memories), avoidance (of reminders), negative alterations in cognition/mood, and alterations in arousal/reactivity (hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, irritability, sleep disturbance). His symptoms have persisted about a year, satisfying the duration criterion and all clusters. Acute stress disorder (option A) has the same symptom domains but lasts 3 days to 1 month after trauma. Option C describes delayed expression, but he has had continuous symptoms, not delayed onset. Option D is incorrect, since avoidance is a core PTSD cluster and he clearly exhibits it. The greater than one-month duration defines PTSD here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his PTSD overall?
options: Long-term scheduled benzodiazepines
Trauma-focused psychotherapy (e.g., prolonged exposure or cognitive processing therapy) and/or an SSRI/SNRI
Antipsychotic monotherapy
Stimulant therapy to improve concentration
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for PTSD is trauma-focused psychotherapy, such as prolonged exposure or cognitive processing therapy, and/or an SSRI/SNRI (sertraline and paroxetine are FDA-approved; venlafaxine is also effective). These have the strongest evidence for durable benefit. Benzodiazepines are generally avoided in PTSD because they do not treat core symptoms, can worsen outcomes, and carry dependence risk, which is especially concerning given his escalating alcohol use. Antipsychotic monotherapy is not first-line and is reserved for refractory cases or specific symptoms. Stimulants do not treat PTSD and could worsen arousal/anxiety. Trauma-focused therapy and/or an SSRI/SNRI, along with addressing his alcohol use, is the appropriate foundation of treatment.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His most distressing residual symptom is recurrent trauma-related nightmares despite starting an SSRI. Which targeted medication is most appropriate?
options: Zolpidem nightly
Prazosin at bedtime
Diphenhydramine
Olanzapine
stages.explanation: Prazosin, an alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist, is the best-supported pharmacologic option for trauma-related nightmares and sleep disturbance in PTSD; it dampens nocturnal noradrenergic activity and is titrated at bedtime while monitoring for orthostatic hypotension. Zolpidem may aid sleep onset but does not specifically reduce trauma nightmares and carries dependence and complex sleep-behavior risks. Diphenhydramine is sedating but anticholinergic, poorly targeted, and not recommended for chronic use. Olanzapine is an antipsychotic with metabolic risks and is not first-line for isolated nightmares. Given his prominent nightmares and hyperarousal, adding bedtime prazosin to his SSRI and trauma-focused therapy is the most appropriate targeted step.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: He says the beers are the only thing that lets him sleep. What is the most appropriate counseling regarding his alcohol use?
options: Encourage continued nightly alcohol as a safe sleep aid
Explain that alcohol worsens sleep architecture and PTSD symptoms and increases risks; address the alcohol use as part of treatment
Recommend switching to nightly benzodiazepines instead of alcohol
Tell him alcohol use is irrelevant to his PTSD
stages.explanation: Alcohol is a common form of self-medication in PTSD but ultimately fragments sleep architecture (reducing REM and causing rebound awakenings), worsens hyperarousal and mood symptoms, and can interfere with treatment and increase risk of a substance use disorder; addressing it is integral to PTSD care. Encouraging continued nightly alcohol is harmful and perpetuates poor sleep. Substituting nightly benzodiazepines trades one dependence-forming agent for another and is contraindicated in PTSD. Saying alcohol is irrelevant ignores its strong bidirectional relationship with PTSD severity and treatment response. Honest education about alcohol's effects, paired with support to reduce use, optimizes his overall outcome and complements prazosin, an SSRI, and trauma-focused therapy.

pearls: PTSD = symptoms >1 month after trauma across four clusters: intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, and hyperarousal; acute stress disorder is the same picture lasting 3 days to 1 month.
First-line treatment is trauma-focused psychotherapy (prolonged exposure, cognitive processing therapy) and/or an SSRI/SNRI (sertraline, paroxetine, venlafaxine).
Prazosin is the targeted agent for trauma-related nightmares; titrate at bedtime and watch for orthostatic hypotension.
Avoid benzodiazepines in PTSD, and address comorbid alcohol/substance use, which worsens symptoms and sleep.

cases.title: A Teen Who Can't Stop Washing Her Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: "I know it doesn't make sense, but I have to wash my hands over and over or something bad will happen."
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student presents to the outpatient clinic with distressing, intrusive thoughts about germs and contamination that have worsened over the past year. She describes recurrent, unwanted thoughts that touching doorknobs, money, or surfaces will make her or her family seriously ill. To relieve the anxiety, she washes her hands ritualistically, often 30-40 times daily for several minutes each, and repeatedly cleans surfaces. She recognizes these thoughts and behaviors are excessive and irrational but feels unable to resist them; the rituals now consume more than two hours daily, make her late to class, and have cracked and chapped her hands. She denies low mood meeting depressive criteria, no hallucinations or delusions, and no substance use. She takes no medications and has no allergies. Family history includes an aunt with OCD. She lives in a dorm and reports significant academic and social impairment. She denies restrictive eating, purging, or body-image distortion, and her insight into the senselessness of the rituals is good.
cases.exam: The patient is well-groomed, cooperative, and anxious. Affect is anxious but reactive; mood is "stressed." Thought process is linear and goal-directed; she describes ego-dystonic obsessions about contamination and compulsive washing, with preserved insight that the behaviors are excessive. She denies suicidal or homicidal ideation, hallucinations, and delusions. She is alert, fully oriented, with intact attention and memory. The dorsa of both hands show dry, erythematous, fissured skin with mild excoriation from repeated washing. The remainder of the skin and the general medical exam are unremarkable. No tremor or thyromegaly is noted.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes obsessive-compulsive disorder from obsessive-compulsive personality disorder in this patient?
options: A lifelong pattern of perfectionism and rigidity experienced as appropriate (ego-syntonic)
Ego-dystonic, intrusive obsessions and compulsive rituals that she recognizes as excessive and distressing
Preoccupation with orderliness without any anxiety
Delusional certainty that germs will kill her, with no insight
stages.explanation: OCD is characterized by ego-dystonic obsessions (unwanted, intrusive, anxiety-provoking thoughts) and compulsions (repetitive behaviors performed to neutralize anxiety) that the person typically recognizes as excessive, as this patient does. In contrast, obsessive-compulsive personality disorder (OCPD) is a pervasive, ego-syntonic pattern of perfectionism, orderliness, and control that the person views as appropriate and is not driven by true obsessions or compulsions. Option A and option C describe OCPD-type traits. Option D would suggest psychotic-level absent insight; while OCD insight varies, hers is good, and her beliefs are not fixed delusions. The hallmark distinguishing OCD here is distressing, ego-dystonic obsessions with compulsive rituals she knows are irrational.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her OCD?
options: A low-dose benzodiazepine as needed
An SSRI combined with cognitive behavioral therapy using exposure and response prevention
An antipsychotic as monotherapy
Reassurance alone with no specific therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for OCD is an SSRI plus cognitive behavioral therapy with exposure and response prevention (ERP); the combination is more effective than either alone for many patients. OCD often requires higher SSRI doses and longer trials (up to 8-12 weeks) than depression to achieve response. Benzodiazepines do not treat core OCD symptoms and carry dependence risk. Antipsychotic monotherapy is not first-line; antipsychotics are used only as augmentation in treatment-resistant OCD. Reassurance alone is inadequate given her significant functional impairment and skin breakdown. The standard of care is an SSRI combined with ERP, which directly targets both the obsessions and the maladaptive compulsive responses.

stages.label: TREATMENT RATIONALE
stages.question: She asks how exposure and response prevention works. What is the best explanation of the core principle of ERP?
options: She avoids all contamination triggers permanently to prevent anxiety
She is gradually and deliberately exposed to feared triggers while refraining from the washing ritual, allowing anxiety to habituate and decline on its own
She performs the handwashing ritual more frequently to satisfy the urge
She takes medication only, with no behavioral component
stages.explanation: ERP works by systematically and gradually exposing the patient to feared stimuli (e.g., touching a doorknob) while preventing the compulsive response (handwashing). With repeated practice, the conditioned anxiety habituates and naturally subsides without the ritual, breaking the obsession-compulsion reinforcement cycle and demonstrating that the feared catastrophe does not occur. Option A (continued avoidance) maintains and worsens OCD by reinforcing the belief that avoidance is necessary. Option C (performing rituals more) directly reinforces compulsions and is the opposite of treatment. Option D ignores the essential behavioral component; while medication helps, ERP is the active psychotherapeutic mechanism. Understanding habituation through response prevention is central to her recovery.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding her SSRI for OCD, which expectation should you set compared with treating depression?
options: OCD typically responds to lower doses and within a few days
OCD often requires higher SSRI doses and longer trials (often 8-12 weeks) to see benefit
SSRIs are ineffective for OCD and should not be used
She should stop the SSRI as soon as she feels slightly better
stages.explanation: Compared with depression, OCD frequently requires higher SSRI doses and a longer time to response, often 8-12 weeks at an adequate dose, before judging efficacy; premature dose reduction or discontinuation risks relapse. Option A is incorrect because OCD generally needs higher doses and a longer trial, not lower doses with rapid response. Option C is false, as SSRIs are first-line and clearly effective for OCD. Option D is unsafe, since stopping early after minimal improvement risks recurrence; treatment should be continued and optimized, with maintenance after response. Setting these expectations improves adherence through the longer latency to benefit that characterizes OCD pharmacotherapy. (Note: dosing is gradually titrated as tolerated.)

pearls: OCD features ego-dystonic obsessions and time-consuming compulsions the patient recognizes as excessive; OCPD is an ego-syntonic personality pattern of perfectionism and control.
First-line treatment is an SSRI plus CBT with exposure and response prevention (ERP).
OCD often needs higher SSRI doses and longer trials (8-12 weeks) than depression before judging response.
ERP works by exposing the patient to feared triggers while preventing the ritual, allowing anxiety to habituate and breaking the obsession-compulsion cycle.

cases.title: A Teenager With a Dangerously Slow Heart Rate and Severe Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for fainting, fatigue, and a very low heart rate noted at a sports physical.
cases.history: A 17-year-old girl is admitted to the medical floor after a near-syncopal episode and a heart rate in the 30s-40s found during a pre-season physical. Her parents report she has lost substantial weight over the past year through severe dietary restriction and intense daily exercise. She eats only small amounts of "safe" low-calorie foods, counts calories obsessively, and is terrified of gaining weight despite being visibly underweight. She sees herself as "fat" and weighs herself multiple times daily. She has not menstruated in five months. She denies binge eating or purging by vomiting or laxatives, identifying solely as restricting. She reports fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, and dizziness. She has no other PMH, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Family history includes maternal anxiety. She is a high-achieving student and competitive runner. Her BMI is 14 kg/m². She denies suicidal ideation but has poor insight into the medical danger of her low weight and continues to want to lose more.
cases.exam: The patient is markedly cachectic with temporal wasting and prominent ribs; BMI is 14 kg/m². She is alert and oriented but appears tired. Skin is dry with fine lanugo hair over the back and forearms; extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill and acrocyanosis. Cardiac exam reveals marked bradycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmur. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is scaphoid and soft. There is mild lower-extremity edema. No goiter or tremor is present. Affect is anxious when discussing weight; insight into medical risk is poor. There are no signs of self-harm, and dentition is intact (no enamel erosion).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on her presentation alone, which finding most urgently justifies inpatient medical admission rather than outpatient management?
options: Her preoccupation with calorie counting
Severe bradycardia (HR 38) with hypotension and hypothermia in a markedly underweight patient
The presence of lanugo hair
Her five months of amenorrhea
stages.explanation: Hemodynamic and thermoregulatory instability is the most urgent indication for medical admission in anorexia nervosa. A heart rate of 38 with hypotension (88/56) and hypothermia (35.6 °C) in a patient with BMI 14 signals dangerous physiologic decompensation from starvation and a high risk of fatal arrhythmia, warranting hospitalization with cardiac monitoring. Calorie preoccupation (option A) is a psychological feature but not itself an emergency. Lanugo (option C) and amenorrhea (option D) reflect chronic starvation and are clinically important, but they are not acute, life-threatening findings that mandate immediate admission. Recognizing unstable vital signs as the trigger for inpatient care is the key initial judgment before further workup.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which features confirm anorexia nervosa, restricting type, over bulimia nervosa?
options: Normal or above-normal body weight with recurrent binge-purge cycles
Significantly low body weight from restriction with intense fear of weight gain and body-image disturbance, without binge eating or purging
Binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting at a normal weight
Episodic overeating without compensatory behaviors
stages.explanation: Anorexia nervosa is defined by restriction of intake leading to significantly low body weight, intense fear of gaining weight, and a disturbance in body-image/self-perception; the restricting subtype lacks recurrent binge-eating or purging behaviors, achieving weight loss through dieting, fasting, and excessive exercise, exactly this patient's pattern (BMI 14, fear of weight gain, distorted body image, no purging). Bulimia nervosa (option A/C) occurs at normal or above-normal weight with recurrent binge eating and compensatory purging. Option D describes binge-eating disorder (no compensatory behaviors). Her low weight, fear of weight gain, body-image distortion, and absence of binge/purge behaviors define anorexia nervosa, restricting type. Amenorrhea and lanugo support chronic starvation.

stages.label: LABS & EKG
stages.content:
K 3.0 mEq/L
Phosphate 2.4 mg/dL (low-normal)
Magnesium 1.6 mg/dL
Glucose 62 mg/dL
TSH 1.4 mIU/L (low-normal free T4)
ECG: sinus bradycardia, rate 38, prolonged QTc 480 ms

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given her bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, and electrolyte abnormalities, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Discharge home with outpatient nutrition counseling
Hospitalization with cautious, gradual refeeding, cardiac monitoring, and correction of electrolytes
Aggressive high-calorie refeeding to rapidly restore weight
Start an SSRI as the primary treatment for the eating disorder
stages.explanation: Her vital sign instability (HR 38, hypotension, hypothermia), prolonged QTc, and electrolyte derangements meet criteria for medical hospitalization. Initial management is admission with cautious, gradual refeeding, continuous cardiac/telemetry monitoring, and correction of electrolytes (potassium, phosphate, magnesium). Discharge home is unsafe given hemodynamic instability and arrhythmia risk. Aggressive, rapid high-calorie refeeding is dangerous because it precipitates refeeding syndrome (severe hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and fluid shifts that can cause arrhythmias, heart failure, and death). SSRIs have limited efficacy in underweight anorexia and do not address the acute medical danger; nutritional rehabilitation is the priority. Slow, monitored refeeding with electrolyte repletion is the cornerstone of safe treatment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two days into refeeding she develops worsening weakness, and labs now show phosphate 1.2 mg/dL with new hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. What is the most likely complication and management?
options: Refeeding syndrome; slow the rate of feeding and aggressively replete phosphate, potassium, and magnesium with close monitoring
Sepsis; start broad-spectrum antibiotics
Diabetic ketoacidosis; begin an insulin drip
Normal refeeding response requiring no intervention
stages.explanation: This is refeeding syndrome. With reintroduction of carbohydrates, surging insulin drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium intracellularly, causing severe hypophosphatemia (1.2 mg/dL here) along with hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, which can precipitate cardiac arrhythmias, heart failure, respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis, and death. Management is to slow or temporarily hold the feeding advancement, aggressively replete phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, supplement thiamine, and monitor electrolytes closely (often with telemetry). Sepsis and DKA do not fit her clinical picture or labs (she is hypoglycemic, not hyperglycemic/ketotic, and afebrile without an infectious source). Calling this a normal response that needs no intervention is dangerous, as untreated refeeding syndrome is life-threatening. Recognizing and correcting hypophosphatemia is key.

stages.label: TREATMENT PLANNING
stages.question: Once she is medically stabilized, which is the most appropriate evidence-based treatment for an adolescent with anorexia nervosa?
options: Long-term benzodiazepine therapy
Family-based treatment (the Maudsley approach) along with nutritional rehabilitation and ongoing medical monitoring
Olanzapine monotherapy to cure the disorder
Immediate discharge once weight is normal, with no follow-up
stages.explanation: Family-based treatment (FBT, the Maudsley approach) has the strongest evidence for adolescents with anorexia nervosa; it empowers parents to take charge of refeeding and weight restoration while addressing the illness as a family, combined with ongoing nutritional rehabilitation and medical monitoring. Benzodiazepines do not treat anorexia and add dependence risk. Olanzapine may modestly help with weight gain and obsessive eating-related thoughts in some patients but is adjunctive, not curative monotherapy. Discharging once weight normalizes without follow-up ignores the high relapse rate and the need for sustained psychological and medical care. Adolescent anorexia is best managed with FBT plus continued nutritional and medical support, ideally within a multidisciplinary team.

pearls: Anorexia nervosa = restriction causing significantly low weight, intense fear of weight gain, and body-image disturbance; restricting type lacks binge/purge behaviors.
Medical instability (severe bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, prolonged QTc, electrolyte derangements) mandates hospitalization with cardiac monitoring.
Refeeding syndrome features hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia from insulin-driven intracellular shifts; prevent it with slow refeeding, thiamine, and electrolyte repletion.
Family-based treatment (Maudsley) is first-line for adolescent anorexia; medications are adjunctive at best.

cases.title: A Quiet Girl Who Won't Speak in Class
cases.chiefComplaint: "Her teacher says she has not said a single word at school all year, but at home she never stops talking."
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl is brought in by her mother because of concerns raised by her first-grade teacher. For the entire school year (about 5 months), she has not spoken to teachers or classmates and communicates only by nodding, pointing, or writing. At home with her parents and younger brother she is chatty, talkative, and bossy, with a rich vocabulary and clear, fluent speech. She also fails to speak with extended family she sees infrequently and freezes when strangers address her. She makes good eye contact with familiar people and plays normally at home. She met all language milestones, speaking in full sentences by age 3. There is no history of trauma, abuse, or recent stressor; she has been like this since starting preschool. She is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no allergies. She was born full-term without complications. Family history is notable for social anxiety disorder in her father and panic disorder in a maternal aunt. She lives with both parents and attends school full time.
cases.exam: A well-appearing girl who clings to her mother and avoids eye contact with the examiner, declining to answer questions verbally but nodding appropriately to yes/no prompts. When her mother steps just outside the room, she whispers a few words to her. Hearing screen is normal and she responds promptly to spoken commands, indicating intact comprehension. Oral-motor exam is normal with no structural abnormality of the palate or tongue. There are no dysmorphic features. She demonstrates reciprocal social play and shared attention with her mother. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal gait and tone. No tics, stereotypies, or repetitive behaviors are observed. Growth parameters are at the 50th percentile.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important first step to clarify the nature of her failure to speak at school?
options: Confirm that she has age-appropriate language and speaks normally in at least one setting
Order an MRI of the brain to evaluate for a structural lesion
Refer immediately for autism spectrum disorder evaluation
Begin a low-dose SSRI empirically
stages.explanation: The key distinction is whether the child has normal language capacity that fails only in specific social settings, versus a global language or developmental disorder. Establishing that she speaks fluently and normally at home but consistently fails to speak in expected social situations (school) defines the core of selective mutism. Documenting intact comprehension and a normal hearing screen further supports this. An MRI is not indicated without focal neurologic findings. Autism evaluation would be premature given her normal reciprocal social interaction and shared attention at home, though it remains on the differential.
