Empiric SSRI therapy precedes proper diagnosis and characterization, which is inappropriate. Confirming preserved language in at least one setting is the foundational diagnostic step.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her speech is fluent at home but consistently absent at school for 5 months. Which diagnosis best fits, and what is the key feature?
options: Autism spectrum disorder, given social communication deficits
Expressive language disorder, given the inability to produce speech
Selective mutism, given consistent failure to speak in specific social situations despite speaking elsewhere
Transient adjustment reaction, given the recent school transition
stages.explanation: Selective mutism (DSM-5, anxiety disorders chapter) is the consistent failure to speak in specific social situations where speaking is expected (e.g., school) despite speaking in other situations, lasting at least 1 month (beyond the first month of school) and interfering with functioning, not attributable to a communication disorder or lack of knowledge of the language. She speaks normally at home, ruling out an expressive language disorder. Autism spectrum disorder is unlikely given her normal reciprocal social play, shared attention, and absence of restricted/repetitive behaviors. A transient adjustment reaction would not persist consistently for 5 months across the whole year. Her presentation is the classic anxiety-driven, situation-specific mutism.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which associated condition is most important to screen for given the strong link with selective mutism?
options: Conduct disorder
Social anxiety disorder
Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder
Oppositional defiant disorder
stages.explanation: Selective mutism is conceptualized as an anxiety disorder and is very strongly associated with social anxiety disorder (social phobia); the large majority of children with selective mutism meet criteria for social anxiety, and the family history here (paternal social anxiety, maternal panic disorder) reinforces the anxiety-spectrum link. Screening for comorbid anxiety guides treatment. Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder involve willful rule-breaking or defiance; her mutism is anxiety-driven, not oppositional, and labeling it as defiance is a common error. ADHD is not specifically linked and her attention is intact. Recognizing the anxiety-spectrum nature is central to both diagnosis and management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her selective mutism?
options: Insistence that she speak before being allowed to participate, to extinguish avoidance
Cognitive behavioral therapy with behavioral techniques such as stimulus fading and shaping
Antipsychotic medication to reduce social withdrawal
Watchful waiting, as the condition reliably resolves on its own by adolescence
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for selective mutism is behaviorally based psychotherapy, principally CBT with techniques such as stimulus fading, shaping, and graded exposure delivered in coordination with the school. These approaches gradually reduce anxiety and increase verbal output in the feared setting. For more severe or refractory cases, an SSRI (e.g., fluoxetine) can be added, mirroring treatment of other childhood anxiety disorders. Coercing her to speak increases anxiety and is counterproductive. Antipsychotics are not indicated and carry undue risk. Watchful waiting is inappropriate because untreated selective mutism can persist and impair academic and social development; early intervention improves outcomes. Behavioral CBT is the evidence-based foundation.

pearls: Selective mutism is classified as an anxiety disorder and requires consistent failure to speak in specific social situations (e.g., school) for at least 1 month while speaking normally elsewhere.
Intact language in at least one setting and normal comprehension distinguish it from a communication disorder or autism spectrum disorder.
It is highly comorbid with social anxiety disorder; family history of anxiety is common.
First-line treatment is CBT with behavioral techniques (stimulus fading, shaping); SSRIs (e.g., fluoxetine) are added for severe or refractory cases.

cases.title: A Withdrawn Boy Who Doesn't Seek Comfort
cases.chiefComplaint: "He doesn't come to us when he's hurt or scared, and he barely shows any feelings."
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is referred by his adoptive parents, who finalized his adoption 4 months ago. He spent his first 3.5 years in an under-resourced orphanage with rotating caregivers and documented neglect, then 8 months in a foster placement before adoption. His adoptive parents describe him as emotionally flat and withdrawn: he rarely seeks comfort when distressed, does not respond to comforting when offered, and shows minimal positive affect even during play. He seldom initiates social or emotional reciprocity and watches other children rather than joining. He has unexplained episodes of irritability and sadness. He does make eye contact and uses language in simple sentences. He was born full-term; prenatal history is unknown. He has no known medical problems, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Hearing and vision screens are normal. He is not aggressive and does not approach strangers indiscriminately. Family history is unknown. He attends kindergarten, where his teacher notes he is quiet and rarely engages but follows classroom routines.
cases.exam: A thin but well-nourished boy who sits quietly and makes intermittent eye contact. Affect is markedly restricted and dysphoric; he does not smile during play and shows little positive emotion. When mildly frustrated by a difficult puzzle, he does not look to his mother for help or comfort and does not respond when she offers a hug. He uses simple, grammatically appropriate sentences and points to share an object once. He does not approach the examiner inappropriately or seek attention from strangers. No stereotypies, hand-flapping, or repetitive behaviors are seen. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There are no dysmorphic features and no signs of current physical injury. Growth is at the 25th percentile for height and weight.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his history and presentation, which element of his social history is most central to the likely diagnosis?
options: His simple sentence structure and limited vocabulary
His documented early neglect and absence of a stable, consistent caregiver
His placement in kindergarten at age 5
His height and weight at the 25th percentile
stages.explanation: The disorders most consistent with this picture (reactive attachment disorder and disinhibited social engagement disorder) are by definition the result of pathogenic care: social neglect, deprivation, or repeated changes of caregivers that prevent formation of stable attachments. His 3.5 years in an orphanage with rotating caregivers and documented neglect is the essential etiologic factor and a required criterion. His sentence structure is age-appropriate and does not point to a language disorder. Starting kindergarten is a normal milestone and not pathogenic. Growth at the 25th percentile is within normal limits and nonspecific. The history of grossly insufficient care is the linchpin of the diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He is emotionally withdrawn, rarely seeks or responds to comfort, and shows minimal positive affect, but does NOT approach strangers indiscriminately. Which diagnosis best fits?
options: Disinhibited social engagement disorder
Autism spectrum disorder
Reactive attachment disorder
Major depressive disorder
stages.explanation: Reactive attachment disorder (RAD) is characterized by a consistent pattern of inhibited, emotionally withdrawn behavior toward caregivers (rarely seeking or responding to comfort) plus persistent social/emotional disturbance (minimal positive affect, unexplained irritability/sadness/fearfulness), arising from a history of insufficient care, with the child having a developmental age of at least 9 months. Disinhibited social engagement disorder is the opposite phenotype, with overly familiar, indiscriminate approach to strangers, which he does not show. Autism spectrum disorder is excluded by his reciprocal eye contact, pointing to share, absence of stereotypies, and the clear pathogenic-care history. Major depression does not capture the attachment-specific withdrawal in this developmental context. The inhibited, withdrawn attachment pattern defines RAD.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which condition must be carefully ruled out because it can mimic the social withdrawal of reactive attachment disorder?
options: Autism spectrum disorder
Tourette syndrome
Specific learning disorder
Conduct disorder
stages.explanation: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is the principal mimic to exclude, because both can present with reduced social reciprocity and restricted affect. Distinguishing features favoring RAD include the clear history of pathogenic care, the capacity for selective social engagement, intact joint attention (pointing to share), absence of restricted/repetitive behaviors and sensory abnormalities, and potential for improvement with a stable caregiving environment. ASD attachment behaviors are typically present even with adequate caregiving and accompanied by core repetitive/restricted behaviors. Tourette syndrome (tics) and specific learning disorder do not explain the attachment disturbance. Conduct disorder involves rule-violating, aggressive behavior, not emotional withdrawal. Carefully differentiating RAD from ASD is the key diagnostic challenge.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT DATA
stages.content:
Hearing screen: pass bilaterally
Vision screen: normal
Autism diagnostic observation (ADOS-2): below cutoff for autism spectrum
Developmental screen: language and motor skills age-appropriate
Lead level: 2 µg/dL (normal)
Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 2.4 mIU/L (normal)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Workup excludes autism and medical mimics. What is the most appropriate first-line intervention for reactive attachment disorder?
options: Start an SSRI to treat the restricted affect and irritability
Provide psychoeducation and caregiver-focused therapy to build a stable, sensitive, consistent caregiving relationship
Begin a stimulant to improve his engagement and attention
Recommend removal from the adoptive home to a specialized residential facility
stages.explanation: The foundation of treatment for RAD is establishing and supporting a stable, emotionally available primary caregiving relationship, delivered through caregiver education and dyadic, attachment-focused psychotherapy (e.g., child-parent psychotherapy) that coaches sensitive, consistent, nurturing responses. The disorder typically improves when the child receives consistent, responsive care. There is no FDA-approved or first-line medication for the core attachment disturbance; SSRIs and stimulants do not treat RAD and are reserved only for specific comorbid disorders. Removing him from a now-stable adoptive home would be counterproductive and further disrupt attachment, the opposite of what is needed. Coercive or controversial 'attachment therapies' (e.g., holding/rebirthing) are contraindicated and unsafe. Caregiver-focused relational intervention is the evidence-based approach.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: His adoptive parents ask about prognosis. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: RAD is permanent and unresponsive to any intervention
With a stable, sensitive, and consistent caregiving environment, attachment behaviors and affect often improve substantially over time
He will inevitably develop a psychotic disorder in adolescence
Improvement is guaranteed within two weeks of placement
stages.explanation: RAD frequently improves when the child is placed in a stable, nurturing, and consistent caregiving environment; emotional responsiveness and the ability to seek and accept comfort can develop over months to years with sensitive, attuned care, making the adoptive parents' role central. It is not a fixed, untreatable condition, so saying it is permanent is incorrect and discouraging. RAD does not inevitably progress to a psychotic disorder; that claim has no basis. Recovery is gradual and depends on sustained, responsive caregiving, not a guaranteed two-week timeline. Honest, hopeful counseling that emphasizes the importance of consistency and patience supports the family and the child's developmental trajectory, and ongoing monitoring for comorbid conditions (e.g., anxiety, ADHD, learning issues) remains important.

pearls: Reactive attachment disorder requires a history of pathogenic care (neglect or repeated caregiver changes) and presents with inhibited, emotionally withdrawn behavior and failure to seek or respond to comfort.
It is the inhibited/withdrawn counterpart to disinhibited social engagement disorder, in which the child indiscriminately approaches strangers.
Autism spectrum disorder is the key mimic; intact joint attention, selective social engagement, absence of repetitive behaviors, and the pathogenic-care history favor RAD.
Treatment centers on building a stable, sensitive caregiving relationship; there is no first-line medication, and coercive 'attachment therapies' are contraindicated.

cases.title: A Boy Falling Behind His Peers
cases.chiefComplaint: "He's struggling in first grade and can't keep up with self-care like his classmates."
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is referred by his school for academic difficulties. His teacher reports he is well behind peers in reading, writing, and math, and struggles to follow multi-step instructions. His parents note he needs help dressing, has trouble with buttons and shoelaces, cannot reliably tell time or handle money, and requires more supervision than other children his age. Developmental history reveals delayed milestones: he walked at 20 months and spoke his first words at 2.5 years. He was born at 38 weeks; his mother reports she drank alcohol during the pregnancy. He has no seizures and no regression of skills. He is generally pleasant and sociable, makes good eye contact, and plays cooperatively, though with younger children. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He has no recurrent infections. Family history is noncontributory with no known genetic conditions. He lives with both parents and one older sibling who is developing typically. Hearing and vision screens at school were normal.
cases.exam: A cooperative, sociable boy in no distress who interacts warmly and maintains good eye contact. Height and head circumference are at the 5th percentile. Facial features include a smooth philtrum, thin vermilion border of the upper lip, and short palpebral fissures. He follows simple one-step commands but struggles with multi-step instructions. Speech is intelligible but simple for age. He cannot identify most letters or count reliably past ten. Neurologic exam shows normal tone, symmetric reflexes, and no focal deficits; gait is normal. No neurocutaneous stigmata such as cafe-au-lait macules or ash-leaf spots are seen. Cardiac and abdominal exams are unremarkable. He demonstrates age-inappropriate difficulty with adaptive tasks during the visit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Establishing a diagnosis of intellectual disability requires deficits in which two domains?
options: Motor coordination and sensory processing
Intellectual functioning and adaptive functioning, with onset during the developmental period
Attention and impulse control alone
Speech articulation and reading fluency only
stages.explanation: Intellectual disability (intellectual developmental disorder) is defined by deficits in BOTH intellectual functioning (reasoning, problem-solving, learning, confirmed by clinical assessment and standardized IQ testing) AND adaptive functioning (conceptual, social, and practical skills such as communication, self-care, and independent living), with onset during the developmental period. This boy shows academic delays (intellectual) plus difficulty with dressing, time, and money (adaptive), beginning early in development. Motor coordination and sensory processing alone describe other disorders. Attention/impulse control alone describes ADHD. Speech articulation and reading fluency alone describe communication or specific learning disorders. The dual requirement of intellectual AND adaptive deficits is the core of the definition, and severity is graded by adaptive functioning.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: His features and history raise concern for a specific etiology. Which test is the recommended first-tier genetic study in the evaluation of unexplained intellectual disability?
options: Karyotype only
Whole-body MRI
Chromosomal microarray analysis (and fragile X testing)
Serum heavy-metal panel
stages.explanation: When the etiology of intellectual disability is unclear, chromosomal microarray analysis is the recommended first-tier genetic test because it detects copy-number variants (microdeletions/duplications) that a standard karyotype misses; fragile X testing (FMR1) is also recommended, especially given its frequency as an inherited cause. Here, the dysmorphic facial features and maternal alcohol use suggest fetal alcohol spectrum disorder clinically, but a genetic workup is still standard to exclude other causes. A karyotype alone has lower diagnostic yield than microarray. Whole-body MRI is not indicated; brain MRI may be considered if there are focal neurologic findings or micro/macrocephaly. A heavy-metal panel is not a routine first-tier test, though lead screening is reasonable. Microarray plus fragile X testing is the standard first-tier genetic evaluation.

stages.label: TESTING RESULTS
stages.content:
WISC-V Full-Scale IQ: 55
Vineland Adaptive Behavior Composite: 58 (well below average)
Chromosomal microarray: no pathogenic copy-number variants
Fragile X (FMR1) testing: normal CGG repeat number
Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 2.2 mIU/L (normal)
Lead level: 3 µg/dL (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: IQ is 55 and adaptive composite is 58, with characteristic facial features and a history of prenatal alcohol exposure. What is the most likely etiology of his intellectual disability?
options: Fragile X syndrome
Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder
Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
Lead encephalopathy
stages.explanation: The combination of prenatal alcohol exposure, growth restriction (height and head circumference at the 5th percentile), and the classic facial triad (smooth philtrum, thin vermilion border, short palpebral fissures) with neurodevelopmental impairment is diagnostic of fetal alcohol spectrum disorder, the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability. Fragile X is excluded by normal FMR1 testing and would classically show a long face, large ears, and macroorchidism. Down syndrome has a distinct facial phenotype and would be detected on microarray/karyotype. Lead encephalopathy is excluded by the normal lead level and lacks the dysmorphic features. The dysmorphology plus exposure history pinpoints fetal alcohol spectrum disorder, while IQ and adaptive scores confirm intellectual disability in the moderate range.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in managing his intellectual disability?
options: Initiate a stimulant to improve his academic performance
Refer for an Individualized Education Program (IEP) with multidisciplinary support and adaptive-skills training
Restrict his schooling until his IQ improves with tutoring
Start an antipsychotic to address developmental delay
stages.explanation: Management of intellectual disability is supportive and multidisciplinary, centered on an Individualized Education Program (IEP) under special-education law, with speech, occupational, and physical therapy as needed and structured adaptive-skills training to maximize independence. Early intervention and family support improve functional outcomes. Stimulants treat comorbid ADHD but do not raise IQ or treat intellectual disability itself, and there is no indication of ADHD here. Restricting schooling is harmful and illegal under disability education protections; these children benefit from inclusive, supported education. Antipsychotics are reserved for specific severe behavioral or psychiatric comorbidities, not for developmental delay per se, and carry significant metabolic risk. A coordinated educational and habilitative plan via an IEP is the cornerstone of care.

cases.diagnosis: Intellectual disability due to fetal alcohol spectrum disorder
pearls: Intellectual disability requires deficits in BOTH intellectual and adaptive functioning with onset in the developmental period; severity is graded by adaptive functioning, not IQ alone.
Chromosomal microarray plus fragile X (FMR1) testing is the first-tier genetic workup for unexplained intellectual disability.
Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder is the leading preventable cause: prenatal alcohol exposure, growth restriction, and the facial triad (smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, short palpebral fissures).
Management is multidisciplinary and supportive, anchored by an IEP and adaptive-skills training; medications target comorbidities only.

cases.title: Wet Sheets in the Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: "He keeps wetting the bed at night and is too embarrassed to go to sleepovers."
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for nighttime bedwetting that has never fully resolved. He wets the bed about 4 nights per week, only during sleep, and has never had a sustained period of dry nights. He has no daytime incontinence, urgency, frequency, dysuria, or straining, and his daytime voiding is completely normal. There is no history of urinary tract infections, constipation, snoring, or daytime sleepiness. He is not excessively thirsty and there is no recent weight change. He is developing normally, doing well academically, and has friends, though he avoids sleepovers and camp because of embarrassment. There has been no recent stressor, new sibling, or change at home, and the bedwetting predates any life events. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He was toilet-trained for daytime by age 3. Family history is notable for the father having wet the bed until about age 9. He lives with both parents and a younger sister.
cases.exam: A healthy, well-developed boy in no distress with normal growth parameters at the 50th percentile. Abdomen is soft and nontender without a palpable bladder, mass, or stool-loaded colon. Genitourinary exam shows a normally positioned urethral meatus with no phimosis or lesions. The lumbosacral spine is normal with no sacral dimple, hair tuft, or skin discoloration. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal lower-extremity tone, strength, reflexes, and sensation; gait is normal. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Mental status is appropriate and he is appropriately embarrassed when discussing the problem. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history, how is his condition best classified?
options: Secondary enuresis, because bedwetting recurred after a dry period
Primary nocturnal enuresis, because he has never achieved sustained nighttime dryness and wets only during sleep
Diurnal enuresis, because of daytime urinary symptoms
Functional urinary incontinence due to a structural anomaly
stages.explanation: Enuresis is repeated voiding into clothing or bed at least twice weekly for 3 months (or causing significant distress/impairment) in a child at least 5 years old. This boy has primary nocturnal enuresis: he has never had a sustained dry period (primary), and wetting occurs only during sleep (nocturnal). Secondary enuresis applies when a child relapses after at least 6 months of dryness, which is not the case here. Diurnal (daytime) enuresis requires daytime symptoms, which he lacks. There is no evidence of a structural anomaly on history or exam. Classifying it as primary nocturnal enuresis is essential because it is overwhelmingly a benign, self-limited maturational issue, often with a positive family history as seen with his father.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test in this child with isolated nocturnal enuresis and a normal exam?
options: Urinalysis
Renal and bladder ultrasound
Voiding cystourethrogram
MRI of the lumbosacral spine
stages.explanation: A simple urinalysis is the only routine test needed in uncomplicated primary nocturnal enuresis with a normal history and exam. It screens for urinary tract infection, glucosuria (diabetes mellitus), and low specific gravity (diabetes insipidus or a concentrating defect). Imaging such as renal/bladder ultrasound or a voiding cystourethrogram is reserved for daytime symptoms, recurrent UTIs, an abnormal stream, or suspected structural disease, none of which are present. Lumbosacral MRI is indicated only if there are neurologic findings or signs of occult spinal dysraphism (sacral dimple, hair tuft), which his exam excludes. Pursuing extensive imaging in a child with isolated, otherwise normal nocturnal enuresis is unnecessary and not cost-effective; urinalysis is the appropriate first step.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: clear, yellow
Specific gravity 1.022
Glucose: negative
Protein: negative
Leukocyte esterase: negative
Nitrites: negative
Microscopy: no bacteria, <5 WBC/hpf

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His urinalysis is normal. After reassurance and behavioral measures, the family wants active treatment. Which option has the highest long-term cure rate and is recommended as first-line active therapy?
options: Oral desmopressin nightly
Enuresis (bedwetting) alarm therapy
Imipramine at bedtime
Oxybutynin nightly
stages.explanation: The enuresis alarm is the first-line active treatment with the highest long-term cure rate and the lowest relapse rate, because it conditions the child to wake to (or inhibit) bladder fullness; it requires motivation and several weeks to work. Desmopressin (an ADH analog reducing nighttime urine production) works faster and is useful for short-term needs such as sleepovers or camp, but relapse is common after stopping, so it is generally second-line for durable cure. Imipramine, a tricyclic, is effective but carries cardiotoxicity and overdose risk, making it a later-line option. Oxybutynin (anticholinergic) targets overactive bladder/daytime symptoms, which he does not have. First-line behavioral measures plus the alarm offer the best chance of lasting resolution.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: The family asks about prognosis and reasonable expectations. What is the most accurate counseling point?
options: He likely has a serious underlying disease that requires lifelong therapy
Most children with primary nocturnal enuresis outgrow it spontaneously, with high rates of resolution each year, and treatment can speed dryness
Punishment for wet nights is an effective strategy to accelerate dryness
Fluid should be severely restricted throughout the entire day
stages.explanation: Primary nocturnal enuresis is overwhelmingly benign and self-limited; a substantial proportion of affected children become dry each year through normal maturation, and a positive family history (his father) predicts eventual resolution. Reassurance plus active treatment (alarm or desmopressin) can hasten dryness and reduce psychosocial distress. There is no serious underlying disease given his normal history, exam, and urinalysis. Punishment is counterproductive, harms self-esteem, and is never recommended; a supportive, non-blaming approach is essential. Sensible measures include avoiding caffeine, limiting fluids in the evening, and voiding before bed, but severe daytime fluid restriction is inappropriate and unnecessary. Accurate, reassuring counseling about the favorable natural history is the key teaching point.

pearls: Enuresis requires age at least 5 years; primary nocturnal enuresis means the child has never achieved sustained nighttime dryness and wets only during sleep.
Urinalysis is the only routine workup needed when the history and exam are otherwise normal; imaging is reserved for daytime symptoms, abnormal stream, or recurrent UTIs.
The bedwetting alarm is first-line active therapy with the highest long-term cure rate; desmopressin works faster but relapses are common, useful for sleepovers/camp.
The condition is benign and self-limited with high annual spontaneous resolution; punishment is harmful and contraindicated.

cases.title: A Racing Heart Before Finals
cases.chiefComplaint: "My heart's been pounding and I can't sleep or stop feeling jittery during finals week."
cases.history: A 21-year-old college junior presents during finals week with palpitations, a sense of nervousness and jitteriness, and difficulty sleeping for the past week. He notes a racing heart, restlessness, mild hand tremor, and frequent trips to the bathroom. Symptoms are worst in the afternoons and evenings while he studies. On questioning, he reports drinking 4 to 6 large energy drinks plus several cups of coffee daily to stay awake cramming, far more than his usual one coffee per day. He also feels his thoughts are racing and reports muscle twitching. He denies chest pain, syncope, fever, weight loss, heat intolerance, or diarrhea. He denies a prior history of anxiety, panic attacks, or psychiatric illness, and reports no symptoms when school is not in session. He takes no prescription medications, uses no illicit drugs, and denies tobacco; he drinks alcohol socially. He has no allergies. Family history is noncontributory. He is otherwise healthy and was at his baseline two weeks ago before exams began.
cases.exam: An anxious-appearing but cooperative young man who is alert and oriented. He has a fine resting tremor of the outstretched hands and mild diaphoresis. Cardiac exam reveals a regular, tachycardic rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no irregularly irregular rhythm is appreciated. Lungs are clear. The thyroid is non-enlarged and non-tender without nodules or bruit; there is no lid lag, proptosis, or stare. Deep tendon reflexes are normal, not hyperreflexic. Skin is warm but not flushed. No goiter is present. Mental status shows mild psychomotor restlessness with logical, linear thought process and no perceptual disturbances. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important next step in clarifying the cause of his symptoms?
options: Obtain a detailed history of caffeine and stimulant intake
Start a benzodiazepine empirically for presumed anxiety
Order a CT angiogram of the chest
Begin a beta-blocker before any further history
stages.explanation: A focused intake history quickly identifies the likely cause: he is consuming an enormous caffeine load (4 to 6 large energy drinks plus several coffees daily, well above his baseline) during finals week, and his symptoms (palpitations, jitteriness, insomnia, tremor, diuresis, restlessness, racing thoughts) are the classic features of caffeine intoxication and caffeine-induced anxiety. Establishing the dose and temporal relationship is the highest-yield step and may obviate unnecessary testing. Empiric benzodiazepines risk dependence and treat a presumed diagnosis before it is established. A CT angiogram is not indicated without features of pulmonary embolism. Starting a beta-blocker before taking a history is premature. Quantifying caffeine intake is the essential first move that anchors the diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His symptoms began with heavy energy-drink use during exams and resolve when school is out. Which diagnosis best explains his presentation?
options: Generalized anxiety disorder
Hyperthyroidism (Graves disease)
Caffeine-induced anxiety disorder
Panic disorder
stages.explanation: Caffeine-induced anxiety disorder is diagnosed when prominent anxiety symptoms develop during or soon after caffeine intoxication/excessive use and are judged to be caused by caffeine rather than a primary anxiety disorder. His symptoms are tightly linked to a massive caffeine load and the stress of finals, with no symptoms at baseline. Generalized anxiety disorder requires excessive worry on most days for at least 6 months, which he lacks. Hyperthyroidism can mimic this, but he has no goiter, lid lag, proptosis, hyperreflexia, weight loss, or heat intolerance, making it less likely (and easily excluded with a TSH). Panic disorder involves recurrent unexpected panic attacks with persistent concern, not a substance-driven, situational course. The clear caffeine relationship makes substance-induced anxiety the best fit.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
Free T4: within normal limits
Electrolytes: within normal limits
Urine toxicology: negative
ECG: sinus tachycardia at 104 bpm, no ischemic changes, normal QTc

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Labs and ECG confirm sinus tachycardia with a normal TSH. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start a long-term SSRI for an anxiety disorder
Counsel on reducing/eliminating caffeine and improving sleep hygiene, with reassurance
Prescribe daily alprazolam through the end of the semester
Initiate methimazole for presumed thyroid overactivity
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment is removing the offending agent: counsel him to taper and markedly reduce caffeine intake, with attention to sleep hygiene, hydration, and stress management around exams; symptoms resolve as caffeine is cleared. Reassurance and education are key, and a gradual taper avoids caffeine-withdrawal headache and fatigue. A long-term SSRI is not warranted for a substance-induced, situational problem without a primary anxiety disorder. Daily alprazolam (a benzodiazepine) carries dependence and sedation risk and treats the wrong target. Methimazole is inappropriate because his TSH and free T4 are normal, excluding hyperthyroidism. Addressing the caffeine source with behavioral counseling is the correct, low-risk management and is curative here.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: He asks what to expect if he cuts back on caffeine abruptly. What should you tell him about caffeine withdrawal?
options: Abrupt cessation commonly causes headache, fatigue, irritability, and difficulty concentrating, peaking within 1-2 days and resolving within about a week
Caffeine withdrawal causes life-threatening seizures and requires hospitalization
There is no such thing as caffeine withdrawal
Withdrawal symptoms last for several months and are permanent in many people
stages.explanation: Caffeine withdrawal is a recognized DSM-5 syndrome: abrupt reduction after regular heavy use causes headache, fatigue, drowsiness, irritability, low mood, and difficulty concentrating, typically beginning 12-24 hours after the last dose, peaking around days 1-2, and resolving within about a week. Counseling him to taper gradually rather than stop cold turkey minimizes these symptoms. Caffeine withdrawal does not cause seizures or require hospitalization; that pattern characterizes alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal, not caffeine. It is incorrect to say withdrawal does not exist, since it is a well-defined, common syndrome. Symptoms are self-limited over days, not permanent or lasting months. Realistic expectations and a gentle taper improve adherence and comfort as he reduces intake.

pearls: Caffeine-induced anxiety disorder presents with palpitations, jitteriness, insomnia, tremor, diuresis, and restlessness temporally linked to excessive caffeine intake.
Energy drinks carry very high caffeine loads; quantifying intake is the key diagnostic step.
Hyperthyroidism is the main mimic; a normal TSH and absence of goiter, lid lag, and weight loss help exclude it.
Treatment is caffeine reduction/elimination plus sleep hygiene and reassurance, not chronic benzodiazepines or SSRIs; taper to avoid withdrawal headache.

cases.title: Grief That Won't Lift After a Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: "It's been seven weeks since my husband died and I still feel like I'm drowning in sadness."
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman presents 7 weeks after her husband of 32 years died of pancreatic cancer. She reports waves of intense sadness, frequent crying, and difficulty falling asleep, especially on the side of the bed he used. She has poor appetite with a 2 kg weight loss and finds it hard to concentrate at work. She still attends her book club intermittently and laughs with her grandchildren, and her sadness comes in 'pangs' often triggered by reminders of him, with periods in between where she functions. She feels his presence at times and once thought she heard his voice, which she found comforting and recognizes as not real. She denies feeling worthless, denies pervasive guilt beyond wishing she had noticed his illness sooner, and denies any thoughts of suicide or wanting to die, though she sometimes wishes she could 'be with him.' She has no prior psychiatric history. She takes lisinopril for hypertension and has no allergies. She drinks wine occasionally and does not use drugs. Family history is notable for depression in a sister. She lives alone now, with supportive adult children nearby.
cases.exam: A tearful but engaged woman who is well-groomed and cooperative. Affect is sad but reactive; she smiles when describing her grandchildren and maintains good eye contact. Speech is normal in rate and volume. Thought process is linear and goal-directed. She denies suicidal or homicidal ideation, plan, or intent, and denies command hallucinations; the fleeting sense of her husband's presence is ego-syntonic and recognized as grief-related. There are no delusions and no psychomotor retardation. She is fully oriented with intact attention and memory. Insight and judgment are good. No signs of self-harm are present. Cardiac, pulmonary, and neurologic exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of her presentation is most consistent with normal grief rather than major depressive disorder?
options: Sadness occurring in waves triggered by reminders, with preserved capacity for positive emotion and self-esteem
Pervasive, persistent low mood with anhedonia present nearly all day, every day
Persistent feelings of worthlessness and global guilt unrelated to the loss
Psychomotor retardation and inability to function in any domain
stages.explanation: Normal (uncomplicated) grief characteristically comes in waves or pangs triggered by reminders of the deceased, interspersed with periods of preserved positive emotion and the ability to be comforted; self-esteem is generally intact, and any guilt tends to focus on the deceased or the circumstances of death (e.g., wishing she had noticed his illness sooner). In contrast, MDD features pervasive, persistent depressed mood and anhedonia most of the day nearly every day, global worthlessness, and broad functional impairment. Her reactive affect, continued enjoyment of her grandchildren, intact self-esteem, and grief-focused (not global) guilt point to normal grief. Persistent worthlessness, anhedonia, and psychomotor retardation would instead raise concern for MDD. Recognizing the wave-like, reactive quality of grief is the key initial distinction.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: She reports sometimes wishing she could 'be with him' and hearing his voice. How should these symptoms be interpreted?
options: They indicate active suicidal ideation and psychosis requiring immediate hospitalization
Passive yearning to be with the deceased and transient, comforting sensory experiences of the deceased can be normal in grief and do not by themselves indicate MDD or psychosis
They are diagnostic of major depressive disorder with psychotic features
They confirm a primary psychotic disorder
stages.explanation: In normal grief, the bereaved may experience a passive wish to join the deceased without any intent, plan, or active suicidality, and may have transient, comforting hallucinatory experiences (hearing or sensing the deceased) that they recognize as not real and that are ego-syntonic and culturally normative. These do not by themselves indicate MDD or a psychotic disorder. However, clinicians must always probe for true suicidal ideation, plan, and intent, which she denies. They are not diagnostic of MDD with psychotic features, which would involve mood-congruent delusions or persistent hallucinations with pervasive depression. A primary psychotic disorder is excluded by the grief context, insight, and absence of other psychotic symptoms. Distinguishing grief phenomena from pathology is essential and avoids both over- and under-treatment.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which scenario would warrant a diagnosis of major depressive disorder rather than normal bereavement in this patient?
options: Continued waves of sadness triggered by anniversaries over the coming months
Development of pervasive depressed mood, anhedonia, worthlessness, marked psychomotor slowing, and active suicidal ideation that persist most of the day, nearly every day
Crying when looking at old photographs of her husband
Difficulty sleeping on his side of the bed for a few weeks
stages.explanation: MDD can be diagnosed even in the context of bereavement when a full major depressive episode emerges: pervasive depressed mood and anhedonia most of the day nearly every day, with associated symptoms such as worthlessness, marked psychomotor retardation, and especially active suicidal ideation, persisting and causing impairment. DSM-5 removed the prior 'bereavement exclusion,' so clinical judgment distinguishes the normal pangs of grief from a superimposed depressive episode. Anniversary reactions, crying at photographs, and transient sleep disturbance on his side of the bed are expected grief phenomena, not MDD. Worthlessness (as opposed to grief-focused guilt), psychomotor retardation, and active suicidality are red flags that tip toward a diagnosable depressive episode requiring treatment. Recognizing these markers guides appropriate intervention.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: At this 7-week visit she meets criteria for normal grief, not MDD. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start an SSRI immediately to prevent progression to depression
Prescribe a standing benzodiazepine for several months
Provide supportive counseling, validation, and close follow-up, with referral to grief support resources
Recommend inpatient psychiatric admission
stages.explanation: Normal grief is not a disorder and does not require pharmacotherapy; the appropriate approach is supportive, with empathic validation, psychoeducation about the normal grief trajectory, encouragement of social support, and grief support resources, plus scheduled follow-up to monitor for emerging MDD or prolonged grief disorder. Starting an SSRI is not indicated for uncomplicated grief and is reserved for a superimposed major depressive episode. Standing benzodiazepines risk dependence and do not treat grief; short-term use for severe insomnia, if any, should be limited. Inpatient admission is unwarranted given no suicidal intent or psychosis. If grief becomes persistent, pervasive, and impairing beyond about a year with intense yearning and preoccupation, prolonged grief disorder should be considered and treated with grief-targeted psychotherapy. Supportive care with follow-up is correct.

pearls: Normal grief comes in waves triggered by reminders, with preserved self-esteem, reactive affect, and grief-focused (not global) guilt.
A passive wish to join the deceased and transient comforting hallucinations of the deceased can be normal, but always screen explicitly for active suicidal ideation.
DSM-5 removed the bereavement exclusion: MDD is diagnosed when a full depressive episode (pervasive mood/anhedonia, worthlessness, psychomotor slowing, active suicidality) emerges.
Uncomplicated grief is managed supportively; prolonged grief disorder (persistent, impairing yearning beyond about a year) warrants grief-targeted psychotherapy.

cases.title: A Worried Woman on a Long-Standing Sleep Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: "I've always been a worrier, and I can't function without my nerve pill, but I've been so unsteady lately."
cases.history: A 77-year-old woman presents for a routine visit and refill. She has chronic, excessive worry about her health, finances, and family that she has had 'all her life,' with associated restlessness, muscle tension, and trouble sleeping. For roughly 12 years she has taken clonazepam 0.5 mg twice daily, originally prescribed for anxiety and insomnia, and she takes an extra dose during stressful periods. Over the past 6 months she reports two falls without fracture, increasing daytime drowsiness, and her daughter notes she seems more forgetful and word-finding has worsened. She denies chest pain, palpitations, or weight loss. Other medications include amlodipine for hypertension and acetaminophen as needed; she has no allergies. She does not drink alcohol or use other sedatives. She lives alone in a two-story home and still drives. Family history is noncontributory. She is fearful about reducing the clonazepam, saying she 'can't sleep or cope' without it. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: An alert, mildly anxious woman who appears her stated age. She is oriented to person, place, and time; a Montreal Cognitive Assessment shows mild deficits in attention and delayed recall. Gait is slightly slow and unsteady with a positive Romberg-like sway, and she requires a moment to rise from the chair. There is no resting tremor, rigidity, or focal weakness; reflexes are symmetric. Mild psychomotor slowing is noted. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. There is no orthostatic hypotension on repeat measurement. No edema. Affect is anxious but reactive, and she denies suicidal ideation. The remainder of the neurologic and general exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most pressing concern related to her chronic clonazepam use in this 77-year-old?
options: Long-term benzodiazepine use is entirely benign in older adults
The main risk is hepatotoxicity from clonazepam
Benzodiazepines protect against dementia in the elderly
Benzodiazepines in older adults increase the risk of falls, fractures, cognitive impairment, and sedation
stages.explanation: In older adults, benzodiazepines (especially long-term and longer-acting agents) substantially increase the risk of falls, hip fractures, cognitive impairment, confusion, daytime sedation, and motor vehicle accidents, and are listed on the Beers Criteria as potentially inappropriate medications to avoid in this population. This patient's two recent falls, unsteady gait, daytime drowsiness, and new cognitive complaints are textbook benzodiazepine-related adverse effects. Long-term use is far from benign in the elderly. Hepatotoxicity is not the principal concern with benzodiazepines. Benzodiazepines do not protect against dementia; if anything, chronic use is associated with cognitive decline and impairs cognition acutely. Recognizing the fall, fracture, and cognitive risks drives the management plan toward deprescribing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Setting aside the medication issue, her lifelong pattern of excessive, hard-to-control worry about multiple domains with restlessness, muscle tension, and sleep disturbance is most consistent with which disorder?
options: Generalized anxiety disorder
Panic disorder
Adjustment disorder
Obsessive-compulsive disorder
stages.explanation: Generalized anxiety disorder is defined by excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least 6 months about multiple domains, difficult to control, with at least three associated symptoms such as restlessness, muscle tension, fatigue, irritability, concentration difficulty, or sleep disturbance. Her lifelong, pervasive worry about health, finances, and family with restlessness, muscle tension, and insomnia fits GAD well. Panic disorder centers on recurrent unexpected panic attacks, which she does not describe. Adjustment disorder requires an identifiable stressor with onset within 3 months and is time-limited, not lifelong. Obsessive-compulsive disorder involves intrusive obsessions and compulsions, which are absent. GAD is the best fit and is common in older adults, where it is frequently undertreated or inappropriately managed with benzodiazepines.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate strategy to address her benzodiazepine use?
options: Stop clonazepam abruptly today to eliminate the fall risk
Continue clonazepam indefinitely at the current dose since she feels she needs it
Initiate a slow, gradual taper of clonazepam while starting an SSRI/SNRI and offering CBT for her anxiety
Switch her to alprazolam, a shorter-acting benzodiazepine, for long-term use
stages.explanation: The correct approach is gradual deprescribing: slowly taper the clonazepam (abrupt discontinuation after years of use risks withdrawal, seizures, and rebound anxiety) while substituting an evidence-based long-term treatment for GAD, namely an SSRI or SNRI, and offering cognitive behavioral therapy, which is highly effective for late-life anxiety. Patient engagement and a slow schedule improve success. Abrupt cessation is dangerous given dependence after 12 years. Continuing indefinitely perpetuates the fall, fracture, and cognitive risks. Switching to alprazolam is worse: short-acting, high-potency benzodiazepines carry greater dependence and inter-dose withdrawal and are even less appropriate for chronic use in the elderly. A structured taper paired with an SSRI/SNRI and CBT is the guideline-concordant plan.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During a too-rapid taper, an older patient on long-term benzodiazepines is at greatest risk of which serious withdrawal complication?
options: Hypertensive emergency from catecholamine surge
Withdrawal seizures and delirium
Serotonin syndrome
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepine withdrawal, particularly after long-term use and with rapid dose reduction, can cause rebound anxiety, insomnia, autonomic instability, and most dangerously withdrawal seizures and a delirium-like state resembling alcohol withdrawal, because both act on GABA-A receptors. This is why tapers must be slow and individualized. A hypertensive emergency from a catecholamine surge characterizes pheochromocytoma or MAOI-tyramine reactions, not benzodiazepine withdrawal. Serotonin syndrome results from excess serotonergic activity (e.g., drug combinations), not benzodiazepine cessation. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a reaction to dopamine-blocking antipsychotics. Anticipating withdrawal seizures and delirium underscores why deprescribing in long-term users must be gradual and closely monitored, especially in a vulnerable older adult.

cases.diagnosis: Generalized anxiety disorder with high-risk long-term benzodiazepine use in an older adult
pearls: Benzodiazepines are Beers Criteria potentially inappropriate medications in older adults due to falls, fractures, sedation, and cognitive impairment.
Generalized anxiety disorder is common and undertreated in the elderly; long-term management is an SSRI/SNRI plus CBT, not chronic benzodiazepines.
Deprescribe long-term benzodiazepines with a slow, individualized taper while substituting evidence-based therapy; never stop abruptly.
Rapid benzodiazepine withdrawal can cause rebound anxiety, seizures, and delirium because both act on GABA-A receptors.

cases.title: Dizziness and Electric Jolts After Stopping a Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: "I feel dizzy and flu-like, and I keep getting these weird electric-shock sensations in my head."
cases.history: A 37-year-old woman presents with 4 days of dizziness, nausea, fatigue, and flu-like malaise, along with brief 'electric shock' or 'zapping' sensations in her head, particularly when she moves her eyes. She also reports irritability, vivid dreams, and a sense of imbalance. About 5 days ago she ran out of her paroxetine 20 mg daily, which she had been taking for the past 2 years for depression, and did not refill it because she 'felt fine.' She denies fever, sick contacts, vomiting, headache with neck stiffness, or focal weakness. She denies a return of depressed mood, anhedonia, or suicidal ideation. She has no other medical problems, takes no other medications, and has no allergies. She does not use alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs. There has been no recent travel. Family history is notable for depression in her mother. She works as an accountant and is concerned the symptoms are a sign of a serious illness. She had no head trauma.
cases.exam: An uncomfortable but nontoxic-appearing woman who is alert and fully oriented. She describes transient paresthesia-like 'zaps' during the exam when shifting her gaze. Neurologic exam shows normal cranial nerves, no nystagmus at rest, symmetric strength and sensation, intact coordination, and a normal but cautious gait; Romberg is negative. There is no neck rigidity and no photophobia. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal, with no orthostatic blood pressure change on repeat testing. Affect is mildly anxious but reactive, with no psychomotor slowing; she denies suicidal ideation. There is no rash. The remainder of the general and neurologic examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the timing relative to her medication, what is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?
options: Relapse of major depressive disorder
Viral labyrinthitis
Serotonin syndrome
Antidepressant (SSRI) discontinuation syndrome
stages.explanation: Her constellation of dizziness, flu-like malaise, nausea, imbalance, vivid dreams, irritability, and especially the hallmark 'brain zaps' (electric-shock sensations, often provoked by eye movement) beginning within days of abruptly stopping paroxetine is classic antidepressant discontinuation syndrome. Paroxetine, a short-half-life SSRI with anticholinergic properties, is among the most likely to cause it. Depression relapse is unlikely this quickly and she denies depressed mood/anhedonia; relapse evolves over weeks, not days, and lacks brain zaps. Viral labyrinthitis would feature true vertigo/nystagmus and lacks the temporal link to stopping medication and the zaps. Serotonin syndrome arises from excess serotonergic activity (often added agents) with hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia, and autonomic instability, the opposite of this picture. The tight temporal relationship to stopping paroxetine clinches discontinuation syndrome.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A useful mnemonic for SSRI discontinuation symptoms is 'FINISH.' Which set of features does it capture?
options: Fever, Insomnia, Nausea, Inattention, Sweating, Hyperthermia
Flu-like symptoms, Insomnia, Nausea, Imbalance, Sensory disturbances, Hyperarousal
Fatigue, Indigestion, Numbness, Itching, Stiffness, Headache
Flushing, Irritability, Nystagmus, Incontinence, Seizures, Hypertension
stages.explanation: The FINISH mnemonic summarizes discontinuation symptoms: Flu-like symptoms (fatigue, myalgias), Insomnia (with vivid dreams/nightmares), Nausea, Imbalance (dizziness, vertigo, gait unsteadiness), Sensory disturbances (paresthesias and the classic 'electric shock'/brain zaps), and Hyperarousal (anxiety, irritability, agitation). This matches the patient's presentation precisely. The other options mix in features not characteristic of discontinuation (e.g., hyperthermia and seizures suggest other syndromes such as serotonin syndrome or withdrawal states). Discontinuation symptoms typically begin within days of stopping or markedly reducing a serotonergic antidepressant, are generally mild and self-limited, and resolve quickly with reinstatement. Knowing FINISH helps clinicians recognize the syndrome and avoid misattributing it to relapse or a new illness.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for her symptoms?
options: Reassure and offer symptomatic care, or restart paroxetine and then taper it slowly
Start a benzodiazepine long-term for the dizziness
Obtain an urgent CT head and lumbar puncture
Add a second SSRI to the regimen
stages.explanation: Discontinuation syndrome is self-limited and benign; management is reassurance and symptomatic care for mild cases, or, when symptoms are distressing, reinstating the original antidepressant (paroxetine) with subsequent gradual tapering over weeks to prevent recurrence. If she and her clinician decide to remain off it, a slow taper or a brief bridge can be used. A long-term benzodiazepine is unnecessary and risks dependence. Urgent CT head and lumbar puncture are not warranted given a benign exam, no fever, no meningismus, and an obvious medication trigger. Adding a second SSRI is inappropriate and could risk serotonin toxicity without addressing the issue. The key principle, and a major prevention strategy, is to taper antidepressants gradually rather than stop abruptly, particularly short-half-life agents like paroxetine.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: She asks how this could have been prevented and which antidepressants are most likely to cause it. What is the best counseling?
options: All antidepressants cause identical discontinuation risk regardless of half-life
Short-half-life agents like paroxetine and venlafaxine carry the highest risk; fluoxetine's long half-life makes it least likely, and gradual tapering prevents the syndrome
Fluoxetine is the most likely to cause discontinuation syndrome because of its long half-life
Discontinuation syndrome only occurs after fewer than 2 weeks of use
stages.explanation: Discontinuation risk is greatest with short-half-life antidepressants that have abrupt drops in plasma levels, notably paroxetine and venlafaxine (and the SNRI duloxetine), whereas fluoxetine, with its long half-life and active metabolite norfluoxetine, self-tapers and rarely causes the syndrome. The principal prevention is gradual dose reduction over weeks to months rather than abrupt cessation, and counseling patients not to stop on their own. It is not true that all agents carry equal risk; fluoxetine is the least, not the most, likely culprit. The syndrome typically follows continuous use beyond several weeks (commonly with chronic therapy), not specifically less than 2 weeks. Educating her about half-life differences and the importance of a planned taper prevents recurrence and unnecessary alarm.

pearls: Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome begins within days of abruptly stopping a serotonergic agent and features the FINISH symptoms, with 'brain zaps' being the hallmark.
Short-half-life agents (paroxetine, venlafaxine, duloxetine) carry the highest risk; fluoxetine's long half-life makes it least likely.
It is benign and self-limited; manage with reassurance, or reinstate and taper slowly if symptoms are distressing.
Prevention is gradual tapering over weeks, not abrupt cessation; distinguish from depression relapse (slower, with mood symptoms) and serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia).

cases.title: Fever in a Patient on a Special Antipsychotic
cases.chiefComplaint: "I've had a fever and sore throat for two days, and I'm on that medication that needs blood tests."
cases.history: A 45-year-old man with treatment-refractory schizophrenia presents with 2 days of fever, sore throat, and malaise. He has been stable on clozapine, titrated to 400 mg daily over the past 8 months after failing trials of risperidone and olanzapine. He reports chills and a painful throat but denies cough, shortness of breath, dysuria, or rash. He has no abdominal pain. He admits he missed his last two weekly blood draws because he 'felt fine' and found the appointments inconvenient. Other medications are none; he has no allergies. He does not use alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs. He lives in a group home and is adherent to clozapine. There are no recent sick contacts that he knows of and no recent travel. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no recent dental work or skin infection. His psychiatric symptoms have been well controlled, with marked improvement in hallucinations and function since starting clozapine.
cases.exam: An ill-appearing but alert man who is febrile and mildly tachycardic. The oropharynx is erythematous with tonsillar exudate and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy. There is no oral thrush, gingival bleeding, or mucosal ulceration elsewhere. Lungs are clear; cardiac exam reveals tachycardia without murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatosplenomegaly. There is no rash, petechiae, or ecchymosis. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with no rigidity, tremor, or clonus, and no altered mental status. There is no neck stiffness. Mental status shows organized thought and no acute psychosis. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given that he is on clozapine and presents with fever and sore throat, what is the most critical immediate test?
options: Complete blood count with differential to assess the absolute neutrophil count
Rapid strep antigen test only
Chest X-ray
Blood alcohol level
stages.explanation: Any patient on clozapine who develops fever, sore throat, or signs of infection must have an urgent CBC with differential to determine the absolute neutrophil count (ANC), because clozapine can cause severe neutropenia/agranulocytosis, a potentially fatal idiosyncratic reaction. Fever and pharyngitis may be the first sign of agranulocytosis, in which the patient cannot mount a normal defense against infection. This is the single most important immediate test and overrides reassurance. A rapid strep test alone misses the life-threatening hematologic concern. A chest X-ray is reasonable if pneumonia is suspected but does not address the urgent ANC question. A blood alcohol level is irrelevant. The ANC determines whether clozapine must be held and guides emergent management.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 1.6 ×10³/µL
Absolute neutrophil count 380 /µL
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
Rapid strep: negative
Blood cultures: pending
Clozapine level: within expected range

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His ANC is 380/µL. How is this best classified, and what is the immediate medication action?
options: Mild neutropenia; continue clozapine and recheck in a week
Normal count; reassure and discharge
Severe neutropenia (agranulocytosis); stop clozapine immediately and do not rechallenge
Thrombocytopenia; transfuse platelets and continue clozapine
stages.explanation: An ANC below 500/µL meets the threshold for severe neutropenia (agranulocytosis) under clozapine REMS monitoring. Clozapine must be stopped immediately and the patient must NOT be rechallenged, because re-exposure can cause recurrent, potentially fatal agranulocytosis. The patient also requires urgent treatment of the febrile neutropenia. An ANC of 380/µL is not mild neutropenia (mild is roughly 1000-1499) and is far from normal, so continuing the drug or reassuring and discharging would be dangerous. The platelets and hemoglobin are normal, so this is isolated neutropenia, not thrombocytopenia, and platelet transfusion is not indicated. Recognizing ANC <500 as agranulocytosis mandating immediate, permanent clozapine cessation is the crucial decision point.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond stopping clozapine, what is the most appropriate management of this febrile patient with an ANC of 380/µL?
options: Discharge home on oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up
Admit for empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for febrile neutropenia, with hematology involvement and consideration of G-CSF
Observe without antibiotics since cultures are pending
Restart clozapine at a lower dose to avoid psychiatric relapse
stages.explanation: This is febrile neutropenia, a medical emergency. He should be admitted for prompt empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., an antipseudomonal beta-lactam such as cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) after cultures, with hematology consultation; granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF, filgrastim) is often used to hasten neutrophil recovery in clozapine-induced agranulocytosis. Delaying antibiotics while awaiting cultures risks rapid deterioration and sepsis in a neutropenic host. Discharge on oral antibiotics is unsafe given the severity. Restarting clozapine, even at a lower dose, is absolutely contraindicated after agranulocytosis because of the risk of recurrent, potentially fatal marrow suppression; an alternative antipsychotic must be selected once he recovers. Aggressive inpatient management of febrile neutropenia plus permanent clozapine discontinuation is the standard of care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What does the clozapine REMS program require regarding ANC monitoring, and why did this case slip through?
options: Monitoring is optional and left to clinician discretion
Regular ANC monitoring (weekly for the first 6 months, then less frequently) is mandatory, and he missed required draws
ANC need only be checked once at baseline
Monitoring is required only for the first two weeks of therapy
stages.explanation: The clozapine REMS (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy) program mandates regular ANC monitoring: weekly for the first 6 months, every 2 weeks for months 6-12, and monthly thereafter if counts remain normal, with pharmacies dispensing only when an acceptable ANC is documented. This patient missed his required weekly draws (he was only 8 months in, when at least biweekly monitoring applies), allowing developing agranulocytosis to go undetected until he became febrile. Monitoring is not optional, not a single baseline check, and not limited to the first two weeks. The structured schedule exists precisely to catch falling counts before life-threatening infection occurs. Reinforcing adherence to REMS monitoring is essential, and his lapse is the proximate reason the agranulocytosis was caught late.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After he recovers from agranulocytosis, his schizophrenia will need ongoing treatment. What is the most appropriate plan?
options: Resume clozapine once his ANC normalizes, since it controlled his symptoms best
Select a non-clozapine antipsychotic and avoid clozapine permanently because of the risk of recurrent, potentially fatal agranulocytosis
Stop all antipsychotics permanently
Combine two other antipsychotics with low-dose clozapine
stages.explanation: Because he developed clozapine-induced agranulocytosis (ANC <500/µL), clozapine is permanently contraindicated and he should never be rechallenged in any dose, as re-exposure can trigger recurrent, faster, and potentially fatal marrow suppression; he must be transitioned to a non-clozapine antipsychotic once recovered. Resuming clozapine after his ANC normalizes is dangerous and explicitly contraindicated. Stopping all antipsychotics is inappropriate given his refractory schizophrenia and high relapse risk; he still requires antimaintenance treatment. Combining other agents with low-dose clozapine still exposes him to clozapine and is unsafe. Although clozapine is uniquely effective for treatment resistance, safety mandates an alternative agent, with shared decision-making and close psychiatric follow-up to manage relapse risk.

pearls: Any patient on clozapine with fever or sore throat needs an urgent CBC with differential to assess the ANC; pharyngitis may herald agranulocytosis.
ANC <500/µL is agranulocytosis: stop clozapine immediately and never rechallenge.
Febrile neutropenia is an emergency: admit for empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics after cultures, involve hematology, and consider G-CSF.
Clozapine REMS mandates scheduled ANC monitoring (weekly x6 months, then biweekly, then monthly); missed draws let agranulocytosis go undetected.

cases.title: A Crushing Headache After Dinner
cases.chiefComplaint: "I have the worst pounding headache of my life and my heart is racing after dinner."
cases.history: A 54-year-old man arrives at the emergency department with a severe, throbbing occipital headache that began about 45 minutes ago, accompanied by neck stiffness, sweating, palpitations, nausea, and a sense of impending doom. He attended a wine-and-cheese gathering this evening and ate an aged cheddar plate and cured salami. He has treatment-resistant depression and started phenelzine 45 mg daily about 3 weeks ago after multiple failed antidepressant trials. He recalls being told to avoid certain foods but did not realize aged cheese was prohibited. He denies head trauma, prior similar headaches, focal weakness, or visual loss. He denies recent cocaine or other stimulant use and takes no over-the-counter cold medicines. He has hypertension treated with amlodipine and no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history includes hypertension. He is anxious and uncomfortable. There has been no fever or recent illness, and he has had no neck injury.
cases.exam: A diaphoretic, anxious man in moderate distress holding his head. He is alert and oriented. Pupils are equal and reactive; fundoscopic exam shows no papilledema or hemorrhage. There is mild nuchal discomfort but no true meningismus, and Kernig and Brudzinski signs are negative. Cranial nerves are intact, with no facial droop, dysarthria, or visual field cut. Motor strength and sensation are symmetric, reflexes are normal, and there is no clonus, tremor, or rigidity; gait is not tested due to the headache. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia and a forceful apical impulse without murmur. Lungs are clear. Skin is flushed and sweaty. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his medication and recent meal, what is the most likely cause of his severe headache and hypertension?
options: Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Hypertensive crisis from an MAOI-tyramine (food) interaction
Migraine with aura
Pheochromocytoma
stages.explanation: He is on phenelzine, a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, and ate tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese and cured meats). MAOIs block the breakdown of dietary tyramine, which then triggers a massive release of stored norepinephrine, producing a hypertensive crisis with severe occipital/throbbing headache, palpitations, diaphoresis, and sometimes neck stiffness, exactly this picture, often called the 'cheese reaction.' Subarachnoid hemorrhage is the key mimic and must be excluded, but the clear MAOI-plus-tyramine exposure and absence of true meningismus or focal deficit point to the food interaction. Migraine with aura would not produce blood pressure of 224/128 mmHg. Pheochromocytoma causes episodic catecholamine surges but is unlikely to coincide precisely with starting an MAOI and a tyramine meal. The medication-food interaction is the unifying explanation.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most important immediate step to exclude a life-threatening alternative cause of thunderclap headache?
options: Carotid ultrasound
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Non-contrast CT of the head
Lower-extremity Doppler studies
stages.explanation: A sudden, severe 'worst headache of my life' (thunderclap) mandates urgent exclusion of subarachnoid hemorrhage, and the first-line study is an emergent non-contrast CT of the head, which is highly sensitive within the first hours of onset. If the CT is negative and suspicion remains, a lumbar puncture (for xanthochromia/red cells) follows, but LP is not performed before imaging in this setting. Carotid ultrasound evaluates extracranial carotid stenosis and is not indicated for acute thunderclap headache. Lower-extremity Dopplers assess for deep vein thrombosis, which is irrelevant here. Even though the clinical story strongly suggests an MAOI-tyramine crisis, ruling out subarachnoid hemorrhage with a non-contrast head CT is the prudent and standard immediate step while blood pressure is being managed.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage, mass, or midline shift
Troponin: within normal limits
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemic changes
Basic metabolic panel: within normal limits
Urine toxicology: negative for cocaine and amphetamines
Repeat BP: 218/126 mmHg

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT excludes hemorrhage and his BP remains 218/126 mmHg. What is the most appropriate treatment for this hypertensive crisis?
options: A short-acting, titratable IV agent such as phentolamine or nicardipine to lower blood pressure in a controlled manner
A nonselective beta-blocker such as propranolol alone
Oral clonidine and discharge home
Intravenous epinephrine
stages.explanation: MAOI-tyramine hypertensive crisis is treated with a short-acting, titratable IV antihypertensive that allows controlled blood pressure reduction, such as phentolamine (an alpha-blocker historically favored for catecholamine-driven crises) or IV nicardipine. Careful titration avoids overshoot hypotension. Importantly, a nonselective beta-blocker alone (e.g., propranolol) is dangerous in a catecholamine surge because unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction can worsen hypertension, the same principle as in pheochromocytoma. Oral clonidine with discharge is inappropriate for a true crisis at 218/126 mmHg requiring monitored, parenteral control. Intravenous epinephrine would catastrophically worsen the catecholamine excess. A titratable IV agent like phentolamine or nicardipine, with close monitoring, is the correct approach, and phenelzine should be reviewed with strict dietary counseling thereafter.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: After stabilization, what dietary counseling is essential for a patient remaining on an MAOI like phenelzine?
options: Avoid all carbohydrates and increase protein intake
Strictly avoid tyramine-rich foods such as aged cheeses, cured/fermented meats, fermented soy products, tap/draft beer, and certain aged or spoiled foods
Avoid only citrus fruits and chocolate
No dietary restrictions are necessary with MAOIs
stages.explanation: Patients on MAOIs must strictly avoid tyramine-rich foods to prevent hypertensive crises: aged cheeses, cured or fermented meats (salami, pepperoni), fermented soy products (soy sauce, miso, tofu in some cases), sauerkraut, tap/draft and certain craft beers, and aged, smoked, or spoiled foods. They should also avoid sympathomimetic drugs (e.g., decongestants like pseudoephedrine, stimulants) and other serotonergic agents to prevent serotonin syndrome. Avoiding carbohydrates or citrus and chocolate is not the relevant restriction (chocolate's tyramine content is minimal). Saying no restrictions are needed is dangerous and exactly the error that led to this crisis. Thorough, explicit dietary and drug-interaction education, ideally with a written list and a 2-week washout when switching agents, is essential to safe MAOI use.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Besides tyramine-related hypertensive crisis, which other life-threatening reaction must be guarded against when combining an MAOI with certain other drugs?
options: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome from co-administered antibiotics
Disulfiram-like reaction with any food
Serotonin syndrome if combined with serotonergic agents such as SSRIs, meperidine, tramadol, or triptans
Hemolytic anemia from vitamin C
stages.explanation: Combining an MAOI with other serotonergic drugs can precipitate serotonin syndrome, a potentially fatal reaction marked by altered mental status, autonomic instability (hyperthermia, tachycardia), and neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor). Dangerous combinations include SSRIs/SNRIs, meperidine, tramadol, dextromethorphan, triptans, and linezolid, which is why adequate washout periods (e.g., about 2 weeks, or 5 weeks after fluoxetine) are mandatory when switching to or from an MAOI. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is caused by dopamine-blocking antipsychotics, not antibiotics combined with MAOIs. A disulfiram-like reaction relates to alcohol with certain drugs, not MAOIs with food. Vitamin C does not cause hemolytic anemia in this context. Vigilance about serotonergic drug interactions, alongside tyramine avoidance, is essential to safe MAOI prescribing.

pearls: MAOIs block tyramine metabolism; tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese, cured meats) trigger a norepinephrine surge causing a 'cheese reaction' hypertensive crisis with severe occipital headache.
Thunderclap headache always warrants an emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage, even when an MAOI reaction is suspected.
Treat with a short-acting titratable IV agent (phentolamine or nicardipine); avoid beta-blocker monotherapy, which causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction.
MAOI patients must avoid tyramine-rich foods and sympathomimetics, and require washout periods when switching to/from other serotonergic agents.

cases.title: Swollen Cheeks and Worn Teeth in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep getting cavities and my face looks puffy along the jaw.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman presents for evaluation of recurrent dental caries and bilateral facial fullness her dentist noticed. She reports she has always been preoccupied with her weight and, over the past 18 months, has had recurring episodes of eating large quantities of food in short periods, feeling out of control, followed by self-induced vomiting. Episodes occur about 4-5 times per week. She also intermittently uses over-the-counter laxatives. She describes intense guilt and shame afterward and hides the behavior from her roommate. She denies fasting for days but skips meals. Menses are irregular. PMH is unremarkable; she takes no regular medications. She drinks alcohol socially, denies illicit drugs, and reports low mood but no active suicidal ideation. Family history is notable for an aunt with an eating disorder and a mother with anxiety. She is a full-time graduate student, exercises moderately, and has a supportive boyfriend. Allergies: none. She reports occasional heartburn and a chronically sore throat.
cases.exam: BMI 22 kg/m². Patient is well-appearing but anxious. Bilateral painless parotid gland enlargement gives a 'chipmunk' facial appearance. Oral exam shows erosion of the lingual enamel of the upper incisors and several caries. There are callused, abraded knuckles on the dorsum of the right hand (Russell's sign). Oropharynx is mildly erythematous. Cardiac exam is regular without murmur; no edema. No lanugo, and there is normal subcutaneous fat without cachexia. Abdomen soft, nontender. Neurologic exam normal. No goiter.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings best explains this patient's parotid swelling, dental erosion, and knuckle calluses?
options: Restriction of food intake leading to a body weight markedly below normal
Recurrent binge eating with compensatory self-induced vomiting at a normal body weight
Persistent eating of nonnutritive substances such as ice or chalk
Primary salivary gland tumor with secondary dental disease
