stages.explanation: Recurrent binges followed by self-induced vomiting at a normal BMI (22) is classic for bulimia nervosa, and the physical triad here is pathognomonic: bilateral parotid hypertrophy (sialadenosis) from repeated emesis, lingual enamel erosion (perimylolysis) from gastric acid, and Russell's sign (knuckle calluses from teeth abrasion during induced vomiting). Option 1 describes anorexia nervosa, which requires significantly low weight—she is normal weight. Option 3 (pica) does not cause this triad and is unrelated to compensatory behaviors. Option 4 is incorrect: a salivary tumor would be unilateral and would not produce perimylolysis or Russell's sign; the findings are explained by a single unifying behavioral disorder, not two coincidental processes.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L
K 3.0 mEq/L
Cl 90 mEq/L
HCO3 32 mEq/L
Mg 1.8 mg/dL
Amylase 180 U/L (elevated, salivary fraction)
beta-hCG: negative
TSH 2.1 mIU/L
ECG: normal sinus rhythm, QTc 430 ms, no U waves

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The metabolic panel shows hypokalemia with an elevated bicarbonate and low chloride. What is the most likely acid-base disturbance and its mechanism?
options: Anion-gap metabolic acidosis from laxative-induced bicarbonate loss
Respiratory alkalosis from anxiety-driven hyperventilation
Hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis from recurrent vomiting
Normal anion-gap metabolic acidosis from renal tubular acidosis
stages.explanation: Recurrent self-induced vomiting causes loss of gastric HCl, producing a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis—exactly the pattern seen (K 3.0, Cl 90, HCO3 32). Volume contraction and secondary hyperaldosteronism worsen potassium and hydrogen loss in the urine. Option 1 is wrong: laxative abuse typically causes a non-gap metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate-rich diarrheal losses, and her HCO3 is high, not low. Option 2 (respiratory alkalosis) would lower, not raise, bicarbonate as a compensation and would not explain hypochloremia. Option 4 (RTA) is a non-gap acidosis with low bicarbonate, opposite to her findings. Recognizing the vomiting-driven alkalosis guides electrolyte repletion (correct potassium and volume) before any focus on weight.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After correcting electrolytes, which pharmacologic agent is the established first-line medication for this disorder—and which agent must be specifically avoided?
options: Start fluoxetine; avoid bupropion because it lowers the seizure threshold in purging patients
Start bupropion; avoid fluoxetine because of QT prolongation
Start olanzapine; avoid all antidepressants
Start lithium; avoid SSRIs due to interaction risk
stages.explanation: Fluoxetine is the only FDA-approved medication for bulimia nervosa and is first-line, typically at a higher target dose (60 mg/day) than used for depression; it reduces binge-purge frequency, ideally combined with cognitive behavioral therapy. Bupropion is contraindicated in patients with bulimia or active purging because it markedly increases seizure risk in this population. Option 1 reverses the correct relationship. Option 3 (olanzapine) is used adjunctively in low-weight anorexia, not as first-line for bulimia, and blanket avoidance of antidepressants is wrong. Option 4 (lithium) has no role here, requires a stable electrolyte/volume status it cannot guarantee in purging patients, and SSRIs are in fact the preferred class. CBT plus fluoxetine is the gold-standard combination.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding in this patient would most strongly indicate the need for inpatient medical admission rather than outpatient management?
options: BMI of 22 kg/m² with normal appearance
Persistent serum potassium of 2.4 mEq/L with ECG changes despite oral repletion
Patient feeling guilty after binge episodes
Russell's sign and parotid enlargement
stages.explanation: Severe, refractory hypokalemia (K 2.4 mEq/L) with ECG changes is a life-threatening complication risking ventricular arrhythmia and is a clear indication for inpatient admission with cardiac monitoring and IV repletion. Most bulimia nervosa patients are managed outpatient, so the medical instability—not the eating behavior alone—drives the level of care. Option 1 is reassuring; normal BMI does not mandate admission. Option 3 (guilt) is a typical psychological feature and is managed outpatient with therapy. Option 4 (Russell's sign, parotid swelling) reflects chronic purging but is not by itself a medical emergency. Other admission criteria include syncope, hemodynamic instability, intractable vomiting, esophageal tears, or acute suicidality—physiologic danger, not the diagnosis itself.

pearls: The triad of parotid (salivary) swelling, lingual enamel erosion (perimylolysis), and Russell's sign at a normal BMI strongly suggests bulimia nervosa.
Self-induced vomiting classically produces a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis; laxative abuse instead causes a non-gap metabolic acidosis.
Fluoxetine (target 60 mg/day) plus CBT is first-line; bupropion is contraindicated because it raises seizure risk in purging patients.
Refractory hypokalemia with ECG changes, hemodynamic instability, or acute suicidality drives inpatient admission—not the eating diagnosis alone.

cases.title: A Boy Who Can't Sit Still or Finish His Work
cases.chiefComplaint: His teacher says he can't focus and is always out of his seat.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought in by his mother at the recommendation of his second-grade teacher. Since kindergarten he has had difficulty staying seated, blurts out answers, interrupts others, and seems 'driven by a motor.' He loses homework, forgets instructions, and is easily distracted; tasks requiring sustained mental effort frustrate him. His mother reports the same behaviors at home: he can't wait his turn during games, climbs on furniture, and leaves chores unfinished. Symptoms have been present for more than 6 months and were clearly evident before age 12. There is no history of a major life stressor, abuse, or recent change at home. He sleeps well and meets developmental milestones; reading and math skills are age-appropriate when he can focus. PMH unremarkable, no medications, no allergies. Birth history normal. Family history: father had similar school difficulties as a child. Social: lives with both parents and a younger sister; no tobacco or substance exposure in the home. Hearing and vision screening normal.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, well-nourished boy who fidgets in his chair and repeatedly gets up during the visit. Growth parameters at the 55th percentile for height and weight. HEENT normal; no dysmorphic features. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; femoral pulses normal and symmetric. Lungs clear. Neurologic exam grossly normal with no focal deficits, normal tone, and no abnormal movements or tics. He is cooperative and engages appropriately when redirected. Thyroid not enlarged. No signs of neglect or injury.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature is most essential to confirm before diagnosing this child with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder?
options: Symptoms are present in only one setting, such as school
Symptoms cause impairment and are present in two or more settings, with onset before age 12
A neuroimaging study shows frontal lobe abnormalities
Symptoms began suddenly after a recent stressful event
stages.explanation: DSM-5 requires that ADHD symptoms be present in two or more settings (here, home and school), cause functional impairment, and have several symptoms present before age 12. This boy meets all of these. Option 1 is wrong because cross-situational impairment is required; symptoms confined to one setting suggest a situational explanation rather than ADHD. Option 3 is incorrect—ADHD is a clinical diagnosis and neuroimaging is not part of the workup. Option 4 is wrong because ADHD is a chronic neurodevelopmental disorder with early, persistent symptoms; an acute onset after a stressor points toward an adjustment disorder, anxiety, or another explanation. Confirming pervasiveness across settings and early onset distinguishes ADHD from situational or reactive behavior.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate next step to support the diagnosis before starting treatment?
options: Order an EEG and brain MRI
Obtain standardized rating scales (e.g., Vanderbilt) from parents and teachers
Begin a trial of an antipsychotic and reassess
Refer immediately for inpatient psychiatric admission
stages.explanation: Standardized, validated rating scales completed by both parents and teachers (such as the Vanderbilt or Conners scales) are the recommended next step; they document symptoms across settings, quantify severity, and screen for comorbidities like oppositional, anxiety, or learning problems. Option 1 is wrong—routine EEG and MRI are not indicated for typical ADHD and add cost and radiation/sedation risk without diagnostic value. Option 3 is inappropriate: antipsychotics are not first-line for ADHD and carry significant metabolic and movement side effects. Option 4 is excessive; ADHD is managed in the outpatient setting and there is no safety emergency. Gathering multi-informant data follows guideline-based practice and confirms cross-situational impairment before committing to pharmacotherapy.

stages.label: RATING SCALES
stages.content:
Vanderbilt (parent): inattention 7/9, hyperactivity/impulsivity 8/9, performance impairment present
Vanderbilt (teacher): inattention 8/9, hyperactivity/impulsivity 7/9, performance impairment present
Screen for oppositional/conduct symptoms: negative
Screen for anxiety/depression: negative
Hearing/vision: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The family elects medication. Before starting a stimulant, what is the most appropriate screening step?
options: Obtain a baseline complete blood count and liver function tests
Take a focused cardiac history and family history of sudden death; obtain an ECG only if abnormalities are found
Order a routine echocardiogram for every patient
Check a fasting lipid panel and hemoglobin A1c
stages.explanation: Before stimulant initiation, guidelines recommend a focused cardiac history and family history (structural heart disease, arrhythmia, sudden unexplained death) plus a cardiac exam; a routine ECG is not required for an asymptomatic child with a normal history and exam, but it is obtained if red flags emerge. This boy has a normal cardiac exam and no concerning history, so a stimulant can be started without an ECG. Option 1 (CBC/LFTs) is unnecessary baseline testing for stimulants. Option 3 is wrong—universal echocardiography is not indicated and wastes resources. Option 4 (lipids/A1c) is unrelated to stimulant safety screening. Stimulants modestly raise heart rate and blood pressure, so the cardiovascular history is the key safety step.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Two months after starting a stimulant, the parents report good symptom control but ask about side effects to monitor. Which is the most appropriate ongoing monitoring?
options: Monitor appetite, sleep, height/weight, and heart rate/blood pressure at follow-up visits
No monitoring is needed once a dose is effective
Check serum stimulant drug levels monthly
Obtain a brain MRI every 6 months
stages.explanation: Stimulants commonly cause appetite suppression, sleep disturbance, and modest increases in heart rate and blood pressure, and they can transiently affect growth velocity. Ongoing monitoring should therefore track appetite, sleep, growth (plot height/weight), and vital signs, with symptom rating scales to gauge efficacy. Option 2 is wrong—periodic monitoring is essential even when effective. Option 3 is incorrect: routine serum stimulant levels are not clinically useful and are not standard practice. Option 4 is inappropriate—there is no role for serial neuroimaging in ADHD management. Effective treatment combines medication with behavioral interventions and school support, and reassessment at each visit ensures benefits continue to outweigh side effects, with dose adjustments as the child grows.

pearls: ADHD requires symptoms in 2+ settings, onset before age 12, and clear functional impairment—it is a clinical diagnosis, not an imaging one.
Multi-informant standardized rating scales (Vanderbilt, Conners) confirm cross-situational symptoms and screen for comorbidities.
Before stimulants, take a focused cardiac and family sudden-death history; routine ECG is reserved for those with red flags.
Monitor appetite, sleep, growth (height/weight), and vital signs on stimulants; combine medication with behavioral and school supports.

cases.title: A Toddler Who Doesn't Talk or Point
cases.chiefComplaint: He barely talks and doesn't seem to notice us.
cases.history: A 4-year-old boy is referred by his pediatrician for evaluation of language delay and unusual behaviors. His parents report he had only a handful of words by age 2 and now speaks in single words or short scripted phrases, often echoing television lines. He rarely uses gestures, does not point to share interest (no joint attention), and makes little eye contact. He prefers to play alone, lining up his toy cars for long periods and becoming very distressed if the order is changed or his routine is altered. He flaps his hands when excited and is intensely preoccupied with spinning objects. He does not respond consistently when his name is called, though hearing testing was normal. He was born at term without complications; motor milestones were on time. PMH unremarkable; no medications; no allergies. Immunizations up to date. He attends daycare, where staff note he does not engage in pretend play with peers. Family history: a maternal cousin with a similar developmental profile. No regression of previously acquired skills.
cases.exam: Alert boy who does not make eye contact and does not orient to his name. Growth parameters normal; head circumference at the 70th percentile. No dysmorphic features. He repetitively flaps his hands and lines up objects; resists transitions. Limited spoken language with echolalia and no spontaneous pointing or showing. Hearing grossly intact (turns to sounds, prior audiology normal). Neurologic exam: normal tone, symmetric reflexes, normal gait, no focal deficits, no ataxia. Skin exam shows no hypopigmented macules or café-au-lait spots. No hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of features is most characteristic of this child's diagnosis?
options: Persistent deficits in social communication plus restricted, repetitive behaviors with early onset
Normal social development with isolated expressive language delay only
Loss of previously acquired language and motor skills after a period of normal development
Hyperactivity and impulsivity that are pervasive but with intact social reciprocity
stages.explanation: Autism spectrum disorder is defined by two core domains: persistent deficits in social communication and interaction (poor eye contact, absent joint attention, no spontaneous pointing/showing) plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior or interests (lining up cars, hand-flapping, insistence on sameness), with onset in early development and functional impairment. This boy fits both domains. Option 2 describes an isolated language disorder, which spares social reciprocity and lacks repetitive behaviors. Option 3 describes a regressive pattern (e.g., Rett or childhood disintegrative disorder); this child has no loss of acquired skills. Option 4 describes ADHD, where social reciprocity is generally preserved and the hallmark is inattention/hyperactivity rather than the social-communication and repetitive-behavior dyad seen here.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which evaluation is most important to obtain as part of the initial assessment?
options: Routine brain MRI in all children with these features
Formal audiologic (hearing) evaluation and a structured developmental/diagnostic assessment
Serum heavy-metal levels and chelation if elevated
Empiric trial of a stimulant medication
stages.explanation: A formal hearing evaluation is essential because hearing impairment can mimic or worsen social-communication delay, and a structured developmental assessment (e.g., ADOS) plus history establishes the diagnosis and characterizes needs. Although prior audiology was reportedly normal, confirming intact hearing is a standard first step. Option 1 is wrong—routine MRI is not indicated unless there are focal neurologic findings, seizures, or signs of a specific syndrome. Option 3 is incorrect: routine heavy-metal testing and chelation are not part of the workup and chelation is dangerous. Option 4 is premature and not diagnostic; stimulants treat comorbid ADHD symptoms, not core autism features, and should not be started during the diagnostic phase. Genetic testing (e.g., chromosomal microarray, fragile X) is also commonly recommended.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT RESULTS
stages.content:
Audiology: hearing within normal limits bilaterally
Structured diagnostic observation: meets criteria across social-communication and restricted/repetitive domains
Cognitive/adaptive testing: mild global delay, nonverbal skills relatively stronger
Lead level: < 3.5 µg/dL (normal)
Chromosomal microarray and fragile X testing: sent, pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important intervention to recommend now for this 4-year-old?
options: Start risperidone as first-line treatment for the core social deficits
Refer for early intensive behavioral and educational intervention (e.g., applied behavior analysis, speech and occupational therapy)
Recommend a gluten-free, casein-free diet as primary therapy
Defer all intervention until genetic results return
stages.explanation: Early, intensive, individualized behavioral and educational intervention—including speech/language therapy, occupational therapy, and structured behavioral programs such as applied behavior analysis—is the cornerstone of management and most strongly improves communication, social skills, and adaptive function. Earlier initiation yields better outcomes. Option 1 is wrong: antipsychotics like risperidone or aripiprazole are FDA-approved only for associated irritability/aggression, not for core social-communication deficits, and carry metabolic risk. Option 3 lacks evidence and is not a primary treatment. Option 4 is harmful—intervention should begin immediately and should not wait on pending genetic testing, since the diagnosis is already clinically established and early intervention is time-sensitive during this critical developmental window.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The parents ask about associated conditions to watch for. Which is the most appropriate counseling point?
options: Children with this disorder cannot develop intellectual disability or seizures
Comorbid intellectual disability, seizures, anxiety, ADHD, and GI/sleep problems are common and warrant ongoing surveillance
These children outgrow all symptoms by school age without support
Vaccines should be stopped to prevent worsening of symptoms
stages.explanation: Autism spectrum disorder frequently co-occurs with intellectual disability, epilepsy/seizures, ADHD, anxiety, and gastrointestinal and sleep disturbances, so longitudinal surveillance and a coordinated care plan are essential. Option 1 is false—these comorbidities are in fact more common in ASD. Option 3 is incorrect: ASD is a lifelong condition; while skills can improve substantially with intervention, children do not simply outgrow it, and ongoing supports are typically needed. Option 4 is dangerous misinformation—vaccines do not cause autism, and stopping immunizations exposes the child and community to preventable, serious disease. Accurate counseling supports families, encourages adherence to interventions, and ensures monitoring for treatable comorbidities that significantly affect quality of life.

pearls: ASD is diagnosed by deficits in social communication PLUS restricted, repetitive behaviors with early developmental onset and impairment.
Always confirm hearing is intact—undetected hearing loss can mimic the social-communication delay of autism.
Early intensive behavioral and educational intervention is the cornerstone; risperidone/aripiprazole treat only associated irritability, not core deficits.
Screen and surveil for common comorbidities: intellectual disability, seizures, ADHD, anxiety, and GI/sleep problems—and counsel that vaccines do not cause autism.

cases.title: Confusion and Agitation After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: She's suddenly confused and pulling at her lines (collateral from nursing).
cases.history: An 81-year-old woman is in the surgical ICU on postoperative day 2 after open repair of a hip fracture. Overnight she became acutely confused, alternating between drowsiness and agitation, pulling at her urinary catheter and IV lines. Her daughter reports she was fully oriented and sharp at baseline, living independently. The confusion waxes and wanes, worse at night. PMH: hypertension, osteoarthritis, mild hearing loss. Home medications: amlodipine and acetaminophen. Postoperatively she has received intermittent oxycodone for pain and was started on diphenhydramine for sleep; she also has an indwelling Foley catheter. She has poor oral intake and has not had a bowel movement since surgery. No prior dementia or psychiatric history. Allergies: none. Social: widowed, lives alone, nonsmoker, rare alcohol. Family history noncontributory. Nursing notes document disorganized speech and inattention; she cannot follow simple commands consistently and reports seeing 'people in the corner' intermittently.
cases.exam: Elderly woman who is inattentive, unable to recite months backward, and drifts off mid-sentence. She is disoriented to place and time, with fluctuating alertness ranging from somnolence to agitation. Speech is disorganized and tangential. She intermittently appears to respond to internal stimuli. No focal motor or sensory deficits; pupils equal and reactive; no facial droop; gait not tested due to recent surgery and agitation. Mild suprapubic fullness and tenderness; urine in catheter bag is cloudy. Surgical hip incision is clean, dry, and intact without surrounding erythema. Lungs clear; cardiac exam shows tachycardia, regular, no murmur. Abdomen mildly distended.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical feature best distinguishes this acute presentation from an underlying dementia?
options: Gradual memory decline over many months with clear consciousness
Acute onset over hours to days with fluctuating attention and altered level of consciousness
Stable, fixed cognitive deficits without any change in alertness
Isolated word-finding difficulty with preserved attention
stages.explanation: Delirium is characterized by an acute onset (hours to days), a fluctuating course, and—most importantly—a disturbance of attention and awareness, often with an altered level of consciousness ranging from hypoactive somnolence to hyperactive agitation. This patient was sharp at baseline and changed abruptly postoperatively, with inattention and waxing/waning alertness, which is the hallmark. Option 1 describes dementia, which is gradual and preserves consciousness early. Option 3 (stable fixed deficits, normal alertness) again fits dementia, not delirium. Option 4 (isolated word-finding with intact attention) suggests a focal/aphasic process, not the global attentional disturbance of delirium. The defining distinction is the acute, fluctuating attentional deficit superimposed on a previously normal cognition.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given the fever, tachycardia, cloudy urine, and suprapubic tenderness, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step to find the trigger?
options: Obtain an urgent noncontrast head CT before any other testing
Send urinalysis and urine culture along with basic labs, and review the medication list
Perform a lumbar puncture immediately
Order an EEG to confirm the diagnosis of delirium
stages.explanation: Delirium is a clinical diagnosis and the priority is to identify and treat the precipitant. Here the fever, tachycardia, cloudy catheter urine, and suprapubic tenderness point to a urinary tract infection, so urinalysis/urine culture plus basic labs (CBC, electrolytes, glucose, renal function) and a careful medication review (diphenhydramine, opioids—deliriogenic) are the highest-yield first steps. Option 1 (head CT) is not first-line without focal neurologic signs, trauma, or anticoagulation. Option 3 (LP) is reserved for suspected meningitis or unexplained fever with meningismus, absent here. Option 4 (EEG) typically shows diffuse slowing in delirium but is not needed to make the clinical diagnosis and would delay treatment of the reversible cause. Find and fix the underlying trigger.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.2 ×10³/µL
Na 134 mEq/L
Glucose 118 mg/dL
Cr 1.1 mg/dL
Urinalysis: cloudy, +leukocyte esterase, +nitrites, WBC 80/hpf, many bacteria
Urine culture: pending (gram-negative rods on gram stain)
CXR: clear lungs, no infiltrate
O2 sat improves to 98% on 2 L nasal cannula

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides treating the urinary tract infection with antibiotics, what is the most appropriate management of her agitation and delirium?
options: Apply physical restraints and start scheduled diphenhydramine to keep her calm
Implement nonpharmacologic measures (reorientation, hearing aids, sleep-wake regulation, early mobilization, remove the catheter, treat constipation) and avoid restraints
Start a benzodiazepine drip for sedation
Begin high-dose opioids to ensure she rests
stages.explanation: First-line management of delirium is nonpharmacologic: treat the cause (antibiotics for the UTI), reorient frequently, restore sensory aids (her hearing aids and glasses), normalize the sleep-wake cycle, mobilize early, remove unnecessary tethers like the Foley, ensure hydration, and address constipation. Restraints worsen agitation, increase injury and aspiration risk, and prolong delirium, so they should be avoided. Option 1 is doubly wrong—restraints and diphenhydramine (a deliriogenic anticholinergic) both worsen outcomes. Option 3 (benzodiazepine drip) can precipitate or worsen delirium except in alcohol/sedative withdrawal. Option 4 (high-dose opioids) adds to the deliriogenic burden. Low-dose antipsychotics are reserved only for severe agitation posing imminent danger, not as routine therapy.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which medication on her current regimen most likely contributed to the delirium and should be discontinued?
options: Amlodipine
Acetaminophen
Diphenhydramine
Normal saline maintenance fluids
stages.explanation: Diphenhydramine is a potent anticholinergic with strong central effects and is a well-known precipitant of delirium in older adults; it appears on the Beers list of medications to avoid in the elderly and should be stopped, with safer nonpharmacologic sleep measures substituted. Option 1 (amlodipine) is a calcium channel blocker with no significant deliriogenic or anticholinergic effect. Option 2 (acetaminophen) is the preferred analgesic in the elderly and can actually reduce opioid requirements, indirectly lowering delirium risk. Option 4 (normal saline) is not deliriogenic and may help if she is volume-depleted. Beyond diphenhydramine, minimizing or rotating the opioid and limiting other anticholinergics/sedatives are key preventive steps; medication review is central to both treating and preventing delirium.

cases.diagnosis: Delirium (hyperactive/mixed) due to urinary tract infection and deliriogenic medications
pearls: Delirium = acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness; dementia is gradual with preserved consciousness early—delirium can be superimposed on dementia.
Always search for and treat the precipitant: infection (UTI, pneumonia), metabolic derangement, hypoxia, medications, urinary retention, and constipation.
Manage with nonpharmacologic measures—reorientation, sensory aids, sleep hygiene, early mobilization, removing tethers—and avoid restraints, which worsen delirium.
Anticholinergics like diphenhydramine and benzodiazepines/opioids precipitate delirium in the elderly; reserve low-dose antipsychotics for dangerous agitation only.

cases.title: Forgetting Recent Conversations
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep forgetting things I was just told, and it's getting worse.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman presents with her husband for a 2-year history of insidiously progressive forgetfulness. She repeats questions, misplaces items, forgets recent conversations and appointments, and has begun struggling to manage finances and follow recipes she has used for decades. Her husband notes she occasionally gets lost driving to familiar places. Notably, she is aware of and frustrated by her memory lapses early on. There is no acute change, no fluctuation in alertness, and no visual hallucinations. Remote memory and personality are relatively preserved. No tremor, falls, or urinary incontinence. PMH: hypertension, hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine, TSH well-controlled). Medications: lisinopril, levothyroxine; no anticholinergics or sedatives. No alcohol or tobacco. Allergies: none. Family history: mother developed similar memory problems in her late 70s. She completed college, was a schoolteacher, and lives at home with her husband. Functional decline is gradual; she still performs basic self-care but needs help with complex tasks.
cases.exam: Pleasant, well-groomed woman, alert and fully oriented to person, partially to date. Attention is intact (can recite months backward), distinguishing this from delirium. On cognitive testing she has marked short-term memory impairment (recalls 0-1 of 3 words at 5 minutes) and difficulty with the clock-drawing and intersecting-pentagon tasks; language and naming are largely preserved. She shows insight into her deficits. Cranial nerves intact; no focal motor or sensory deficits; normal tone with no rigidity, tremor, or bradykinesia; gait normal; no myoclonus. No carotid bruits. Cardiac and pulmonary exams unremarkable. No depressive psychomotor slowing on exam.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical pattern is most consistent with major neurocognitive disorder due to Alzheimer disease in this patient?
options: Stepwise cognitive decline with focal deficits and a history of strokes
Insidious, gradually progressive memory loss (prominent recent/episodic memory) with preserved attention and alertness
Early prominent visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and fluctuating cognition
Early personality change and disinhibition with relatively spared memory
stages.explanation: Alzheimer disease typically presents with an insidious onset and slow, gradual progression of episodic (recent) memory impairment—repeating questions, misplacing items, forgetting conversations—while attention and level of consciousness remain intact, distinguishing it from delirium. This patient's 2-year gradual course, prominent short-term memory loss, and preserved alertness fit. Option 1 describes vascular neurocognitive disorder (stepwise decline, focal signs, vascular history). Option 3 describes dementia with Lewy bodies (early visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, cognitive fluctuations)—absent here. Option 4 describes frontotemporal dementia (early personality/behavioral change and disinhibition with relatively preserved memory). The amnestic, gradually progressive presentation with preserved attention and insight early is the classic Alzheimer pattern.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup to evaluate for reversible contributors to cognitive decline?
options: Lumbar puncture for amyloid and tau in every patient as the first test
Check basic labs including TSH and vitamin B12, review medications, and obtain noncontrast brain imaging
Start a cholinesterase inhibitor empirically without any workup
Order genetic testing for APOE before any other evaluation
stages.explanation: Evaluation of new cognitive decline should screen for reversible or contributing causes: laboratory testing (TSH, vitamin B12, CBC, metabolic panel, sometimes folate), a medication review for cognitively impairing agents, and structural brain imaging (CT or MRI) to exclude tumor, subdural hematoma, normal-pressure hydrocephalus, or significant vascular disease. Although her TSH is controlled, completing the screen is standard. Option 1 is wrong—CSF amyloid/tau biomarkers are not first-line and are reserved for selected diagnostic uncertainty. Option 3 is premature: a reversible-cause workup should precede empiric therapy. Option 4 is incorrect—APOE genotyping is a risk allele, not diagnostic, and is not part of routine evaluation. Identifying treatable contributors before attributing decline to Alzheimer disease is essential.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH 2.0 mIU/L (normal)
Vitamin B12 520 pg/mL (normal)
CBC, CMP: within normal limits
RPR: nonreactive
MRI brain: generalized cortical atrophy with disproportionate medial temporal/hippocampal volume loss; no infarcts, mass, or hydrocephalus
MoCA: 18/30 (deficits in delayed recall and visuospatial domains)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After a reversible workup is negative and imaging supports Alzheimer disease, which medication is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy?
options: A cholinesterase inhibitor such as donepezil
A benzodiazepine to reduce her frustration and anxiety
An anticholinergic agent to improve focus
High-dose vitamin E as monotherapy to reverse the disease
stages.explanation: A cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil, rivastigmine, or galantamine) is first-line for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease; it modestly improves or stabilizes cognition and function by increasing synaptic acetylcholine. Memantine (an NMDA antagonist) is added for moderate-to-severe disease. Option 2 is wrong—benzodiazepines worsen cognition, increase fall and delirium risk, and are inappropriate. Option 3 is harmful: anticholinergics impair memory and can worsen Alzheimer symptoms—exactly the opposite of the cholinergic strategy. Option 4 is incorrect—vitamin E is not disease-reversing and is not standard monotherapy. Pharmacologic benefits are modest, so therapy is paired with caregiver support, safety planning (driving, finances), structured routines, and management of comorbidities; goals are symptom stabilization and quality of life, not cure.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: The husband asks about prognosis and safety. Which is the most appropriate counseling point at this stage?
options: She should continue driving indefinitely because her memory is the only problem
Discuss progressive decline, address driving safety and getting lost, establish advance directives/power of attorney, and arrange caregiver support
No safety planning is needed until she is bedbound
Reassure them the cholinesterase inhibitor will cure the disease within a year
stages.explanation: Alzheimer disease is progressive, so proactive counseling should address the expected gradual decline, safety concerns already evident (getting lost while driving—warranting a driving evaluation and likely cessation), financial and medication supervision, and early legal/medical planning such as advance directives and durable power of attorney while she retains capacity and insight. Connecting caregivers to support resources reduces burden. Option 1 is unsafe—getting lost in familiar places is a red flag for driving impairment. Option 3 is wrong—safety and advance planning are most valuable early, not deferred to end-stage. Option 4 is false—cholinesterase inhibitors modestly stabilize symptoms but do not cure or reverse the disease. Honest, structured planning protects the patient and supports the family.

cases.diagnosis: Major neurocognitive disorder due to Alzheimer disease
pearls: Alzheimer disease: insidious, gradually progressive episodic memory loss with preserved attention/alertness early—contrast with delirium, vascular, Lewy body, and frontotemporal patterns.
Work up reversible contributors first: TSH, B12, metabolic panel, medication review, and structural brain imaging before attributing decline to Alzheimer.
Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil) are first-line for mild-moderate disease; add memantine for moderate-severe. Avoid anticholinergics and benzodiazepines.
Counsel early on driving safety, advance directives/power of attorney, and caregiver support—benefits of therapy are modest and the disease is progressive.

cases.title: Found Unresponsive with Pinpoint Pupils
cases.chiefComplaint: He was found barely breathing and blue at home (brought in by EMS).
cases.history: A 29-year-old man is brought to the ED by EMS after being found unresponsive and cyanotic in his apartment by his girlfriend. Paramedics noted pinpoint pupils, a respiratory rate of 4, and shallow breathing; they administered intranasal naloxone with rapid improvement in respiration and arousal. On arrival he is drowsy but rousable. He admits to escalating heroin use over the past 2 years, originally starting prescription oxycodone after a sports injury. He uses intravenously several times daily, has tried to quit multiple times with severe withdrawal, and has lost his job and relationships due to use. He has had prior nonfatal overdoses. He denies suicidal intent—'I just wanted to get high.' PMH: prior skin abscess. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: lives with girlfriend, drinks alcohol occasionally, smokes tobacco; shares needles 'sometimes.' Family history: father with alcohol use disorder. He is not currently in any treatment program. Last use was about an hour before being found.
cases.exam: Young man, now drowsy but arousable and answering questions after naloxone. Pupils 3 mm and reactive (previously pinpoint). No focal neurologic deficits; moving all extremities. Track marks and hyperpigmentation over the antecubital fossae bilaterally; a healed abscess scar on the left forearm. Lungs clear bilaterally; no crackles. Cardiac exam regular without murmur (no new murmur to suggest endocarditis). Abdomen soft, nontender, no hepatomegaly. No signs of trauma. He becomes mildly anxious and yawns as the visit proceeds. No piloerection or diaphoresis yet.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After naloxone reversal, what is the most important immediate consideration in the ED?
options: Discharge him immediately now that he is awake
Observe him for recurrent respiratory depression because naloxone has a shorter duration than many opioids
Administer a large dose of an opioid to prevent withdrawal seizures
Give flumazenil to reverse any benzodiazepine component
stages.explanation: Naloxone has a relatively short duration of action (30-90 minutes), often shorter than that of the opioid that caused the overdose, so re-sedation and recurrent respiratory depression can occur once it wears off. The priority is a period of monitored observation, with repeat naloxone or an infusion if needed. Option 1 is dangerous—premature discharge risks fatal rebound respiratory depression. Option 3 is wrong and harmful: opioids are not used to 'prevent withdrawal seizures' (opioid withdrawal, unlike alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal, is not typically life-threatening), and giving opioids could re-precipitate overdose. Option 4 (flumazenil) is not indicated—there is no evidence of benzodiazepine toxicity, and flumazenil can precipitate seizures. Airway, breathing, and continued monitoring are paramount.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on his 2-year history, which features confirm a diagnosis of opioid use disorder rather than simple physiologic dependence?
options: Tolerance and withdrawal alone are sufficient and define the disorder
Loss of control over use, continued use despite harm (job/relationships), failed attempts to quit, and craving—beyond tolerance/withdrawal
A single episode of recreational use
Use of a prescribed opioid exactly as directed for acute pain
stages.explanation: Opioid use disorder is a DSM-5 diagnosis based on a pattern of impaired control and continued use despite harm: using more than intended, unsuccessful efforts to cut down, craving, failure to fulfill role obligations (lost job), social/interpersonal problems, and use despite recurrent harm—this patient has many of these. Option 1 is a key distractor: tolerance and withdrawal alone, when occurring in someone taking opioids appropriately under medical supervision, do not count toward the diagnosis; the disorder requires the behavioral/loss-of-control criteria. Option 3 (single recreational use) does not meet criteria. Option 4 (taking a prescription as directed) is appropriate therapeutic use, not a disorder. His escalating IV use, repeated quit attempts, and functional/social decline clearly establish OUD.

stages.label: LABS & MONITORING
stages.content:
Urine drug screen: positive opiates, negative for benzodiazepines, cocaine, amphetamines
Acetaminophen and salicylate levels: undetectable
Venous blood gas: normalized after observation
HIV and hepatitis C screening: ordered/pending
COWS at 2 hours: 8 (mild early withdrawal—yawning, anxiety, mild mydriasis)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is now in mild early withdrawal and asks for help stopping. What is the most appropriate evidence-based pharmacologic treatment to initiate?
options: Start buprenorphine-naloxone once he is in sufficient withdrawal to avoid precipitated withdrawal
Start full-dose methadone immediately while he still has opioids on board
Prescribe as-needed oxycodone to taper at home unsupervised
Provide naltrexone now while he is still physiologically dependent
stages.explanation: Buprenorphine (with naloxone) is a first-line, life-saving medication for opioid use disorder; as a partial agonist it reduces craving and overdose risk. It must be started once the patient is in adequate withdrawal (e.g., COWS roughly ≥8-12) to avoid precipitated withdrawal, since buprenorphine displaces full agonists from receptors. His COWS of 8 makes initiation appropriate. Option 2 is wrong—starting methadone is reasonable for OUD but is given in regulated programs, and the error here is 'while he still has opioids on board' is fine for methadone but the immediate point is buprenorphine timing; methadone is not started haphazardly. Option 3 (unsupervised oxycodone taper) is not evidence-based and risks continued misuse/overdose. Option 4 is wrong—naltrexone (an antagonist) requires full detoxification first and would precipitate withdrawal in a dependent patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As part of harm reduction and discharge planning, which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Tell him to simply use willpower and avoid all medication-assisted treatment
Prescribe take-home naloxone, counsel on overdose prevention/not using alone, offer needle-exchange and HIV/HCV testing, and link to addiction treatment
Advise him that needle sharing is acceptable if needles are rinsed with water
Discharge with no follow-up since the overdose has resolved
stages.explanation: Comprehensive harm reduction and warm linkage to care save lives: prescribe take-home naloxone and teach the patient and girlfriend how to use it, counsel on not using alone and recognizing overdose, offer sterile syringe/needle-exchange services, screen for HIV and hepatitis C (he shares needles), and connect him to ongoing medication-assisted treatment and addiction services. Option 1 is wrong—willpower alone has poor outcomes, and MAT (buprenorphine/methadone) markedly reduces mortality. Option 3 is dangerous misinformation; rinsing with water does not reliably prevent HIV/HCV transmission. Option 4 abandons a high-risk patient after a near-fatal overdose, ignoring the opportunity to start treatment. Integrating MAT initiation with harm-reduction counseling and follow-up is the standard of care.

cases.diagnosis: Opioid use disorder with heroin overdose (naloxone-reversed)
pearls: Naloxone is shorter-acting than most opioids—observe for recurrent respiratory depression and re-dose as needed; opioid overdose triad is pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, and decreased consciousness.
OUD is diagnosed by loss of control and continued use despite harm; tolerance/withdrawal alone in appropriate medical use do NOT count.
Buprenorphine-naloxone is first-line MAT but must be started in adequate withdrawal (COWS ~8-12) to avoid precipitated withdrawal; naltrexone needs full detox first.
Harm reduction saves lives: take-home naloxone, do-not-use-alone counseling, syringe services, HIV/HCV testing, and warm linkage to addiction treatment.

cases.title: Yawning, Sweating, and Stomach Cramps
cases.chiefComplaint: I feel awful—runny nose, chills, cramps, can't stop yawning.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man is admitted to an inpatient detoxification unit voluntarily to stop using opioids. He reports a 6-year history of using prescription oxycodone and, more recently, heroin. His last use was about 14 hours ago. He now feels increasingly miserable: persistent yawning, watery eyes, runny nose, sweating, chills with goosebumps, muscle aches, abdominal cramps, nausea, and several episodes of loose stools. He is anxious and restless but oriented and cooperative. He denies suicidal ideation and has no chest pain or shortness of breath. PMH: hepatitis C (untreated). Medications: none currently. Allergies: none. Social: drinks alcohol rarely, smokes tobacco, no recent benzodiazepine or alcohol use (no concern for those withdrawals). Family history: noncontributory. He has detoxed once before and wants to start long-term treatment afterward. He describes intense craving but is motivated to complete detox safely.
cases.exam: Restless, uncomfortable but cooperative man, frequently yawning. Pupils are dilated (mydriasis). Lacrimation and rhinorrhea are evident. Skin is diaphoretic with visible piloerection ('cold turkey' gooseflesh). Mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without rebound or guarding; hyperactive bowel sounds. Fine resting tremor of the outstretched hands. No focal neurologic deficits; he is alert and fully oriented without confusion or hallucinations. Cardiac exam tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs clear. No track-mark abscesses actively infected. Reflexes brisk but symmetric.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings is most characteristic of opioid withdrawal in this patient?
options: Pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, and somnolence
Yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, piloerection, mydriasis, diaphoresis, GI cramps/diarrhea, and dysphoria
Hallucinations, seizures, and severe autonomic instability that is commonly fatal
Bradycardia, hypothermia, and constricted pupils
stages.explanation: Opioid withdrawal produces a hyperadrenergic, 'flu-like' syndrome: yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, piloerection (gooseflesh), mydriasis (dilated pupils), sweating, muscle aches, nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, anxiety, and dysphoria—exactly this patient's picture. Option 1 describes opioid intoxication/overdose (pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, somnolence), the opposite of withdrawal. Option 3 describes severe alcohol or sedative-hypnotic withdrawal (delirium tremens), which can be life-threatening with seizures—opioid withdrawal is intensely uncomfortable but generally not fatal in otherwise healthy adults. Option 4 (bradycardia, hypothermia, miosis) again reflects opioid intoxication, not withdrawal. Recognizing the hyperadrenergic, dilated-pupil, GI-prominent syndrome confirms opioid withdrawal.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which standardized tool is most appropriate to objectively quantify the severity of his withdrawal and guide treatment?
options: CIWA-Ar score
Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS)
Glasgow Coma Scale
PHQ-9 depression questionnaire
stages.explanation: The Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS) is the standard instrument to quantify opioid withdrawal severity; it scores signs and symptoms such as resting pulse, sweating, restlessness, pupil size, bone/joint aches, rhinorrhea/lacrimation, GI upset, tremor, yawning, anxiety, and gooseflesh, guiding both symptomatic treatment and the timing of buprenorphine induction. Option 1 (CIWA-Ar) is for alcohol withdrawal, not opioids. Option 3 (Glasgow Coma Scale) measures level of consciousness after head injury and is irrelevant here—he is fully alert. Option 4 (PHQ-9) screens for depression severity, not withdrawal. Using the correct, validated scale (COWS) allows objective tracking over time and standardizes when and how to medicate.

stages.label: WITHDRAWAL SCORING
stages.content:
COWS components: resting pulse 104 (1), sweating/diaphoresis (2), restlessness (2), pupils dilated (2), bone/joint aches (1), rhinorrhea/lacrimation (2), GI upset/diarrhea (2), tremor (1), yawning (1), anxiety (1), gooseflesh/piloerection (3)
Total COWS: 18 (moderate withdrawal)
Urine drug screen: positive opiates only

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If buprenorphine is not being used, which adjunctive medication is the classic agent to reduce the autonomic (hyperadrenergic) symptoms of opioid withdrawal?
options: Clonidine (an alpha-2 agonist)
Naloxone
Lorazepam scheduled around the clock
Haloperidol
stages.explanation: Clonidine, a central alpha-2 adrenergic agonist (lofexidine is the FDA-approved analog), reduces the hyperadrenergic autonomic symptoms of opioid withdrawal—sweating, tachycardia, hypertension, anxiety, and agitation—and is the classic adjunct when an opioid agonist is not used; monitor for hypotension and sedation. Symptomatic agents (antiemetics, antidiarrheals like loperamide, NSAIDs for aches) are added as needed. Option 2 (naloxone) is an antagonist that would worsen and precipitate withdrawal—the opposite of what is wanted. Option 3 (scheduled lorazepam) is not first-line for opioid withdrawal and adds oversedation/dependence risk; benzodiazepines are for alcohol/sedative withdrawal. Option 4 (haloperidol) treats psychosis/agitation, not the autonomic syndrome, and lowers the seizure threshold without addressing the underlying withdrawal physiology.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After his acute withdrawal is managed, what is the most important step to reduce his risk of relapse and overdose?
options: Discharge him with no further treatment once symptoms resolve
Initiate and maintain medication for opioid use disorder (buprenorphine or methadone) and link to ongoing treatment
Recommend detox alone is sufficient and curative
Advise complete abstinence with no medication or follow-up support
stages.explanation: Detoxification alone has high relapse rates and—because tolerance falls during abstinence—paradoxically increases overdose death risk if the person resumes use. The most important step is to initiate and maintain medication for opioid use disorder (buprenorphine or methadone, which reduce mortality and craving) and to link the patient to ongoing psychosocial treatment. Option 1 abandons him at his most vulnerable point. Option 3 is a common misconception—detox is not curative and is best viewed as a bridge to maintenance treatment. Option 4 (abstinence-only without support) carries poor outcomes and elevated overdose risk after lost tolerance. His stated motivation should be channeled directly into MAT and continued care rather than discharge after acute symptom relief.

pearls: Opioid withdrawal is hyperadrenergic and flu-like: yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea, piloerection, mydriasis, sweating, cramps, and diarrhea—uncomfortable but rarely fatal in healthy adults.
Use COWS (not CIWA, which is for alcohol) to objectively grade opioid withdrawal severity and time buprenorphine induction.
Clonidine (alpha-2 agonist) eases autonomic symptoms when an opioid agonist isn't used; add antiemetics, loperamide, and NSAIDs for symptom control.
Detox alone raises overdose risk via lost tolerance—the key relapse-prevention step is initiating and maintaining MAT (buprenorphine or methadone) with follow-up.

cases.title: Cutting After a Breakup, Again
cases.chiefComplaint: I cut my arm after my boyfriend said he needed space.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents to the ED with superficial cuts to her left forearm made after an argument in which her boyfriend said he needed 'some space.' She describes a long pattern of stormy, intense relationships that swing between idealizing partners as perfect and then viewing them as cruel and abandoning. She reports chronic feelings of emptiness, rapid mood shifts lasting hours, and a poorly defined sense of who she is. She has a history of multiple ED visits for self-harm and two prior low-lethality overdoses around relationship conflicts. She frequently feels terrified of being abandoned and acts impulsively (spending, binge drinking, risky sex). Tonight she initially praised the ED nurse as 'the only one who gets me' while calling the triage clerk 'heartless.' PMH: prior diagnosis of depression, partial response to an SSRI. Medications: sertraline 100 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: works inconsistently, lives alone, drinks heavily at times, denies current intent to die—'I just needed the pain to stop.' Family history: mother with mood instability. No psychosis.
cases.exam: Tearful, intermittently angry young woman who alternates between warmth and hostility during the interview. Several superficial, linear, clotted lacerations on the left volar forearm; no active bleeding, no tendon or deep structure involvement, distal neurovascular exam intact. Multiple well-healed linear scars of varying ages on both forearms. No signs of acute intoxication (clear speech, steady gait). She is alert and oriented; thought process is linear without looseness; no hallucinations or delusions; insight is limited. Affect is labile. No psychomotor retardation. No evidence of mania (no grandiosity, normal sleep need reported). Abdomen and cardiopulmonary exams unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports borderline personality disorder over a primary mood disorder in this patient?
options: Discrete episodes of depressed mood lasting two weeks with neurovegetative symptoms
A pervasive, longstanding pattern of unstable relationships, identity disturbance, fear of abandonment, impulsivity, and recurrent self-harm with rapid mood shifts tied to interpersonal events
Sustained elevated mood with decreased need for sleep and grandiosity
Fixed paranoid delusions and auditory hallucinations
stages.explanation: Borderline personality disorder is defined by a pervasive, enduring pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, and affect with marked impulsivity: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, idealization/devaluation (splitting—praising the nurse while denigrating the clerk), identity disturbance, recurrent self-harm/suicidal behavior, chronic emptiness, and affective instability that shifts within hours and is reactive to interpersonal stress. Option 1 describes a major depressive episode—BPD mood shifts are rapid and reactive, not sustained two-week episodes. Option 3 describes mania/bipolar disorder, with sustained elevated mood and decreased sleep need, absent here. Option 4 describes a primary psychotic disorder; she has no delusions or hallucinations. The chronic, interpersonally reactive instability with splitting and repeated self-harm is the hallmark distinguishing BPD from episodic mood disorders.

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate step in the ED regarding her self-harm and safety?
options: Automatically admit her to inpatient psychiatry for every self-harm episode
Perform a structured suicide risk assessment, treat the wounds, and determine disposition based on current risk rather than reflexively admitting
Discharge her immediately without assessment because cutting is 'just attention-seeking'
Place her in restraints to prevent further self-harm
stages.explanation: Each presentation requires an individualized, structured suicide risk assessment distinguishing chronic self-harm (often tension-relieving, as she describes—'needed the pain to stop') from acute suicidal intent, alongside wound care. Disposition is based on current risk, protective factors, and the ability to engage outpatient supports—not on reflexive admission, which can reinforce maladaptive patterns and is not protective for chronic self-harm. Option 1 is wrong—routine admission for every episode is neither evidence-based nor helpful and can worsen regression. Option 3 is dangerous and dismissive—self-harm signals real distress and elevated long-term suicide risk and must be assessed, never trivialized. Option 4 (restraints) is inappropriate for a cooperative, non-acutely-dangerous patient and would damage the therapeutic alliance. Careful assessment guides safe, proportionate disposition.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Structured risk assessment: chronic self-harm for tension relief; denies current intent or plan to die; future-oriented (has a therapy appointment next week)
No access to lethal means at home reported; agrees to a safety plan
Urine tox: negative; ethanol: negative
Collateral from outpatient therapist: engaged in care, recommends continuing outpatient DBT
No acute medical instability

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate evidence-based treatment to recommend for the core disorder?
options: Long-term benzodiazepines to control affective instability
Referral for dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) as first-line treatment
Initiation of an antipsychotic as definitive monotherapy for the personality disorder
No treatment is effective, so supportive listening only
stages.explanation: Psychotherapy is the cornerstone of BPD treatment, and dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) has the strongest evidence base; it reduces self-harm, suicidality, and hospitalizations by teaching emotion regulation, distress tolerance, interpersonal effectiveness, and mindfulness. Other structured therapies (mentalization-based, transference-focused) also help. Option 1 is wrong—benzodiazepines are generally avoided in BPD because they can cause disinhibition, worsen impulsivity, and carry dependence risk. Option 3 is incorrect: no medication treats the core disorder, though low-dose agents may target specific symptoms (e.g., transient antipsychotics for stress-related cognitive-perceptual symptoms, or treating comorbid depression); they are adjuncts, not definitive monotherapy. Option 4 is false and nihilistic—BPD has a substantial recovery rate with appropriate psychotherapy. Refer her to DBT while continuing the SSRI for comorbid depression.

stages.label: THERAPEUTIC APPROACH
stages.question: She tells you that you are 'the best, most caring doctor' and asks you to be her primary therapist and give her your personal phone number. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Agree to all requests to maintain her trust and avoid upsetting her
Set clear, consistent, compassionate limits and boundaries while maintaining a professional, nonjudgmental stance and referring to appropriate ongoing care
Respond with anger and tell her she is being manipulative
Abruptly end the encounter and avoid further contact
stages.explanation: Splitting and idealization are characteristic of BPD, and the therapeutic approach is to set clear, consistent, and compassionate limits while remaining professional and nonjudgmental—acknowledging her distress, validating feelings, but maintaining appropriate boundaries (no personal phone number; care continues with her established outpatient team). Consistency among staff prevents staff-splitting. Option 1 is wrong—agreeing to boundary violations reinforces maladaptive patterns and is unsustainable, ultimately leading to the very devaluation that follows idealization. Option 3 is harmful—labeling her as 'manipulative' and reacting with anger is countertherapeutic and stigmatizing; her behavior reflects fear of abandonment and difficulty with emotion regulation. Option 4 (abandonment) confirms her abandonment fears and damages care. Validation plus firm, kind limit-setting is the balanced, evidence-based stance.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given her assessment results, what is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Involuntary psychiatric hold for chronic self-harm despite no acute intent
Discharge with wound care, a collaborative safety plan, means-restriction counseling, and continuation of outpatient DBT and her therapist
Admit indefinitely until she 'no longer self-harms'
Discharge without follow-up since wounds are superficial
stages.explanation: She has chronic self-harm without current suicidal intent, is future-oriented, has no access to lethal means, agrees to a safety plan, and is actively engaged with an outpatient DBT therapist—so the appropriate, least-restrictive disposition is discharge with wound care, a collaborative safety plan, means-restriction counseling, and reinforcement of her ongoing outpatient treatment. Option 1 (involuntary hold) is not justified absent acute dangerousness and would be both legally inappropriate and clinically counterproductive. Option 3 (indefinite admission) is unrealistic and harmful—chronic self-harm cannot be 'admitted away,' and prolonged hospitalization can foster regression. Option 4 abandons her without the structured follow-up that reduces risk. Matching intensity of care to actual acute risk, while ensuring robust outpatient linkage, is the standard for BPD-related ED presentations.

pearls: BPD = pervasive instability in relationships, identity, and affect with impulsivity, abandonment fears, splitting, chronic emptiness, and recurrent self-harm—mood shifts are rapid and interpersonally reactive, unlike MDD's sustained episodes.
Assess each self-harm presentation individually for acute intent; reflexive admission for chronic self-harm is not protective and may reinforce regression.
DBT is first-line and reduces self-harm and hospitalizations; avoid benzodiazepines (disinhibition); medications are adjuncts for specific symptoms only.
Manage splitting with consistent, compassionate limit-setting and a nonjudgmental stance; avoid both boundary violations and angry/abandoning responses.

cases.title: Down in the Dumps for Years
cases.chiefComplaint: I've just felt down and low-energy for as long as I can remember.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents asking whether he might be depressed. He describes feeling persistently low, 'gray,' and pessimistic for the past 3-4 years, essentially every day, without a clear period of feeling well lasting more than a week or two. He has low energy, poor concentration, low self-esteem, and mild hopelessness, but he continues to work as an accountant and function, albeit at reduced capacity. He denies discrete episodes of two weeks of severe symptoms with anhedonia, marked weight change, or significant psychomotor changes. He has never been suicidal and denies prior manic or hypomanic episodes. Appetite and sleep are mildly reduced but stable. He drinks one or two beers on weekends, no other substances. PMH: unremarkable; not on medications. Allergies: none. He has never sought treatment, assuming this is 'just my personality.' Family history: father was 'always negative,' a sister with major depression. Social: married, two children, stable job, no recent major stressor. Thyroid disease ruled out previously per records.
cases.exam: Well-groomed, cooperative man with a mildly constricted, dysphoric affect and a somewhat pessimistic outlook. Speech is normal in rate and volume; thought process linear and goal-directed. No psychomotor retardation or agitation. He denies suicidal or homicidal ideation and has no hallucinations or delusions. Cognition grossly intact; attention and memory normal on bedside testing. No psychomotor signs of mania. General medical exam unremarkable: no goiter, normal cardiac and pulmonary exams, no edema, normal neurologic exam. He appears physically healthy and his demeanor is low-key but engaged.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) from major depressive disorder in this man?
options: Presence of psychotic features
Chronic depressed mood most of the day, more days than not, for at least 2 years, without meeting full criteria for a sustained major depressive episode
A single 2-week episode of severe anhedonia and neurovegetative symptoms
Episodes of elevated mood alternating with depression
stages.explanation: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) is defined by depressed mood for most of the day, more days than not, for at least 2 years (1 year in children/adolescents), with at least two additional symptoms (poor appetite/overeating, insomnia/hypersomnia, low energy, low self-esteem, poor concentration, hopelessness) and no symptom-free period exceeding 2 months. This man's chronic 3-4 year low-grade depression without a clear sustained major episode fits. Option 1 (psychotic features) is not part of the core distinction and is absent here. Option 3 describes a discrete major depressive episode—the hallmark of MDD, which he denies. Option 4 describes bipolar disorder, with elevated mood episodes, which he also denies. The chronicity (≥2 years) at a lower symptom intensity, rather than discrete severe episodes, defines dysthymia.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which step is most appropriate to complete the evaluation before settling on the diagnosis?
options: Order a brain MRI to rule out a structural cause
Screen for prior manic/hypomanic episodes, substance use, and a contributing medical condition (e.g., thyroid), and assess suicide risk
Begin lithium empirically
Refer for electroconvulsive therapy
stages.explanation: Before diagnosing a chronic depressive disorder, it is essential to screen for a history of mania or hypomania (which would indicate bipolar disorder and change treatment), assess substance use and medical contributors such as hypothyroidism (already ruled out per records), and complete a suicide risk assessment. This ensures the mood symptoms are not better explained by another condition. Option 1 (brain MRI) is not indicated without focal neurologic findings. Option 3 (empiric lithium) is wrong—lithium is for bipolar disorder, which has not been established, and starting it without that diagnosis is inappropriate. Option 4 (ECT) is reserved for severe, treatment-resistant, or life-threatening depression, not for an ambulatory patient with low-grade chronic symptoms. A careful differential screen finalizes the diagnosis safely.

stages.label: SCREENING & LABS
stages.content:
PHQ-9: 11 (moderate, chronic low-grade symptoms)
Mood disorder questionnaire for mania/hypomania: negative
TSH 2.2 mIU/L (normal); CBC and CMP within normal limits
AUDIT-C: low risk
Suicide risk assessment: no ideation, intent, or plan

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this patient's persistent depressive disorder?
options: Watchful waiting alone since he is still functioning
Combination of an SSRI and psychotherapy (e.g., CBT), as combined treatment is particularly effective for chronic depression
A benzodiazepine for daily use
An antipsychotic as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Persistent depressive disorder responds best to the combination of an antidepressant (an SSRI such as sertraline or escitalopram is first-line) and psychotherapy; for chronic depression, combined pharmacotherapy plus psychotherapy (e.g., CBT or the cognitive behavioral analysis system of psychotherapy) is more effective than either alone. Option 1 (watchful waiting) is inadequate—his symptoms have been impairing for years and warrant active treatment. Option 3 (daily benzodiazepine) does not treat depression, risks dependence, and is inappropriate as primary therapy. Option 4 (antipsychotic monotherapy) is not first-line; atypical antipsychotics are at most adjuncts in treatment-resistant cases, not initial monotherapy. Set expectations that response may take several weeks, ensure adequate dose and duration, and reassess; because the course is chronic, longer-term maintenance is often needed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: At a later visit, he reports that for the past 3 weeks he has had markedly worse mood with near-total loss of interest, early-morning awakening, a 10-pound weight loss, and new passive death wishes superimposed on his baseline. What does this most likely represent?
options: Resolution of his disorder
A superimposed major depressive episode ('double depression'), requiring reassessment, treatment intensification, and renewed suicide risk evaluation
Development of bipolar disorder
A normal personality variant requiring no change
stages.explanation: When a full major depressive episode is superimposed on chronic persistent depressive disorder, it is termed 'double depression'; the new onset over weeks of pervasive anhedonia, early-morning awakening, significant weight loss, and emerging death wishes signals this and mandates prompt reassessment, intensification of treatment (optimize/augment antidepressant, increase psychotherapy), and a renewed, careful suicide risk assessment. Option 1 is plainly wrong—symptoms have worsened, not resolved. Option 3 (bipolar disorder) is incorrect because there is no elevated/expansive mood, decreased need for sleep, or other manic features—this is a worsening of depression, not a switch to mania. Option 4 is dangerous—new neurovegetative symptoms and death wishes are not a benign personality variant and require active clinical response. Recognizing double depression prevents undertreatment of a higher-risk state.

pearls: Persistent depressive disorder = depressed mood most days for ≥2 years (≥1 year in youth) with ≥2 associated symptoms and no symptom-free gap >2 months; lower intensity but longer duration than a discrete MDD episode.
Always screen for prior mania/hypomania, substance use, and medical contributors (thyroid) and assess suicide risk before finalizing a chronic depression diagnosis.
First-line treatment is combined antidepressant (SSRI) plus psychotherapy (CBT/CBASP)—combination is especially effective for chronic depression and often needs long-term maintenance.
A superimposed major depressive episode on dysthymia is 'double depression'—watch for new neurovegetative symptoms and suicidality, and intensify treatment.

cases.title: Terrified to Give a Class Presentation
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm panicking about an upcoming presentation and want to drop the class.
cases.history: A 20-year-old college sophomore presents because he is considering dropping a course that requires an oral presentation. For as long as he can remember, he has had an intense fear of speaking in front of others or being the center of attention. He worries for days beforehand that he will embarrass himself, that classmates will judge him as foolish, and that they will notice him blushing or his voice shaking. During presentations he experiences pounding heart, sweating, trembling, and a dry mouth. He avoids speaking up in class, eating in front of others, and has turned down a leadership role to avoid public speaking. The fear is clearly excessive and he recognizes this, but he cannot control it; it is interfering with his grades and social life. He has no panic attacks 'out of the blue' in unrelated settings, no fear of being trapped, and no generalized worry about many topics. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. No alcohol or drug use to cope yet. Family history: mother is 'very shy.' He is otherwise healthy and high-functioning academically when not presenting.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young man who is mildly anxious in the office but converses comfortably one-on-one. He becomes visibly tense and his voice quavers when describing public-speaking scenarios. Affect is anxious but full range; mood reactive. Thought process linear; no suicidal ideation; no hallucinations or delusions. No psychomotor agitation at rest. Cardiac exam regular without murmur; no tremor at rest; no thyromegaly or exophthalmos; reflexes normal. He has good eye contact and rapport in the dyadic setting, contrasting with his reported distress in performance situations. The remainder of the general medical exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this young man's presentation?
options: Panic disorder with recurrent unexpected panic attacks
Social anxiety disorder (social phobia), performance-related fear of scrutiny
Generalized anxiety disorder with worry about many domains
Agoraphobia with fear of open spaces
stages.explanation: Social anxiety disorder is a marked, persistent fear of social or performance situations in which the person fears being scrutinized or negatively evaluated and embarrassed; the fear is excessive, leads to avoidance, and causes significant impairment—here, fear of public speaking, blushing/voice shaking being noticed, and avoiding presentations and a leadership role. His anxiety is specifically tied to scrutiny in performance/social settings (a performance-type pattern). Option 1 (panic disorder) requires recurrent unexpected panic attacks not tied to a specific trigger—his anxiety is cued by social/performance situations. Option 3 (GAD) involves excessive worry about multiple life domains, not specifically social scrutiny. Option 4 (agoraphobia) involves fear of situations where escape is difficult (crowds, open spaces, transit), driven by panic-like symptoms, not fear of negative judgment. The scrutiny-focused fear defines social anxiety disorder.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate step before initiating treatment?
options: Order thyroid function tests and consider screening for substance use and comorbid depression
Obtain an echocardiogram to evaluate his palpitations
Start a stimulant to improve his class performance
Refer for inpatient psychiatric admission
stages.explanation: A brief medical and psychiatric screen is appropriate: checking thyroid function (hyperthyroidism can mimic anxiety with palpitations, tremor, and sweating) and screening for substance use (including alcohol used to self-medicate) and comorbid depression, which frequently accompanies social anxiety disorder. This ensures the symptoms are not secondary to a medical or substance cause and identifies comorbidities that affect management. Option 2 (echocardiogram) is unnecessary—his palpitations are clearly situational and anxiety-related, with a normal cardiac exam. Option 3 (stimulant) is wrong and could worsen anxiety; stimulants are not a treatment for social anxiety. Option 4 (inpatient admission) is excessive—he is not dangerous and is managed in the outpatient setting. A focused screen, not extensive testing, precedes treatment.

stages.label: SCREENING & LABS
stages.content:
TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal); free T4 normal
PHQ-9: 4 (minimal depressive symptoms)
AUDIT-C: low risk, denies using alcohol/drugs to cope
Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale: elevated, consistent with significant social anxiety
ECG (for palpitations): normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For generalized social anxiety disorder needing ongoing treatment, what is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: A benzodiazepine taken daily as the mainstay of treatment
An SSRI (e.g., sertraline or paroxetine) and/or cognitive behavioral therapy
A daily antipsychotic
A beta-blocker taken every day around the clock for all social situations
stages.explanation: For generalized social anxiety disorder, first-line treatment is an SSRI (or SNRI) and/or cognitive behavioral therapy, particularly CBT with exposure; these address the pervasive pattern of fear and avoidance and have the best long-term evidence. Option 1 (daily benzodiazepine) is not first-line because of dependence, tolerance, and cognitive side effects; benzodiazepines are at most short-term adjuncts. Option 3 (daily antipsychotic) is inappropriate and carries unnecessary metabolic and movement risks. Option 4 (around-the-clock beta-blocker) is wrong as a generalized treatment—beta-blockers do not treat the broad disorder and are reserved for specific, predictable performance situations on an as-needed basis. CBT plus an SSRI provides durable benefit and is the standard of care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He says his main problem is the discrete, predictable physical symptoms (pounding heart, tremor) during occasional performances like this one presentation. Which option is most appropriate for this performance-type situation?
options: A daily SSRI started the morning of the presentation for immediate effect
A beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) taken as needed shortly before the performance to blunt the physical symptoms
A long-term benzodiazepine prescription for daily use
Nothing can be done for performance anxiety
stages.explanation: For the performance type of social anxiety—discrete, predictable situations like a single presentation—an as-needed beta-blocker such as propranolol taken shortly beforehand effectively blunts the peripheral autonomic symptoms (tachycardia, tremor, sweating) and is a well-established option. Option 1 is wrong—SSRIs take several weeks to work and provide no acute benefit when started the same morning; they suit the generalized/ongoing form, not a one-time event. Option 3 (long-term daily benzodiazepine) is inappropriate due to dependence and side-effect risk and is overkill for an occasional performance. Option 4 is false and discouraging—effective options clearly exist. Matching the intervention to the pattern (as-needed beta-blocker for isolated performance anxiety vs SSRI/CBT for generalized social anxiety) is the key teaching point.

pearls: Social anxiety disorder = marked, persistent fear of scrutiny/negative evaluation in social or performance settings, with avoidance and impairment; distinguish from panic disorder (unexpected attacks), GAD (multi-domain worry), and agoraphobia.
Screen for thyroid disease, substance use (alcohol self-medication), and comorbid depression before treatment; extensive cardiac testing is unnecessary for clearly situational palpitations.
Generalized social anxiety disorder: first-line is an SSRI/SNRI and/or CBT with exposure; avoid daily benzodiazepines and antipsychotics.
Performance-type (discrete, predictable events): as-needed beta-blocker (propranolol) blunts autonomic symptoms—SSRIs take weeks and don't help acutely.

cases.title: A Promotion She Can't Accept
cases.chiefComplaint: "I was offered a regional manager job but I can't take it because it requires flying every month."
cases.history: A 30-year-old marketing associate presents requesting help with an intense fear of flying that is now threatening her career. She was offered a promotion to regional manager but declined because the role requires monthly air travel. She reports that even the thought of boarding a plane triggers a racing heart, sweating, nausea, and a sense of impending doom; she has not flown in 8 years after a turbulent flight.
