Telling him to 'use willpower' ignores that this is a recognized disorder with effective structured treatments and that he has already failed repeated solo attempts. Evidence-based behavioral treatment with peer support, plus management of comorbidity, is the standard approach.

pearls: Gambling disorder is the model behavioral addiction—escalating bets, chasing losses, failed quit attempts, and lying mirror substance-use criteria.
It engages the same mesolimbic dopaminergic reward pathway as addictive drugs, supporting its classification with addictive disorders.
Always screen for suicidality—gambling disorder carries a high suicide risk, especially amid financial catastrophe.
First-line treatment is psychotherapy (CBT) plus peer support (Gamblers Anonymous), with treatment of comorbid mood/substance disorders.

cases.title: Older Man Who Answers 'I Don't Know' to Everything
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for evaluation of memory loss and concentration problems over the past few months.
cases.history: A 71-year-old man is referred to the memory clinic by his daughter for a 3-month history of forgetfulness, poor concentration, and 'not being himself.' He repeatedly states 'I don't know' or 'I can't' when asked questions and seems to give up easily on tasks. The decline began rather abruptly after his wife of 45 years died; since then he has had low mood, early-morning awakening, poor appetite with a 6-kg weight loss, anhedonia, and excessive guilt about 'not doing enough' for his late wife. His daughter says he often complains loudly about his memory, in contrast to many forgetful elders who minimize it. PMH: well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; no prior psychiatric diagnoses. NKDA. Social history: retired teacher, lives alone since his wife's death, was previously highly functional; minimal alcohol, no tobacco or drugs. Family history: no known dementia. He denies hallucinations and has no parkinsonian features. He admits to passive thoughts that he 'wouldn't mind not waking up.'
cases.exam: A thin, sad-appearing older man with psychomotor slowing and poor eye contact. He is oriented to person, place, and time but answers many cognitive questions with 'I don't know,' often before genuinely attempting them. With encouragement and cueing, his recall improves noticeably. Speech is slow with long latency; affect is depressed and tearful. No tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, or gait abnormality. No focal neurologic deficits, no aphasia, and no visual hallucinations. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. He emphasizes and complains about his memory problems rather than concealing them.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly suggests depression (pseudodementia) rather than a primary neurodegenerative dementia?
options: Insidious onset over many years with the patient unaware of deficits
Prominent complaints about memory, frequent 'I don't know' answers, and improvement with cueing in the setting of depressive symptoms
Presence of parkinsonism and visual hallucinations
Confabulation with preserved mood
stages.explanation: Depressive pseudodementia is suggested by the patient's prominent complaints about his memory (rather than minimizing it), frequent 'I don't know' / 'I can't' responses with poor effort, and improvement on recall with cueing—all in the context of clear depressive symptoms and a precipitant (bereavement). In true neurodegenerative dementia, onset is typically insidious over years, patients often minimize or are unaware of deficits, and cueing does not normalize performance. Parkinsonism and visual hallucinations point toward Lewy body dementia, not depression. Confabulation with preserved mood suggests an amnestic/Korsakoff process, not depression. The effort-dependent, mood-linked pattern is the discriminator.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: Immediately start a cholinesterase inhibitor for presumed Alzheimer disease
Screen for depression with a validated tool, assess suicide risk, and obtain labs (TSH, B12, CBC, metabolic panel) to exclude reversible contributors
Order genetic testing for APOE status
Refer directly for electroconvulsive therapy
stages.explanation: Given the depressive picture and reversible-cause concern, the next step is to formally screen for depression (e.g., Geriatric Depression Scale), assess suicide risk (he has passive ideation), and obtain basic labs—TSH, B12, CBC, metabolic panel—to rule out hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, anemia, and metabolic disturbances that mimic cognitive decline. Starting a cholinesterase inhibitor presumes Alzheimer disease prematurely and ignores a treatable depression. APOE testing is not diagnostic or appropriate for routine workup. ECT is highly effective for severe/refractory or life-threatening depression but is not the first step before basic evaluation and a medication trial; jumping to it now is inappropriate.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
TSH 2.1 mIU/L | Free T4 normal
Vitamin B12 540 pg/mL
CBC: Hgb 13.8 g/dL, normal indices
Na 139 mEq/L | Cr 1.0 mg/dL | glucose 96 mg/dL
Geriatric Depression Scale: 12/15 (markedly elevated)
MMSE 25/30 (errors largely from poor effort/'I don't know'; improves with cueing)
MRI brain: mild generalized atrophy appropriate for age; no focal lesions or disproportionate hippocampal atrophy

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given normal labs, an elevated depression score, and effort-dependent cognitive testing, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Start a cholinesterase inhibitor and arrange dementia support services
Initiate an antidepressant (e.g., an SSRI such as sertraline) with close follow-up and psychotherapy, monitoring both mood and cognition
Provide reassurance only and recheck cognition in one year
Begin an antipsychotic for his guilt-laden thoughts
stages.explanation: The picture is major depression presenting with cognitive impairment (pseudodementia); the correct treatment is an antidepressant—an SSRI such as sertraline is appropriate and well tolerated in older adults—combined with psychotherapy and close follow-up, with monitoring of both mood and cognition. Cognitive symptoms of pseudodementia characteristically improve as the depression remits. A cholinesterase inhibitor treats Alzheimer disease, not depression, and the workup does not support that diagnosis. Reassurance with a one-year recheck neglects active, treatable depression with suicidal ideation. An antipsychotic is not first-line for non-psychotic depression and adds risk in the elderly. Treat the depression and reassess cognition.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Eight weeks after starting treatment, his mood, sleep, appetite, and cognitive testing have all improved substantially. What does this response most strongly indicate?
options: He has early Alzheimer disease that coincidentally improved
The cognitive impairment was driven by depression (pseudodementia), supporting continued antidepressant treatment and ongoing monitoring
He requires immediate discontinuation of the antidepressant
He should now be started on memantine
stages.explanation: Marked improvement in cognition paralleling improvement in mood confirms depressive pseudodementia—the cognitive deficits were a manifestation of depression and reverse with effective antidepressant treatment. This supports continuing the antidepressant for an adequate duration and monitoring, since some patients with pseudodementia carry a higher long-term risk of later developing true dementia and warrant follow-up. Alzheimer disease would not improve cognitively with an antidepressant alone. Discontinuing the antidepressant after only 8 weeks risks relapse—treatment should continue for months. Memantine is for moderate-to-severe Alzheimer disease and is not indicated when cognition normalizes with depression treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Late-life major depression presenting as pseudodementia
pearls: Pseudodementia: depressed elders complain about memory, answer 'I don't know,' and improve with cueing; true dementia patients minimize deficits.
Onset is often relatively abrupt with an identifiable precipitant (e.g., bereavement) and prominent depressive symptoms.
Always exclude reversible causes (TSH, B12, CBC, metabolic) and assess suicide risk before attributing cognition to dementia.
Cognitive deficits improve as depression remits with antidepressant treatment; monitor long-term, as pseudodementia can precede later true dementia.

cases.title: Man Whose Alertness and Visions Come and Go
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought by his wife for fluctuating confusion and 'seeing people who aren't there.'
cases.history: A 73-year-old man is brought to the neurology clinic by his wife for 18 months of progressive cognitive decline notable for striking fluctuations—some days he is lucid and conversational, other days drowsy, staring, and markedly confused, with swings even within a single day. He has recurrent, well-formed visual hallucinations, most often of small children or animals in the house, which can be vivid and detailed. His wife notes he has become slower and stiffer, with a shuffling gait and a tremor, and he acts out his dreams at night, sometimes kicking and shouting. He has had two unexplained falls. PMH: hypertension on amlodipine; no diabetes. NKDA. Social history: retired engineer, lives with his wife; rare alcohol, no tobacco. Family history: noncontributory. There is no history of stepwise strokes, head trauma, or alcohol misuse. The cognitive and motor symptoms began within about a year of each other. He has not yet been prescribed any antipsychotic or dopaminergic medication.
cases.exam: Alert but fluctuating in attention during the visit, at times drifting and staring. Cognitive testing shows impaired attention and visuospatial deficits (poor clock draw and intersecting pentagons) with relatively preserved early memory. Motor exam reveals symmetric bradykinesia, mild cogwheel rigidity, reduced arm swing, and a slow, shuffling gait; resting tremor is subtle. He describes seeing small figures in the room and is only partly reassured they are not real. Orthostatic blood pressure drop is noted. No focal weakness, no aphasia, no acute distress. Cranial nerves intact.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of features is most characteristic of this patient's likely diagnosis?
options: Stepwise decline with focal motor deficits and prominent vascular risk
Early disinhibition and personality change with relatively preserved memory
Fluctuating cognition, recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations, and spontaneous parkinsonism
Anterograde amnesia with confabulation following thiamine deficiency
stages.explanation: The core triad of dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is fluctuating cognition (with variations in attention/alertness), recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations, and spontaneous parkinsonism—often with REM sleep behavior disorder and dysautonomia (his orthostasis), as seen here. Stepwise decline with focal deficits and vascular risk describes vascular dementia. Early disinhibition and personality change with preserved memory describes behavioral-variant frontotemporal dementia. Anterograde amnesia with confabulation from thiamine deficiency is Korsakoff syndrome. The simultaneous, fluctuating cognitive, perceptual, and motor features—plus dream-enactment and autonomic signs—are the hallmark constellation of DLB.

stages.label: TIMING
stages.question: His cognitive decline and parkinsonism began within about a year of each other. How does this timing help distinguish DLB from Parkinson disease dementia?
options: In DLB, dementia onset precedes or occurs within ~1 year of parkinsonism (the '1-year rule')
In DLB, parkinsonism must precede dementia by at least 10 years
The timing is irrelevant to the distinction
Parkinson disease dementia requires hallucinations before any motor symptoms
stages.explanation: The conventional '1-year rule' distinguishes the Lewy body spectrum by timing: in dementia with Lewy bodies, cognitive impairment begins before, or within about one year of, the onset of parkinsonism. In Parkinson disease dementia, well-established parkinsonism precedes dementia by many years (typically >1 year). So the near-simultaneous onset here favors DLB. Requiring parkinsonism to precede dementia by 10 years is incorrect and is not the rule. The timing is clinically central—not irrelevant—because it determines the label and informs prognosis and management. Hallucinations are common in both but are not required to precede motor symptoms in PDD.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MMSE 23/30 (attention and visuospatial subscores most affected)
MRI brain: relative preservation of medial temporal lobe volume; mild generalized atrophy
TSH, B12, CBC, metabolic panel: within normal limits
Polysomnography: REM sleep without atonia, consistent with REM sleep behavior disorder
DaTscan (when obtained): reduced striatal dopamine transporter uptake

stages.label: MANAGEMENT — SAFETY
stages.question: His visual hallucinations become more distressing. Which medication choice poses the GREATEST danger and should be avoided?
options: A cholinesterase inhibitor such as rivastigmine
A typical (first-generation) antipsychotic such as haloperidol
Low-dose quetiapine if pharmacotherapy is truly required
Non-pharmacologic measures and reduction of offending medications
stages.explanation: Patients with DLB have severe neuroleptic (antipsychotic) sensitivity: dopamine-blocking agents—especially high-potency typical antipsychotics like haloperidol—can precipitate life-threatening reactions including severe parkinsonism, autonomic instability, and a neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like picture. Haloperidol is therefore the most dangerous choice and should be avoided. Cholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., rivastigmine) are actually beneficial for cognition and hallucinations in DLB and are first-line pharmacologically. If an antipsychotic is unavoidable, a cautious low dose of quetiapine (or pimavanserin) is preferred over typical agents. Non-pharmacologic strategies and removing offending drugs are appropriate first steps. The key teaching point: never give haloperidol to a DLB patient.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic treatment to address his cognitive symptoms and hallucinations?
options: High-dose levodopa to maximize motor and cognitive benefit
A cholinesterase inhibitor (e.g., rivastigmine or donepezil)
A benzodiazepine scheduled around the clock
An anticholinergic agent to reduce tremor
stages.explanation: Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine, donepezil) are first-line in DLB and can improve cognition, attention, and visual hallucinations, given the prominent cholinergic deficit in this disease. High-dose levodopa can worsen hallucinations and psychosis and offers limited motor benefit in DLB, so it is not first-line for cognition; if motor symptoms need treatment, the lowest effective levodopa dose is used cautiously. Around-the-clock benzodiazepines worsen confusion and fall risk in the elderly. Anticholinergic agents are particularly harmful—they impair cognition and exacerbate hallucinations in a disease defined by cholinergic loss. The cholinesterase inhibitor best targets his core symptoms.

pearls: Core DLB features: fluctuating cognition, recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations, and spontaneous parkinsonism (plus REM sleep behavior disorder and dysautonomia).
The '1-year rule' separates DLB (dementia within ~1 year of parkinsonism) from Parkinson disease dementia (motor symptoms precede dementia by years).
Severe antipsychotic sensitivity—avoid typical agents like haloperidol; use low-dose quetiapine or pimavanserin only if essential.
Cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line and can improve cognition and hallucinations; avoid anticholinergics.

cases.title: Accountant Whose Personality Changed Before His Memory
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought by his wife for two years of personality change, poor judgment, and socially inappropriate behavior.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man is brought to the memory clinic by his wife, who reports about two years of profound personality change. Once meticulous and reserved, he has become disinhibited—making crude comments to strangers, shoplifting candy without apparent guilt, and laughing at inappropriate moments. He is increasingly apathetic, neglecting hygiene and abandoning hobbies, yet his wife insists 'his memory is actually pretty good.' He has developed rigid daily rituals and a craving for sweets, eating compulsively. He shows little empathy for family members. Notably, he lost his accounting job after repeated errors and odd interpersonal behavior. PMH: none significant. Medications: none. NKDA. Social history: married, two adult children; minimal alcohol, no tobacco or drugs. Family history: a brother developed a 'behavior problem and dementia' in his late 50s. He has no early prominent amnesia, no visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism, and no history of strokes or stepwise decline. His wife emphasizes that the behavioral and personality changes clearly preceded any memory problems.
cases.exam: A disheveled middle-aged man who is jocular and disinhibited, making off-color jokes and showing reduced concern for social norms during the interview. He is oriented and, on bedside testing, has relatively preserved memory and orientation, but shows poor judgment, impaired set-shifting, and perseveration on frontal/executive tasks. Speech is at times tangential with some loss of empathy in tone. No tremor, rigidity, or bradykinesia; gait normal. No visual-field deficits or focal weakness. No resting tremor and no myoclonus. He appears indifferent to his wife's distress.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical pattern most strongly points to behavioral-variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD)?
options: Early prominent memory loss with later behavioral change
Early personality change, disinhibition, apathy, and loss of empathy with relatively preserved memory
Fluctuating cognition with visual hallucinations and parkinsonism
Stepwise decline with focal neurologic deficits
stages.explanation: Behavioral-variant FTD characteristically presents with early changes in personality and conduct—disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsive/ritualistic behaviors, and hyperorality (e.g., carbohydrate craving)—while memory and visuospatial skills are relatively spared early, exactly this patient's picture. Early prominent memory loss preceding behavior change is typical of Alzheimer disease, not bvFTD. Fluctuating cognition with visual hallucinations and parkinsonism describes dementia with Lewy bodies. Stepwise decline with focal deficits describes vascular dementia. The defining bvFTD feature is that behavioral/personality change clearly precedes and overshadows memory impairment, reflecting predominant frontal and anterior temporal degeneration.

stages.label: NEUROANATOMY
stages.question: His disinhibition, poor judgment, and loss of social conduct localize to degeneration of which brain region?
options: Hippocampus and medial temporal lobes
Frontal lobes (especially orbitofrontal/prefrontal) and anterior temporal lobes
Occipital cortex
Cerebellum
stages.explanation: bvFTD reflects degeneration of the frontal lobes—particularly orbitofrontal and prefrontal cortex—and anterior temporal lobes, regions governing social conduct, judgment, impulse control, and empathy; their loss produces disinhibition, apathy, and personality change. The hippocampus/medial temporal lobes mediate episodic memory and are the early target in Alzheimer disease, which is why memory is relatively preserved early in FTD. The occipital cortex subserves vision, unrelated to his behavioral syndrome. The cerebellum governs coordination, not social behavior. Frontal and anterior temporal atrophy is the hallmark of bvFTD on imaging and explains his clinical deficits.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
MMSE 27/30; memory and orientation relatively intact
Frontal Assessment Battery: markedly impaired (perseveration, poor inhibitory control)
TSH, B12, CBC, metabolic panel, RPR, HIV: unremarkable
MRI brain: focal atrophy of the bilateral frontal and anterior temporal lobes; medial temporal/hippocampal volume relatively preserved
No white-matter infarcts or lacunes

stages.label: GENETICS
stages.question: His brother developed similar behavior change and dementia in his late 50s. Which statement about FTD inheritance is most accurate?
options: FTD is never hereditary
A substantial minority of bvFTD is familial, associated with genes such as MAPT, GRN, and C9orf72
FTD follows X-linked recessive inheritance exclusively
FTD is caused solely by acquired vascular injury
stages.explanation: A meaningful proportion of FTD is familial, and several causative genes are recognized—most notably MAPT (tau), GRN (progranulin), and the C9orf72 hexanucleotide repeat expansion—often with autosomal dominant inheritance and onset in the 50s–60s, fitting the affected brother. So 'never hereditary' is wrong. FTD is not exclusively X-linked recessive. It is a primary neurodegenerative proteinopathy (tau or TDP-43 pathology), not solely caused by acquired vascular injury—indeed his MRI shows focal frontotemporal atrophy without infarcts. Recognizing the familial component is important for counseling and possible genetic evaluation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach for his behavioral symptoms?
options: Start a cholinesterase inhibitor, which reliably reverses bvFTD
Use behavioral/environmental strategies and caregiver support first; consider an SSRI for disinhibition/compulsions, reserving low-dose antipsychotics for severe refractory agitation
Begin high-dose levodopa
Prescribe a stimulant to improve his apathy as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: There is no disease-modifying therapy for FTD, so management is symptomatic: non-pharmacologic, behavioral, and environmental strategies plus caregiver education and safety planning come first. SSRIs can help disinhibition, compulsions, and overeating; antipsychotics are reserved for severe, refractory agitation at the lowest effective dose given FTD patients' heightened sensitivity to extrapyramidal effects. Cholinesterase inhibitors do not reliably help bvFTD and may worsen agitation—unlike in Alzheimer disease. Levodopa is not indicated absent parkinsonism and could worsen behavior. Stimulants are not standard first-line therapy. A behavioral-first, judicious-pharmacology approach is the appropriate strategy.

pearls: bvFTD presents with early personality/behavioral change—disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsions, hyperorality—with relatively preserved early memory.
Pathology and atrophy localize to frontal (orbitofrontal/prefrontal) and anterior temporal lobes; onset is often in the 50s–60s.
A substantial minority is familial (MAPT, GRN, C9orf72); ask about family history and consider genetic counseling.
Management is symptomatic—behavioral strategies and caregiver support first, SSRIs for behaviors; cholinesterase inhibitors are not reliably helpful.

cases.title: Man Whose Thinking Worsened in Sudden Steps
cases.chiefComplaint: Presents with his wife for cognitive decline that seems to worsen 'in steps' along with new weakness.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with his wife, who describes cognitive decline occurring in discrete steps rather than a smooth slope: he was stable, then abruptly worsened, plateaued, and later dropped again, each drop sometimes accompanied by a new physical problem such as a weak hand or a change in walking. His thinking is slowed, with poor planning and attention, and he is emotionally labile. PMH: long-standing poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.1%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a prior transient ischemic attack; he smokes one pack daily. Medications: metformin, lisinopril, atorvastatin, aspirin 81 mg. NKDA. Social history: retired truck driver, 40-pack-year smoking history, moderate alcohol. Family history: father had a stroke. His wife notes his memory is affected but his executive function and gait seem disproportionately impaired. There is no resting tremor, no visual hallucinations, and no early disinhibition or personality change preceding the cognitive decline.
cases.exam: An alert older man with slowed processing and impaired executive function (poor on trail-making and verbal fluency) but better-preserved episodic memory than expected for his disability. Cranial nerves intact. Motor exam reveals mild left-hand pronator drift and subtly increased left-sided reflexes with an extensor plantar response on the left. Gait is slightly unsteady and small-stepped. Affect is labile, with abrupt tearfulness. No resting tremor, no cogwheel rigidity, no visual hallucinations. Carotid auscultation without bruits; irregularly irregular rhythm is NOT present (regular rhythm). No focal sensory loss.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature combination most strongly supports vascular dementia over Alzheimer disease?
options: Insidious, gradual memory loss as the earliest and dominant symptom
Stepwise decline temporally linked to focal neurologic deficits in a patient with multiple vascular risk factors
Fluctuating attention with recurrent visual hallucinations
Early personality change and disinhibition with preserved memory
stages.explanation: Vascular dementia classically shows a stepwise (rather than steadily progressive) decline temporally associated with cerebrovascular events and focal neurologic signs (here, left pronator drift, hyperreflexia, extensor plantar), in someone with heavy vascular risk (diabetes, hypertension, smoking, prior TIA). Insidious, gradual memory loss as the dominant earliest symptom is typical of Alzheimer disease. Fluctuating attention with visual hallucinations suggests dementia with Lewy bodies. Early personality change/disinhibition with preserved memory suggests frontotemporal dementia. The stepwise course plus focal deficits and vascular burden is the discriminating pattern for vascular dementia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
HbA1c 9.1% | LDL 142 mg/dL
TSH, B12, CBC: within normal limits
MoCA 20/30 (executive and attention domains most impaired)
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
MRI brain: multiple bilateral lacunar infarcts in basal ganglia and white matter; confluent periventricular white-matter hyperintensities (small-vessel disease); old right MCA-territory cortical infarct

stages.label: MANAGEMENT — PRIMARY
stages.question: Beyond cognitive symptom management, what is the cornerstone of treatment to slow further decline?
options: Aggressive control of vascular risk factors—blood pressure, glycemia, lipids, antiplatelet therapy, and smoking cessation
High-dose vitamin E and ginkgo biloba
Anticoagulation with warfarin for everyone with vascular dementia
Cholinesterase inhibitor monotherapy with no attention to vascular risk
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of managing vascular dementia is aggressive secondary prevention—optimizing blood pressure, glycemic control, and lipids; ensuring antiplatelet therapy; and supporting smoking cessation—to reduce further ischemic injury and slow stepwise decline. High-dose vitamin E and ginkgo lack convincing benefit and carry risks. Routine warfarin for 'everyone' is wrong; anticoagulation is indicated only for specific cardioembolic sources (e.g., atrial fibrillation), which he does not have (regular rhythm, sinus on ECG). Cholinesterase inhibitors offer modest benefit in some patients but ignoring vascular risk factors misses the key modifiable driver. Risk-factor control is the highest-yield intervention here.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: His wife asks about medications for the cognitive symptoms themselves. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine cure vascular dementia
These agents may provide modest symptomatic benefit, especially with mixed Alzheimer/vascular pathology, but vascular risk-factor control remains paramount
No cognitive medication should ever be considered
Antipsychotics are first-line to improve his cognition
stages.explanation: Cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine may offer modest symptomatic cognitive benefit in vascular dementia, particularly when mixed Alzheimer pathology coexists (common in this age group), but they are not curative—and aggressive vascular risk-factor control remains the central, evidence-based strategy to limit progression. Saying they 'cure' the disease is false. Categorically refusing any cognitive medication is too absolute; a trial can be reasonable. Antipsychotics do not improve cognition and carry significant risks (including increased mortality) in older patients with dementia—they are reserved for severe behavioral symptoms, not cognition. Balanced counseling emphasizes modest symptomatic options plus risk-factor optimization.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which single modifiable factor, if addressed, would most reduce his risk of further vascular cognitive decline?
options: Switching from coffee to tea
Optimizing blood pressure control along with the other vascular risk factors (he is hypertensive and a smoker with poor glycemic control)
Taking a daily multivitamin
Increasing dietary protein only
stages.explanation: Hypertension is the most important modifiable risk factor for vascular cognitive impairment, and this patient is frankly hypertensive (152/92) on top of smoking and poor glycemic control; optimizing blood pressure (alongside the other risk factors) most effectively reduces further small-vessel and large-vessel injury and stepwise decline. Switching coffee to tea has no meaningful impact on his cerebrovascular risk. A daily multivitamin does not prevent vascular dementia. Increasing dietary protein alone does not address his vascular risk. Comprehensive risk-factor management anchored by blood-pressure control offers the greatest protection against further decline.

pearls: Vascular dementia classically shows stepwise decline with focal neurologic deficits in patients with multiple vascular risk factors.
Executive dysfunction, slowed processing, gait disturbance, and emotional lability often predominate over early episodic memory loss.
The cornerstone of treatment is aggressive vascular risk-factor control (BP, glucose, lipids, antiplatelet, smoking cessation).
Cholinesterase inhibitors/memantine give only modest symptomatic benefit; hypertension is the top modifiable risk factor.

cases.title: Irritable Man With Restless, Fidgety Movements
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for evaluation of mood changes, irritability, and abnormal fidgety movements.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man is referred to the neurology clinic for about a year of worsening irritability, low mood, and 'twitchy' movements. His wife reports he has become uncharacteristically short-tempered, impulsive, and depressed, with declining performance at work and trouble organizing tasks. Over recent months she has noticed involuntary, dance-like fidgety movements of his hands and face that he often incorporates into purposeful gestures, and he seems clumsier and drops things. He has had fleeting thoughts that life is not worth living but denies a current plan. PMH: none. Medications: none. NKDA. Social history: engineer, married with two teenagers; social alcohol, no drugs. Family history is striking: his father died in his early 50s in a nursing home with a 'movement disorder and dementia,' and a paternal uncle had similar problems—details were never clearly explained to the family. He has no history of streptococcal infection, no neuroleptic exposure, and no stimulant use. He is anxious about 'whatever his father had.'
cases.exam: An anxious, irritable man with frequent brief, irregular, non-rhythmic involuntary movements of the fingers, face, and trunk (chorea), partially masked by voluntary movement. He has motor impersistence ('milkmaid grip' and inability to keep the tongue protruded). Saccadic eye movements are slowed and he has difficulty initiating voluntary saccades. Gait is mildly irregular and dance-like. Cognitive testing shows impaired executive function and processing speed with relatively preserved naming. Affect is dysphoric and irritable. No resting tremor, no rigidity, no Kayser-Fleischer rings noted, and no focal weakness or sensory loss.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best unifies his psychiatric symptoms, chorea, executive dysfunction, and family history?
options: Wilson disease
Sydenham chorea
Huntington disease
Tardive dyskinesia
stages.explanation: Huntington disease (HD) classically presents in mid-adulthood with the triad of psychiatric disturbance (irritability, depression, impulsivity), chorea, and progressive cognitive (especially executive) decline, plus characteristic oculomotor findings (slowed saccades) and motor impersistence—all with an autosomal dominant family history, as suggested by his father and uncle. Wilson disease can cause psychiatric and movement symptoms but typically presents younger, with Kayser-Fleischer rings, liver disease, and copper abnormalities (none here). Sydenham chorea follows streptococcal infection in children and is self-limited. Tardive dyskinesia requires dopamine-receptor-blocker exposure, which he denies. HD best integrates his presentation and dominant inheritance pattern.

stages.label: SAFETY
stages.question: Given his presentation, what must be assessed and addressed early in this encounter?
options: His exact CAG repeat number before anything else
Suicide risk, since depression and suicidality are common and HD carries a markedly elevated suicide rate
His ability to drive a manual transmission
His preference for inpatient versus outpatient genetic testing logistics
stages.explanation: Huntington disease carries a substantially elevated suicide risk, and this patient already has depression and passive suicidal thoughts, so a focused suicide risk assessment and safety planning are an early priority. Obtaining the exact CAG repeat number is part of confirmatory diagnosis but is not the immediate clinical priority over safety. Driving ability and testing logistics are relevant later but secondary to addressing acute psychiatric risk. Whenever depression and suicidality are present—especially in a disease with known heightened suicide risk—evaluating and managing that risk takes precedence in the encounter.

stages.label: CONFIRMATORY TESTING
stages.question: Which test confirms the diagnosis, and what counseling should accompany it?
options: Serum ceruloplasmin; no counseling needed
Genetic testing for the CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene, preceded by formal genetic counseling
Antistreptolysin O titer with reassurance
Empiric levodopa trial as a diagnostic test
stages.explanation: HD is confirmed by genetic testing demonstrating an expanded CAG trinucleotide repeat in the HTT (huntingtin) gene; ≥40 repeats is fully penetrant. Because results carry profound implications for the patient and at-risk relatives (it is autosomal dominant), testing must be preceded by formal genetic counseling addressing psychological impact, family implications, insurance/employment concerns, and reproductive options. Serum ceruloplasmin screens for Wilson disease, not HD, and counseling is certainly needed for any predictive genetic test. ASO titer evaluates post-streptococcal disease, not HD. An empiric levodopa trial neither confirms HD nor is appropriate—HD chorea is hyperkinetic and would not be a levodopa-responsive parkinsonian condition.

stages.label: GENETIC RESULTS
stages.content:
HTT gene CAG repeat analysis: 44 repeats on the expanded allele (normal <27; 27–35 intermediate; 36–39 reduced penetrance; ≥40 full penetrance)
MRI brain: caudate nucleus atrophy with enlargement of the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles ('boxcar' ventricles)
Ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urine copper: normal
TSH, B12: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His chorea is now interfering with daily function and he remains depressed. What is the most appropriate symptomatic management?
options: No treatment is available, so offer only supportive care with no medications
Treat depression (e.g., an SSRI) and, for disabling chorea, consider a VMAT2 inhibitor (e.g., tetrabenazine/deutetrabenazine), monitoring closely for worsening depression/suicidality
Start high-dose levodopa to smooth out the movements
Begin lifelong prophylactic penicillin
stages.explanation: HD has no cure, but symptoms are treatable: depression should be addressed (an SSRI is reasonable), and disabling chorea can be reduced with a VMAT2 inhibitor such as tetrabenazine or deutetrabenazine (antipsychotics are an alternative for chorea, especially with prominent psychiatric symptoms). Importantly, tetrabenazine can worsen depression and suicidality, so close monitoring is essential given his existing risk. Saying 'no medications' ignores effective symptomatic therapies. Levodopa would worsen chorea, since it is a hyperkinetic, dopaminergic-excess-related movement—the opposite of parkinsonism. Prophylactic penicillin treats rheumatic/Sydenham disease, not HD. Targeted symptomatic treatment with vigilant psychiatric monitoring is correct.

pearls: Huntington disease triad: psychiatric symptoms (irritability, depression, impulsivity), chorea, and progressive executive cognitive decline—often with psychiatric onset.
Autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in HTT; ≥40 repeats is fully penetrant—test only after formal genetic counseling.
Suicide risk is markedly elevated; assess and manage it early, and monitor closely when using VMAT2 inhibitors that can worsen depression.
MRI shows caudate atrophy with 'boxcar' ventricles; treat chorea with VMAT2 inhibitors and depression with antidepressants—there is no cure.

cases.title: Teen Brought In After Swallowing a Bottle of Pills
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought to the ED by her mother after taking a handful of pills following a breakup.
cases.history: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her mother after she impulsively swallowed approximately fifteen 500-mg acetaminophen tablets a few hours ago, following a painful breakup with her boyfriend earlier today. She told a friend by text, who alerted the mother. She now says she 'didn't really want to die' but felt overwhelmed and hopeless in the moment. She reports two months of depressed mood, poor sleep, declining grades, and social withdrawal since the relationship became strained, and she admits to prior thoughts of self-harm but no previous attempts. PMH: none. Medications: none. NKDA. Social history: 10th grader, lives with her mother and younger brother; denies alcohol, tobacco, and drugs; reports recent academic stress and conflict with peers. Family history: maternal depression. She is tearful but cooperative. She asks anxiously whether 'everything will be told to my mom' and whether she will be 'locked up,' clearly worried about confidentiality and disposition.
cases.exam: A tearful, mildly anxious adolescent in no acute physical distress, fully alert and oriented. No scleral icterus; abdomen soft and nontender without hepatomegaly. No pallor, no signs of trauma, no fresh self-inflicted wounds, and no track marks; well-healed superficial scars are noted on the forearm. Cardiopulmonary exam normal; neurologic exam nonfocal. Mood is depressed with a constricted, congruent affect; she is cooperative, makes appropriate eye contact, and denies current active intent to die but expresses ongoing hopelessness. Thought process is linear without psychosis. No asterixis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After ensuring she is medically stable, what is the most urgent step given the substance she ingested?
options: Defer all labs and observe her mood overnight
Obtain a serum acetaminophen level (timed to ingestion) and check liver function, applying the Rumack-Matthew nomogram to guide N-acetylcysteine therapy
Administer flumazenil empirically
Discharge her once she promises not to harm herself
stages.explanation: Acetaminophen overdose can cause delayed, potentially fatal hepatotoxicity even when the patient initially looks well, so the urgent step is a timed serum acetaminophen level (typically at/after 4 hours post-ingestion) with LFTs, plotting on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram to decide on N-acetylcysteine—the antidote. Deferring labs to merely observe mood risks missing lethal toxicity. Flumazenil treats benzodiazepine overdose, which is irrelevant here and potentially harmful. Discharging her on a verbal 'no-harm' promise is unsafe and not evidence-based—'no-suicide contracts' do not reduce risk, and she requires further psychiatric evaluation. Medical stabilization with toxicology assessment comes first.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Acetaminophen level (drawn 4 h post-ingestion): 165 µg/mL — above the treatment line on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram
AST 28 U/L | ALT 24 U/L | total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL | INR 1.0
Basic metabolic panel: within normal limits
Urine pregnancy test: negative
Salicylate level: negative | Ethanol: <10 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her acetaminophen level falls above the treatment line. What is the appropriate management?
options: Reassure and recheck the level in 24 hours
Begin N-acetylcysteine and admit for monitoring, with psychiatric evaluation once medically managed
Perform gastric lavage immediately several hours after ingestion
Give activated charcoal as the sole intervention and discharge
stages.explanation: A level above the treatment line mandates starting N-acetylcysteine (NAC), the antidote that replenishes glutathione and prevents hepatotoxicity; she should be admitted for monitoring of LFTs/INR, with formal psychiatric evaluation arranged once she is medically managed. Reassurance with a 24-hour recheck would dangerously delay antidote therapy. Gastric lavage is not indicated this far out and is generally not recommended. Activated charcoal is most useful within ~1–2 hours of ingestion and is not adequate alone here, and discharging an actively suicidal adolescent is unsafe. Prompt NAC plus admission and psychiatric assessment is the correct combined medical-psychiatric approach.

stages.label: DISPOSITION & SAFETY PLANNING
stages.question: Which element is most essential in her psychiatric safety management before any discharge planning?
options: Have her sign a 'no-suicide contract' and send her home
Develop a collaborative safety plan, restrict access to lethal means (e.g., remove medications/firearms from the home), and arrange close mental-health follow-up
Restrict her phone use as the primary intervention
Avoid involving her mother to protect confidentiality
stages.explanation: Evidence-based suicide prevention centers on a collaborative safety plan (recognizing warning signs, coping strategies, contacts, crisis resources), means restriction—counseling the family to secure or remove medications and firearms from the home—and ensuring timely, close mental-health follow-up. Means restriction is one of the most effective interventions for reducing suicide deaths, especially given impulsive adolescent attempts. 'No-suicide contracts' do not reduce risk and are not a substitute for a safety plan. Restricting phone use alone does not address risk. Excluding her mother is inappropriate here: imminent safety concerns override routine confidentiality, and a supportive parent is essential to means restriction and monitoring.

stages.label: CONFIDENTIALITY
stages.question: She asks you to keep everything confidential from her mother. What is the most appropriate response regarding adolescent confidentiality?
options: Promise absolute confidentiality about everything she discloses
Explain that while you respect her privacy and protect most information, safety concerns such as suicide risk are an exception that must be shared with her guardian to keep her safe
Tell her nothing she says is confidential under any circumstances
Refuse to discuss confidentiality at all
stages.explanation: Adolescent confidentiality should be respected for most sensitive information to build trust, but there is a critical exception: risk of serious harm to self or others. Active suicidality is a clear limit—you must involve her parent/guardian to ensure safety, means restriction, and follow-up. The honest, therapeutic approach is to explain this nuance: most of what she shares stays private, but safety information will be shared with her guardian to protect her. Promising 'absolute' confidentiality is false and unethical when she is at risk. Saying 'nothing is confidential' needlessly destroys rapport, and refusing to discuss it leaves her anxious and distrustful. Transparent boundary-setting is best practice.

cases.diagnosis: Suicide attempt (acetaminophen overdose) with major depressive symptoms in an adolescent
pearls: After any overdose, stabilize medically first: for acetaminophen, obtain a timed level, plot on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, and give N-acetylcysteine when indicated.
Means restriction (securing/removing medications and firearms) is among the most effective interventions to prevent suicide, especially in impulsive adolescents.
Use collaborative safety planning and ensure close follow-up; 'no-suicide contracts' do not reduce risk.
Adolescent confidentiality is respected except for safety risks—suicidality must be shared with a guardian to keep the patient safe.

cases.title: Forearm Cuts Found by the School Nurse
cases.chiefComplaint: A teacher referred her to the school clinic after noticing linear marks on her forearm during gym class.
cases.history: A 15-year-old 10th-grader is brought to the school-based clinic after a teacher saw parallel cuts on her left forearm. On private interview, she admits to cutting the inner forearm with a razor blade two to three times weekly over the past four months, usually after arguments with her mother or feeling 'numb and overwhelmed.' She describes cutting as something that 'releases the pressure' and makes her 'feel real again,' with relief immediately afterward. She denies ever wanting to die, denies overdoses or other methods, and says she hides the cuts because she does not want to be hospitalized. She has no formal psychiatric history and takes no medications. She reports low mood since her parents' divorce, intact appetite, and adequate sleep. She has close friends, plays soccer, and maintains a B average. She denies alcohol or drug use. Family history is notable for maternal depression. She denies abuse. Menarche was at age 12; periods are regular. No allergies.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative, appropriately groomed adolescent in no distress. On the volar aspect of the left forearm there are roughly a dozen superficial, parallel, linear lacerations in varying stages of healing; several are scabbed and none are actively bleeding or gaping. No deep wounds, no tendon or vascular involvement, distal sensation and capillary refill intact. No ligature marks at the neck, no pill fragments. Mood 'okay,' affect reactive and full range. Thought process linear and goal-directed. No psychomotor retardation. She denies suicidal ideation, plan, or intent and denies hallucinations. Cognition grossly intact. Insight fair; judgment age-appropriate.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After ensuring the wounds need no acute medical care, what is the most important next step in the interview?
options: Conduct a focused, nonjudgmental assessment of suicidal intent and the function the behavior serves
Immediately call her mother to disclose the cutting before talking further
Tell her cutting is dangerous and she must promise to stop right now
Arrange involuntary transfer to the emergency department for psychiatric admission
stages.explanation: The priority after wound assessment is a calm, nonjudgmental exploration of suicidal intent and the behavior's function. Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and suicide attempts carry very different risk and management, and distinguishing them hinges on intent and motivation, so this must be clarified first. Immediately phoning the mother (B) breaches the therapeutic alliance before you understand the risk and her safety; confidentiality with adolescents is limited but disclosure should be planned, not reflexive. Lecturing her to stop (C) is moralizing, ineffective, and ruptures rapport. Involuntary ED transfer (D) is unwarranted without evidence of acute suicidal risk or medical emergency and would be disproportionate to superficial wounds in an organized, future-oriented teen.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly distinguishes her non-suicidal self-injury from a suicide attempt?
options: The presence of visible scars on the forearm
Her report that cutting relieves tension and that she has no intent to die
The fact that she used a razor blade rather than pills
Her history of low mood after the divorce
stages.explanation: The defining distinction is intent: NSSI is deliberate, self-inflicted tissue damage performed without suicidal intent, typically to regulate intolerable affect, relieve tension, or feel 'something.' Her explicit absence of intent to die plus the tension-relief function are the core diagnostic features. Scars (A) simply reflect repetition and say nothing about intent. The method/instrument (C) does not define intent; lethality of method is only one input into risk and razors are common in NSSI. Depressed mood (D) is a frequent comorbidity and risk factor but is neither necessary nor sufficient to distinguish NSSI from a suicide attempt. Importantly, NSSI and suicidality can coexist, so ongoing screening remains essential.

stages.label: COLLATERAL & SCREENING
stages.content:
PHQ-9 (adolescent): 11 (mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms)
Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale: denies active or passive suicidal ideation, no prior attempts
Urine pregnancy: negative
Urine toxicology: negative
Guidance counselor: grades stable, no recent disciplinary issues, two close friends
Mother (reached by phone, with patient's awareness): reports increased irritability and withdrawal since divorce, no concerns for substance use

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment approach for her recurrent NSSI?
options: Start fluoxetine immediately and defer any psychotherapy
Require a written no-suicide contract as the primary intervention
Admit to an inpatient psychiatric unit to stop the cutting
Refer for psychotherapy emphasizing distress tolerance and emotion-regulation skills, with safety planning and outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: First-line management of NSSI is evidence-based psychotherapy that builds emotion-regulation and distress-tolerance skills (e.g., DBT-informed therapy or CBT), paired with collaborative safety planning, means-restriction counseling, and close outpatient follow-up. Pharmacotherapy alone (A) is not first-line for NSSI; an SSRI may be considered for comorbid moderate-severe depression but does not replace skills-based therapy and is not started in isolation here. 'No-suicide/no-harm contracts' (B) have no proven efficacy and can create false reassurance; safety planning is preferred. Inpatient admission (C) is reserved for acute suicidality or inability to maintain safety, neither of which is present in this organized, low-acuity adolescent.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding disclosure to her parents, what is the most appropriate course?
options: Maintain complete confidentiality and tell the parents nothing
Involve a parent/guardian in safety planning and follow-up while preserving as much of the teen's confidentiality as is safe
Disclose every detail of the sessions to satisfy parental authority
Discharge her with a self-help pamphlet and no parental involvement
stages.explanation: For a minor engaging in recurrent self-injury, safe management generally requires looping in a parent/guardian for means restriction, monitoring, and ensuring follow-up, while protecting the adolescent's confidentiality to the extent consistent with safety. Best practice is to prepare the teen for this disclosure and, when possible, support her in sharing it. Absolute secrecy (A) is not appropriate when ongoing self-harm requires home safety measures. Disclosing all session content (C) over-shares, damages trust, and is not required—only safety-relevant information should be shared. Discharging with only a pamphlet and no parental involvement or follow-up (D) leaves a recurrent self-injuring minor without the structure and supervision needed for safety.

cases.diagnosis: Non-suicidal self-injury (superficial cutting for affect regulation)
pearls: NSSI is self-inflicted tissue damage without suicidal intent, usually to relieve intolerable affect or tension—intent, not method or scars, distinguishes it from a suicide attempt.
NSSI is a strong risk factor for future suicide attempts and the two can coexist, so screen for suicidality at every visit even when intent is currently absent.
First-line treatment is skills-based psychotherapy (DBT/CBT) with safety planning and means restriction; SSRIs are adjuncts for comorbid depression, not the primary fix.
No-harm/no-suicide contracts are ineffective; use collaborative safety plans and involve guardians for monitoring while preserving appropriate adolescent confidentiality.

cases.title: The Man Who Refuses to Lose His Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: The vascular surgery team requests a psychiatric consult because the patient is refusing a recommended limb-saving amputation.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, peripheral arterial disease, and hypertension is admitted with wet gangrene of the right foot and ascending cellulitis. Vascular surgery recommends a below-knee amputation, warning that without it he faces overwhelming sepsis and death. He refuses, stating, 'I've lived 78 years on two legs and I'd rather die whole than live as half a man.' Psychiatry is consulted to assess capacity. Medications: metformin 1000 mg BID, lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily; he was started on IV piperacillin-tazobactam on admission. He lives alone, manages his own finances and medications, and drives. No psychiatric history; no alcohol or drug use. Family history noncontributory. He has no allergies. His daughter says he has always been 'stubborn but sharp' and previously told her he never wanted to be 'cut up.' He is afebrile this morning after fluids and antibiotics; he is not delirious by nursing report and sleeps normally.
cases.exam: Elderly man, alert and conversant, sitting up in bed. Oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent and coherent; thought process logical and goal-directed. No hallucinations or delusions. Mood 'frustrated'; affect congruent and reactive. Attention intact: recites months of the year backward without error. Recalls 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. He accurately restates that surgeons believe he will die without amputation, that he could survive and walk with a prosthesis, and that refusing risks fatal sepsis. The right foot is dusky with malodorous necrotic tissue and crepitus; erythema extends to mid-calf. Distal pulses absent on the right. MMSE 29/30.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the first thing the consulting psychiatrist should clarify about the consult question?
options: That this is a request to assess decision-making capacity for a specific medical decision, which physicians can determine—distinct from competence, a legal status decided by a court
Whether the patient is legally competent to manage his estate
Whether the patient meets criteria for an involuntary psychiatric hold
Whether the family wants to pursue guardianship
stages.explanation: The core conceptual point is the distinction between capacity and competence. Decision-making capacity is clinical, decision-specific, and can be assessed at the bedside by any qualified physician; competence is a global legal status determined by a court. The consult asks whether he has capacity to refuse this amputation, so framing the question correctly comes first. Assessing competence to manage an estate (B) is a legal/financial determination, not the clinical question. An involuntary psychiatric hold (C) addresses dangerousness from mental illness and does not authorize forced surgery. Guardianship pursuit (D) is premature and presupposes incapacity; it is a downstream legal step only if he is found to lack capacity and a surrogate is needed.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which set of abilities must be intact for him to have capacity to refuse the amputation?
options: Orientation, memory, and a normal MMSE score alone
Communicating a choice, understanding the relevant information, appreciating how it applies to him, and reasoning about the options
Agreement with the physicians' recommendation
Absence of any psychiatric diagnosis
stages.explanation: Capacity rests on four functional abilities: (1) communicating a stable choice, (2) understanding the relevant facts (diagnosis, treatment, risks/benefits, alternatives), (3) appreciating how that information applies to one's own situation, and (4) reasoning/manipulating the information to reach a choice consistent with one's values. A normal MMSE or intact orientation (A) is supportive but neither necessary nor sufficient—capacity is decision-specific, not a cognitive score. Agreement with the doctors (C) is never the standard; a capacitated patient may refuse recommended care. The presence or absence of a psychiatric diagnosis (D) does not by itself establish or negate capacity—what matters is whether illness impairs the four abilities for this decision.

stages.label: CAPACITY INTERVIEW & WORKUP
stages.content:
Na 138 mEq/L  |  K 4.1 mEq/L  |  Glucose 142 mg/dL  |  Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
WBC 12.8 ×10³/µL
TSH 2.0 µIU/mL  |  B12 480 pg/mL
CAM (Confusion Assessment Method): negative for delirium
PHQ-9: 4 (no significant depression)
Patient, in his own words: 'They want to take my leg below the knee. If I keep it, the infection can spread to my blood and kill me. I understand a fake leg could let me walk. I've thought about it—I would rather take that risk than lose my leg. That's been my view my whole life.'

stages.label: DETERMINATION
stages.question: Based on the assessment, what is the most appropriate conclusion?
options: He lacks capacity because refusing a life-saving operation is inherently irrational
He has capacity to refuse the amputation; his consistent, value-based choice with intact understanding, appreciation, and reasoning must be respected
He lacks capacity because he is 78 years old and chronically ill
Capacity cannot be determined because he has no psychiatric diagnosis
stages.explanation: He demonstrates all four abilities: he communicates a stable choice, accurately understands the diagnosis and consequences, appreciates that the risk applies to him personally, and reasons in a manner consistent with long-standing values. No delirium, depression, or psychosis distorts the decision. Therefore he has capacity and his refusal must be honored, even though it is high-risk. A choice is not incapacitous merely because it rejects medical advice or risks death (A)—that conflates capacity with concurrence. Age and chronic illness (C) are not criteria; capacity is functional and decision-specific. The absence of a psychiatric diagnosis (D) does not preclude a capacity determination—if anything it removes a common confound.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given that he is found to have capacity and continues to refuse, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain a court order to perform the amputation over his objection
Activate his daughter as surrogate decision-maker to consent for surgery
Respect his refusal, document the capacity assessment, and offer palliative and comfort-focused care including a clear path to revisit the decision
Place him on an involuntary psychiatric hold to compel treatment
stages.explanation: A patient with capacity has the right to refuse even life-sustaining treatment. The correct course is to honor the refusal, thoroughly document the capacity assessment and informed-refusal discussion, optimize comfort (analgesia, wound care, antibiotics if he accepts them, palliative care involvement), and leave the door open to reconsider. Seeking a court order to operate (A) violates the autonomy of a capacitated adult. Activating a surrogate (B) is appropriate only when a patient lacks capacity—he does not, so a surrogate cannot override his own valid refusal. An involuntary psychiatric hold (D) addresses danger from mental illness and never authorizes forced surgical procedures; it would be a misuse of commitment law.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two days later he becomes febrile and confused, oriented only to person, with fluctuating attention and new disorganized thinking. How does this change the picture?
options: His earlier capacity determination still stands, so no reassessment is needed
He should now be presumed to permanently lack capacity for all future decisions
Capacity is decision- and time-specific; a likely septic delirium can impair it, so he must be reassessed and, if he now lacks capacity, a surrogate decides per his known wishes
He should automatically be made a ward of the court
stages.explanation: Capacity is dynamic—decision- and time-specific—so a new delirium (here, likely from worsening sepsis) can impair the very abilities that were intact before, requiring fresh assessment. If he now lacks capacity, decisions default to a surrogate guided by his previously expressed wishes and best interests (substituted judgment), making prior conversations and any advance directive critical. Treating the earlier determination as permanent (A) ignores that delirium can reverse a prior finding. Presuming he permanently lacks capacity for everything (B) overgeneralizes; capacity is assessed per decision and may return once delirium clears. Court wardship (D) is not automatic and is a last resort when no appropriate surrogate exists.

cases.diagnosis: Patient retains decision-making capacity to refuse below-knee amputation
pearls: Capacity is clinical, decision-specific, and physician-assessed; competence is a global legal status decided by a court—do not conflate them.
Capacity requires four abilities: communicating a choice, understanding, appreciation, and reasoning—not agreement with the recommended plan.
A capacitated patient may refuse even life-saving treatment; an unwise or fatal choice is not by itself evidence of incapacity.
Always screen for reversible confounds (delirium, depression, psychosis) before concluding incapacity; a normal MMSE supports but does not establish capacity.

cases.title: Found Disrobed and Disorganized Downtown
cases.chiefComplaint: Police bring in a disheveled man found wandering into traffic, partially undressed, and shouting at parked cars.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man is brought to the ED by police after he was seen wandering into a busy intersection, partially undressed despite cold weather, and shouting at parked cars. He is unable to give a coherent history. Per the officers, a shelter worker reports he stopped his medications weeks ago and has not been eating; he was recently evicted. On interview he is guarded and intermittently responds to internal stimuli, stating that 'the broadcasts told me the food is poisoned' and that he hasn't eaten in days because 'they' will harm him. He cannot state where he will sleep tonight or how he will obtain food. He has a history of schizophrenia with two prior hospitalizations and was previously on risperidone, now discontinued. No known medical illness. Social history: homeless, estranged from family, no current substance use reported; tox screen pending. Family history: a brother with schizophrenia. No known allergies. He appears thin and unkempt, with poor hygiene.
cases.exam: Thin, malodorous man in layered, partially soiled clothing, appearing older than stated age. Dry mucous membranes; skin tenting suggests mild dehydration. He is alert but oriented only to person; attention is poor. Speech is tangential with loosening of associations. He responds to unseen voices and endorses paranoid delusions about poisoned food. Mood 'fine,' affect flat and incongruent. He denies wanting to harm himself or others but cannot articulate any plan to obtain food, shelter, or care for himself. No focal neurologic deficits; gait steady. No tremor or asterixis. Insight and judgment severely impaired.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before attributing his presentation to a psychiatric relapse, what is the most important initial step?
options: Immediately administer a long-acting antipsychotic injection
Perform a medical evaluation—vitals, glucose, electrolytes, and toxicology—to exclude medical causes and treat dehydration
Discharge him to the shelter once he is calm
Obtain a court order for guardianship
stages.explanation: Even with a known psychiatric history, acute disorganization and altered mental status require medical clearance to exclude reversible or dangerous causes (hypoglycemia, electrolyte derangement, intoxication/withdrawal, infection) and to treat his dehydration. Empiric long-acting injectable antipsychotic (A) is inappropriate before medical workup and is not how acute agitation/relapse is managed in the ED. Discharging him to a shelter (C) ignores that he cannot care for himself and is wandering into traffic—he is unsafe. Pursuing guardianship (D) is a longer-term legal process irrelevant to the acute disposition and does not address the immediate safety and medical needs. Medical stabilization always precedes definitive psychiatric disposition.

stages.label: LABS & SCREENING
stages.content:
Fingerstick glucose 88 mg/dL
Na 134 mEq/L  |  K 3.8 mEq/L  |  BUN 28 mg/dL  |  Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (prerenal pattern)
WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL
Urine toxicology: negative
Blood alcohol: undetectable
Urinalysis: concentrated, no infection
CT head: no acute intracranial abnormality

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which legal criterion for civil commitment does he most clearly meet?
options: Imminent danger to others due to homicidal threats
Grave disability—inability, due to mental illness, to provide for basic needs such as food, shelter, and safety
Voluntary request for admission
Failure to pay for prior hospitalizations
stages.explanation: Civil commitment statutes generally permit involuntary hold when a person, as a result of mental illness, is a danger to self, a danger to others, or gravely disabled. His paranoid refusal to eat, inability to secure food or shelter, and wandering into traffic exemplify grave disability and danger to self stemming from psychosis. He has made no homicidal threats (A), so danger to others is not the clearest basis. He is not requesting admission (C); his presentation is involuntary and he lacks insight. Ability to pay (D) is never a commitment criterion. Grave disability is the cleanest fit because his illness directly prevents him from meeting survival needs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Workup is unremarkable and he refuses voluntary admission. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge with an outpatient psychiatry appointment in two weeks
Place him on an emergency involuntary psychiatric hold for evaluation and stabilization, then rehydrate and restart antipsychotic treatment
Restrain and forcibly sedate him for transfer to jail
Allow him to leave against medical advice since labs are normal
stages.explanation: He meets criteria for involuntary hold (grave disability and danger to self from psychosis) and lacks the insight to accept voluntary care, so initiating an emergency psychiatric hold is appropriate; he can then be rehydrated and have antipsychotic therapy reinstated in a safe setting. Outpatient follow-up in two weeks (A) is unsafe given his immediate inability to care for himself. Transfer to jail (C) criminalizes mental illness and is not a medical disposition; restraint/sedation is reserved for acute agitation, not routine transfer. Letting him leave AMA (D) ignores that medically normal labs do not negate the psychiatric danger—his risk arises from untreated psychosis, not a metabolic cause.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding the duration and oversight of the involuntary hold, which statement is most accurate?
options: An emergency hold permits indefinite detention without any review
Once held, he loses the right to refuse all medications permanently
The hold automatically converts him to a court-determined incompetent person
An emergency hold is time-limited and must be followed by physician re-evaluation and, for continued involuntary commitment, a judicial or formal review with due-process protections
stages.explanation: Emergency psychiatric holds are short, statutorily limited (commonly around 72 hours, varying by jurisdiction) and exist to allow evaluation and stabilization. Continued involuntary commitment beyond the emergency period requires re-evaluation and a formal/judicial review with due-process protections (notice, hearing, right to counsel). Indefinite detention without review (A) would violate constitutional due process. A hold does not strip the right to refuse medication permanently (B); forced medication generally requires a separate legal determination except in emergencies. A hold is not the same as a court adjudication of incompetence (C)—commitment and competence are distinct legal determinations.

cases.diagnosis: Acute psychotic relapse of schizophrenia meeting criteria for involuntary hold (grave disability/danger to self)
pearls: Civil commitment generally requires that, due to mental illness, the person is a danger to self, danger to others, or gravely disabled.
Grave disability = inability to provide for basic needs (food, shelter, safety) because of mental illness—as with paranoid food refusal and wandering into traffic.
Always medically clear altered, disorganized patients (glucose, electrolytes, tox, infection) before attributing the picture to psychiatric relapse.
Emergency holds are time-limited; continued commitment and involuntary medication require separate due-process/judicial review—commitment is not the same as a finding of incompetence.

cases.title: Nighttime Aggression and Wandering in a Long-Term Resident
cases.chiefComplaint: Nursing staff request evaluation for escalating aggression toward caregivers and repeated wandering, especially in the evenings.
cases.history: An 82-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia (diagnosed 5 years ago) has had two weeks of escalating agitation: she strikes at aides during bathing, paces the hallways, tries to exit through locked doors in the evening, and accuses staff of stealing. She sleeps poorly and naps during the day. There is no fever, cough, or dysuria reported, but staff note she has been less interested in meals. Medications: donepezil 10 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and as-needed acetaminophen. No psychiatric history before her dementia. She has chronic osteoarthritis of the hips and knees. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: widowed, formerly a schoolteacher, two visiting children. Family history noncontributory. Baseline she is pleasant and redirectable; this change is new over two weeks. Staff have already tried increasing one-to-one supervision. They are asking whether she should be started on an antipsychotic for the aggression.
cases.exam: Elderly woman, agitated and resistant to redirection during the exam, repeatedly trying to stand and leave. Oriented to person only. She grimaces and guards when her hips are passively moved and pulls away during perineal inspection. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No focal neurologic deficit; no nuchal rigidity. Lungs clear; abdomen soft and nontender. No suprapubic tenderness elicited, though exam is limited by agitation. Skin intact without pressure injury. Attention fluctuates. No tremor or asterixis. She intermittently appears to respond to the wall but denies pain when asked directly, despite grimacing on movement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What should be done first before any decision about psychotropic medication?
options: Start risperidone to control the aggression
Evaluate for medical and environmental triggers of the behavior—pain, infection, constipation, dehydration, sensory deficits, and unmet needs
Apply physical restraints during evening hours
Transfer her to a psychiatric hospital
stages.explanation: New-onset or escalating behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) should trigger a search for treatable precipitants before any psychotropic is considered. Common drivers include pain (she has osteoarthritis and guards her hips), infection (notably UTI), constipation, dehydration, medication effects, sensory impairment, and unmet needs or environmental stressors. Reaching for risperidone first (A) skips the workup and exposes her to serious risks. Physical restraints (C) increase injury, delirium, and mortality and are not a behavioral treatment. Transfer to a psychiatric hospital (D) is premature and disruptive before reversible causes are excluded. Identifying and treating the trigger frequently resolves the behavior without antipsychotics.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Urinalysis: cloudy; leukocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive, >50 WBC/hpf, many bacteria
Urine culture: pending
CBC: WBC 11.6 ×10³/µL
Basic metabolic panel: Na 141 mEq/L, glucose 102 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Bowel record: last bowel movement 4 days ago
Pain assessment (PAINAD): elevated, consistent with significant pain on movement
Medication review: no recent changes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given these findings, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Begin scheduled haloperidol for the agitation
Treat the urinary tract infection, address constipation, and provide scheduled analgesia for her arthritis, with non-pharmacologic behavioral strategies
Increase the donepezil dose to treat the behavior
Place her on hospice care
stages.explanation: The workup reveals reversible drivers: a urinary tract infection, constipation (no BM for 4 days), and undertreated arthritic pain (elevated PAINAD with guarding). Treating the UTI, relieving constipation, and providing scheduled analgesia—alongside non-pharmacologic measures (consistent routines, calm environment, redirection, activity, addressing the bathing approach)—directly target the causes and frequently resolve BPSD. Scheduled haloperidol (A) treats a symptom while ignoring the cause and carries the boxed-warning mortality risk and EPS/QT concerns. Increasing donepezil (B-distractor C) does not treat acute agitation and may worsen GI symptoms. Hospice (D) is unwarranted—she has reversible, treatable conditions, not an imminent terminal decline.

stages.label: RISK COUNSELING
stages.question: Her family asks why staff are reluctant to use an antipsychotic. What is the most accurate explanation?
options: Antipsychotics are completely contraindicated in all elderly patients
Antipsychotics cure dementia but are too expensive

Antipsychotics carry an FDA boxed warning for increased mortality (largely cardiovascular and infectious) and stroke in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis, so they are reserved for severe, dangerous symptoms after non-drug measures fail
Antipsychotics are avoided only because they cause weight gain
stages.explanation: Atypical and typical antipsychotics carry an FDA boxed warning for increased all-cause mortality (predominantly cardiovascular events and infections such as pneumonia) and increased stroke risk when used for dementia-related psychosis/behavioral symptoms. Because of this, they are reserved for severe agitation, aggression, or psychosis posing danger when non-pharmacologic and cause-directed measures have failed, using the lowest dose for the shortest time with reassessment. They are not absolutely contraindicated in all elderly patients (A)—they have a role in select severe cases. They do not cure dementia (B). Metabolic side effects exist but are not the principal safety concern here (D); the mortality/stroke signal is.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If, after treating the reversible causes, she still has severe aggression that endangers herself or staff, what is the most appropriate approach?
options: Use long-term physical restraints
Consider a cautious, time-limited trial of a low-dose antipsychotic with informed consent, clear target symptoms, and a plan to reassess and taper
Start a benzodiazepine scheduled around the clock
Discontinue all her medications including donepezil
stages.explanation: When dangerous agitation/aggression persists despite addressing reversible causes and optimizing non-pharmacologic strategies, a carefully selected antipsychotic may be justified—using the lowest effective dose, defined target symptoms, informed consent/shared decision-making about the boxed-warning risks, and a plan for early reassessment and tapering. Long-term physical restraints (A) increase falls, delirium, deconditioning, and death and are not therapeutic. Scheduled benzodiazepines (C) worsen confusion, falls, and disinhibition in dementia and are generally avoided. Stopping all medications, including donepezil (D), is not indicated and could destabilize her further; the decision must be targeted, not indiscriminate.

cases.diagnosis: Behavioral and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) precipitated by UTI, constipation, and undertreated pain
pearls: New or worsening BPSD is a prompt to hunt for reversible triggers—pain, infection (UTI), constipation, dehydration, sensory deficits, and unmet needs—before any psychotropic.
Non-pharmacologic, cause-directed care is first-line; treating the UTI, constipation, and arthritic pain often resolves agitation.
Antipsychotics carry an FDA boxed warning for increased mortality (cardiovascular/infectious) and stroke in dementia—reserve for severe, dangerous symptoms, lowest dose, shortest duration.
Physical restraints and scheduled benzodiazepines worsen outcomes in dementia and are not behavioral treatments.

cases.title: Racing Thoughts After a Lupus Flare
cases.chiefComplaint: The rheumatology team requests psychiatric input for a patient who has become hyperactive, sleepless, and grandiose.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman admitted five days ago for a systemic lupus erythematosus flare has developed pressured speech, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, and irritability over the past 48 hours. She was started on high-dose prednisone 60 mg daily for lupus nephritis. Nursing reports she has been awake most of the night reorganizing her room, making elaborate plans to 'start three businesses,' and at times appears suspicious that staff are recording her. She has no prior psychiatric history and no family history of bipolar disorder. Prior to admission she was euthymic. Other medications: hydroxychloroquine 200 mg BID, lisinopril 10 mg daily. No alcohol or drug use. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Her renal flare is improving on therapy. She has had no fever, headache, or focal neurologic symptoms.
